clauses.title: Chinese Automotive Cybersecurity Standards Overview
clauses.guidance:
China's automotive cybersecurity regulatory framework centers on three national standards published jointly by the Standardization Administration of China (SAC) and the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT): GB/T 40857-2021 for vehicle cybersecurity technical requirements, GB/T 40856-2021 for software update engineering, and GB/T 40855-2021 for gateway security. These are complemented by GB 44495-2024, a mandatory national standard governing vehicle data security. Together they form the regulatory backbone that every vehicle manufacturer must satisfy before obtaining China Compulsory Certification (CCC) for connected and intelligent vehicles sold in the Chinese domestic market.

GB/T 40857 maps directly to UN Regulation No. 155 (UN R155) and ISO/SAE 21434. China participated in the UNECE WP.29 discussions and structured its national standards to align with the international framework while adding China-specific obligations. The mapping is intentional but not identical — GB/T 40857 imposes additional requirements around data localization, cryptographic algorithm selection, and V2X security that have no direct equivalent in R155 or ISO 21434. Manufacturers holding a valid UN R155 type approval still need to demonstrate compliance with these China-specific additions through MIIT and the State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR).

China's type approval process runs through MIIT, which delegates technical testing to designated laboratories: the China Automotive Technology and Research Center (CATARC) and the China Automotive Engineering Research Institute (CAERI). Testing follows the procedures defined in GB/T 40856, which specifies both functional verification and adversarial testing methods. Manufacturers must submit complete documentation packages in Chinese, including a cybersecurity management system description, threat analysis and risk assessment results, technical security architecture documents, and evidence of post-production monitoring capabilities.

Data localization is a non-negotiable requirement under China's Cybersecurity Law (2017) and the Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL, 2021). Vehicle telemetry, positioning data, facial recognition data, and any personal information collected within China must be stored on servers physically located within Chinese territory. Cross-border data transfers require a security assessment conducted by the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) and explicit data subject consent. GB 44495-2024 codifies these requirements specifically for the automotive sector, mandating data classification, minimum retention periods, and deletion procedures.

V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication security receives particular attention in China's ICV (Intelligent Connected Vehicle) roadmap. China has adopted the C-V2X (Cellular V2X) technology path based on LTE-V2X and NR-V2X rather than DSRC. Security requirements for V2X include certificate-based authentication using China's SM2/SM3/SM4 cryptographic algorithms, pseudonym certificate management through a national-level security credential management system, and message integrity verification. These requirements apply to both on-board units and roadside infrastructure and are tested under GB/T 40856 procedures.

Implementation requires coordination across organizational, development, and operational domains. Plan for twelve to eighteen months from program initiation to CCC certification readiness, factoring in documentation translation, laboratory scheduling, and the iterative nature of Chinese type approval reviews.

clauses.title: GB/T 40855-2021 Gateway Security Requirements
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40855-2021 establishes security requirements for vehicle gateways, which China's standards framework designates as the primary security boundary between vehicle network domains. The standard addresses access control, message filtering, authentication, anomaly detection, secure boot, and diagnostic protection for gateway electronic control units. It maps to UN R155 paragraph 7.3.4, which requires manufacturers to implement measures protecting communication channels, and extends ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 9 (cybersecurity concept) with China-specific technical prescriptions for gateway implementations.

The gateway must enforce strict access control between network domains. This means implementing allow-list-based message filtering that specifies exactly which CAN IDs, Ethernet frames, or other protocol data units may traverse each interface. The standard requires bidirectional filtering — controlling traffic from the external-facing network toward safety-critical domains and from internal domains toward external interfaces. Each filtering rule must be documented in the gateway security specification, and the rule set must be validated through both static analysis and runtime testing per GB/T 40856 test procedures.

Authentication requirements mandate that the gateway verify the source and integrity of messages crossing domain boundaries. For Ethernet-based architectures, this means implementing SecOC (Secure On-board Communication) or MACsec at the gateway interfaces. For CAN-based legacy architectures, the standard accepts message authentication codes appended to selected safety-critical messages, provided that the authentication covers at minimum all messages that can influence vehicle dynamics. China's cryptographic requirements apply here — the gateway must support SM4 for symmetric encryption and SM3 for message authentication when those algorithms are specified in the manufacturer's cryptographic implementation document.

Anomaly detection requires the gateway to identify and log deviations from normal traffic patterns. This includes detection of message frequency anomalies (bus flood attacks), unexpected CAN IDs or Ethernet traffic on protected segments, and diagnostic session abuse. The gateway must maintain a local event log that persists across power cycles and can be extracted for forensic analysis. MIIT test laboratories will verify anomaly detection effectiveness during the CCC certification process using standardized attack scenarios.

Secure boot is mandatory. The gateway must verify the integrity and authenticity of its firmware at every boot cycle using cryptographic signature verification. The root of trust must be anchored in hardware — typically an HSM (Hardware Security Module) or a secure element integrated into the gateway SoC. Rollback protection must prevent installation of firmware versions with known vulnerabilities. The complete boot chain verification process must be documented and demonstrated during CATARC or CAERI laboratory testing.

Diagnostic protection requires the gateway to enforce security access controls for diagnostic sessions traversing the gateway. UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services) sessions must be authenticated before the gateway permits routing of diagnostic requests to ECUs on protected network segments. Extended diagnostic sessions and programming sessions require elevated authentication levels consistent with the vehicle manufacturer's security policy.

clauses.title: GB/T 40855 Section 6: Update Process Governance
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40855 Section 6 governs the update process requirements for vehicle gateways, establishing how firmware updates to the gateway ECU must be managed throughout the vehicle lifecycle. This section connects directly to GB/T 40856 (software update requirements) and maps to the UN R155 requirement that manufacturers maintain the security of vehicle systems throughout their operational life. It also reflects ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 13 on cybersecurity monitoring and Clause 14 on vulnerability management, applied specifically to the gateway component.

The update process governance framework requires manufacturers to define and document a complete gateway update lifecycle. This includes specifying who has authority to approve gateway firmware updates, what validation and verification steps must be completed before deployment, how updates are distributed to vehicles in the field, and what rollback procedures exist if an update fails or introduces new vulnerabilities. The governance documentation must be written in Chinese and submitted as part of the CCC certification package to MIIT through the designated testing laboratory.

China requires that all gateway firmware updates be cryptographically signed using algorithms approved under the Chinese Cryptography Law. The signing authority, key management procedures, and certificate chain must be documented. For manufacturers using international PKI infrastructure, the standard requires either integration with a China-based certificate authority or deployment of a parallel signing infrastructure using SM2 digital signatures. The update package must include version metadata that enables the gateway to enforce version ordering and prevent rollback to versions with known vulnerabilities.

Distribution channels for gateway updates must satisfy data localization requirements under China's Cybersecurity Law and PIPL. If the manufacturer uses over-the-air (OTA) update infrastructure, the update servers delivering content to vehicles in China must be physically located within Chinese territory. Update telemetry — including vehicle identification, update status, and diagnostic data collected during the update process — must also be stored domestically. Cross-border transfer of update telemetry requires CAC security assessment approval.

Testing per GB/T 40856 requires the manufacturer to demonstrate that the gateway update process handles failure scenarios gracefully. This includes power interruption during flashing, corrupted update packages, version mismatch conditions, and partial update scenarios where some gateway functions are updated while others retain the previous version. CATARC and CAERI test laboratories will execute these failure scenarios during type approval testing.

V2X gateway interfaces add another layer of update governance complexity. When the gateway routes V2X messages, updates to V2X security parameters — including certificate revocation lists and pseudonym certificate pools — must be managed through the gateway update process. The update mechanism must ensure that V2X security credentials remain valid and synchronized with China's national V2X security credential management system throughout the update cycle.

mappings.notes: SUMS process governance alignment.
Update planning governance alignment.

clauses.title: GB/T 40855 Section 7: Update Integrity and Rollback
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40855 Section 7 specifies requirements for update integrity verification and rollback protection for vehicle gateway ECUs. This section addresses the technical mechanisms that ensure gateway firmware updates are authentic, complete, and reversible in failure scenarios. It maps to UN R155 paragraph 7.3.4 requirements for communication channel protection and extends those requirements with China-specific cryptographic and testing obligations.

Update integrity verification begins at the package level. Every gateway firmware update package must carry a cryptographic signature that the gateway can verify before beginning the installation process. China's cryptographic requirements mandate support for SM2 (elliptic curve digital signature) and SM3 (hash function) algorithms as defined in the GM/T 0003 and GM/T 0004 standards. The gateway's secure boot environment must contain the public key or certificate needed to verify the signature, stored in tamper-resistant memory. Verification must occur in a trusted execution environment that is isolated from the gateway's normal operating firmware.

The integrity verification chain extends beyond the package signature. Section 7 requires that the gateway verify the integrity of individual firmware blocks or segments after they are written to non-volatile memory. This protects against bit-flip errors during the flash programming process, storage media degradation, and partial write failures. The verification method must use cryptographic hashes rather than simple CRC checks — SM3 is the prescribed hash algorithm for China market implementations. Each segment hash must be compared against a manifest included in the signed update package.

Rollback protection is mandatory under GB/T 40855 Section 7. The gateway must maintain a monotonically increasing version counter stored in one-time-programmable (OTP) memory or a secure counter protected by the gateway's hardware security module. This counter prevents an attacker from installing older firmware versions that contain known vulnerabilities, even if the older firmware carries a valid cryptographic signature. The version counter mechanism must be demonstrated during MIIT type approval testing at CATARC or CAERI facilities.

The rollback mechanism for failed updates requires the gateway to maintain a known-good firmware image that can restore the gateway to a functional state. Section 7 specifies that the gateway must detect update failure within a defined timeout period and automatically revert to the previous firmware version without operator intervention. The rollback process itself must not create a window where the gateway operates without security protections — the fallback firmware must include the most recent security configurations that were successfully validated.

Testing requirements under GB/T 40856 include adversarial scenarios: presenting the gateway with update packages signed with revoked certificates, packages with corrupted segment hashes, packages with version numbers below the current counter value, and packages where the signature is valid but the payload targets a different ECU type. Each scenario must result in the gateway rejecting the package and logging a security event. V2X-related gateway updates must additionally verify that updated V2X security parameters are compatible with China's C-V2X credential management infrastructure before committing the update.

mappings.notes: Supports safe execution and rollback controls.
Rollback validation alignment.

clauses.title: GB/T 40856-2021 Software Update Requirements
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40856-2021 defines the engineering requirements for vehicle software update systems, covering both over-the-air (OTA) and wired update mechanisms. This standard is China's national counterpart to UN Regulation No. 156 (UN R156) on software update management systems, and it incorporates elements from ISO 24089 (software update engineering) while adding requirements specific to the Chinese regulatory environment. Compliance with GB/T 40856 is a prerequisite for CCC certification of any vehicle that receives software updates post-production.

The standard requires manufacturers to establish and maintain a Software Update Management System (SUMS) that governs the entire update lifecycle from development through deployment and post-update verification. The SUMS must be documented, auditable, and submitted to MIIT through the CCC certification process. Documentation must be in Chinese and must describe the organizational structure responsible for update management, the technical infrastructure used for update creation and distribution, the validation and verification procedures applied to each update, and the monitoring capabilities that track update deployment success rates and post-update vehicle health.

GB/T 40856 mandates that all software update packages be cryptographically signed. For the China market, this means using SM2 digital signatures with SM3 hashing, consistent with the Chinese Cryptography Law requirements. The standard also accepts RSA and ECDSA signatures when used alongside SM-series algorithms in a dual-signature scheme, accommodating manufacturers who maintain global signing infrastructure while meeting China-specific obligations.

The OTA update infrastructure presents specific challenges under China's data localization requirements. The Cybersecurity Law and PIPL require that update distribution servers serving vehicles in China be hosted on servers physically located within Chinese borders. Update telemetry — vehicle identification numbers, software version inventories, update success or failure status, and diagnostic data — constitutes personal information under PIPL when linked to vehicle owners and must be stored domestically. Manufacturers operating global OTA platforms must implement China-specific data routing to ensure compliance.

Testing under GB/T 40856 is extensive. Sections 7 and 8 prescribe both functional testing and penetration testing of the update system. Functional tests verify that updates install correctly, that the vehicle remains safe during and after the update process, that rollback mechanisms work, and that the vehicle can recover from interrupted updates. Penetration testing targets the update communication channel, the package validation mechanism, the server infrastructure, and the vehicle-side update client. CATARC and CAERI conduct these tests using attack scenarios derived from known automotive software update vulnerabilities.

V2X software components receive additional scrutiny. Updates to V2X communication stacks must be validated against China's C-V2X standards (including T/CSAE 53-2020 and related specifications) and must not disrupt the vehicle's V2X security credential management. The update system must ensure that V2X certificates and pseudonym pools remain valid across the update boundary.

clauses.title: GB/T 40856 Section 7: Security Testing
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40856 Section 7 specifies the security testing requirements for vehicle software update systems, defining the verification activities that manufacturers must complete and that MIIT-designated test laboratories will validate during the CCC certification process. This section maps to UN R156 Annex 1 testing provisions and extends them with China-specific test procedures, acceptance criteria, and laboratory protocols.

The security testing framework is structured in three tiers: component-level testing, system-level testing, and vehicle-level testing. Component-level testing targets individual elements of the update system — the update client on the vehicle, the update server infrastructure, the cryptographic signing subsystem, and the communication channel between server and vehicle. Each component must be tested independently before integration. System-level testing verifies the end-to-end update process, including package creation, distribution, installation, verification, and rollback. Vehicle-level testing validates that the update system operates correctly within the complete vehicle environment, including interactions with safety-critical systems.

China requires specific test scenarios that go beyond the UN R156 baseline. These include: replay attack testing (presenting previously captured legitimate update packages to verify that anti-replay mechanisms reject them), downgrade attack testing (attempting to install older software versions to verify version enforcement), man-in-the-middle testing on the update communication channel, denial-of-service testing against the vehicle-side update client, and firmware extraction testing to verify that update packages are protected against reverse engineering. Each test must produce documented results with pass/fail criteria traceable to specific GB/T 40856 requirements.

Cryptographic testing verifies the correct implementation of SM2/SM3/SM4 algorithms in the update system. Test laboratories at CATARC and CAERI will verify that signature verification rejects packages signed with incorrect keys, that hash verification detects single-bit modifications to update payloads, and that encrypted update channels use approved cipher suites. For manufacturers using dual-signature schemes (SM2 plus RSA or ECDSA), testing must verify that both signature chains are validated and that compromise of either chain alone is insufficient to install unauthorized updates.

Data localization testing verifies that the update system's data flows comply with the Cybersecurity Law and PIPL. Test laboratories will examine network traffic during the update process to confirm that vehicle telemetry and update status data are transmitted to servers within Chinese territory. Manufacturers must provide network architecture documentation showing the physical location of all servers involved in the update process for vehicles in the China market.

V2X-related update testing includes verification that V2X stack updates do not disrupt active V2X communication sessions, that updated V2X security parameters are compatible with China's C-V2X security credential management system, and that V2X certificate validity is maintained across the update boundary. These tests reflect China's investment in C-V2X infrastructure and the safety implications of V2X communication disruptions.

mappings.notes: Test strategy and validation scope alignment.

clauses.title: GB/T 40856 Section 8: Penetration Assessment
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40856 Section 8 defines penetration assessment requirements for vehicle software update systems, mandating adversarial security testing that goes beyond functional verification. This section requires that manufacturers or their designated testing partners conduct structured penetration testing of the complete update system and submit results as part of the CCC certification package to MIIT. The requirements align with ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 10 (cybersecurity validation) penetration testing provisions while adding China-specific scope and reporting requirements.

The penetration assessment scope covers four attack surfaces: the vehicle-side update client, the server-side update infrastructure, the communication channel between vehicle and server, and the update package itself. For each attack surface, the assessment must evaluate confidentiality (can an attacker extract proprietary firmware or cryptographic keys), integrity (can an attacker modify update packages or inject unauthorized code), and availability (can an attacker prevent legitimate updates from being installed). The assessment methodology must follow a recognized framework — the standard references the OWASP Testing Guide, NIST SP 800-115, and China's own GB/T 20984 information security risk assessment methodology.

For the vehicle-side attack surface, penetration testers must attempt to extract firmware from the update client's storage, reverse-engineer the update verification logic, bypass signature verification through fault injection or glitching attacks, and exploit any debugging interfaces that remain accessible. For connected vehicles in China, testers must also evaluate whether the cellular or Wi-Fi interface used for OTA updates can be compromised to deliver malicious payloads.

Server-side assessment targets the update distribution infrastructure within Chinese territory. Since data localization requirements mandate that China-market update servers reside within China, the penetration assessment must cover the servers, load balancers, CDN nodes, and administrative interfaces located in Chinese data centers. Testing must include authentication bypass attempts, privilege escalation, injection attacks against update management APIs, and evaluation of server hardening against known vulnerability classes.

Communication channel assessment must evaluate the TLS or DTLS implementation protecting the update channel, including certificate validation, cipher suite selection, and resistance to protocol downgrade attacks. China requires that the communication channel support SM-series cipher suites (using SM2 for key exchange and SM4 for symmetric encryption) and that testing verify correct negotiation and enforcement of these cipher suites.

Reporting requirements are specific. The penetration assessment report must be written in Chinese, must classify findings using the CVSS v3.1 scoring system, must include proof-of-concept evidence for each finding, and must document the remediation applied by the manufacturer. CATARC and CAERI reviewers will evaluate the report as part of the CCC certification decision. Findings rated CVSS 7.0 or above must be remediated before certification is granted. V2X-related update components must be included in the penetration scope if the vehicle supports V2X communication, with specific test cases targeting the C-V2X security credential handling during the update process.

mappings.notes: Penetration results support type approval evidence packs.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857-2021 Vehicle Cybersecurity Technical Requirements
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40857-2021 is China's primary national standard for vehicle cybersecurity technical requirements, published by the Standardization Administration of China (SAC) in coordination with MIIT. It establishes the technical framework that vehicle manufacturers must implement to achieve cybersecurity compliance for vehicles sold in the Chinese market. The standard was developed with explicit reference to UN Regulation No. 155 (UN R155) and ISO/SAE 21434, and its clause structure maps closely to both international documents while incorporating China-specific requirements.

The standard maps to UN R155 paragraphs 7.2 (CSMS provisions) and 7.3 (vehicle type provisions). Where UN R155 requires manufacturers to demonstrate a certified Cybersecurity Management System and apply it to specific vehicle types, GB/T 40857 requires equivalent documentation but submitted through the Chinese CCC certification process administered by MIIT and SAMR rather than through a UNECE Contracting Party's type approval authority. Manufacturers holding UN R155 type approval can reuse significant portions of their CSMS documentation, but must translate materials into Chinese, supplement them with China-specific technical evidence, and submit through CATARC or CAERI.

GB/T 40857 is organized into sections covering organizational cybersecurity management (Section 5), vehicle development cybersecurity (Section 6), technical security requirements (Section 7), post-market monitoring (Section 8), and incident response (Section 9). Each section defines requirements that must be satisfied and evidence that must be produced. The standard applies to all M-category and N-category vehicles (passenger cars and commercial vehicles) equipped with electronic control systems connected to external networks, which effectively covers all new vehicles with telematics, OTA update capability, or V2X communication.

China-specific additions beyond the UN R155 baseline include mandatory use of Chinese cryptographic algorithms (SM2, SM3, SM4) for security functions where the manufacturer's cryptographic architecture permits algorithm selection, data localization requirements for vehicle-collected data under the Cybersecurity Law and PIPL, V2X security requirements aligned with China's C-V2X technology roadmap, and testing procedures conducted at Chinese national laboratories rather than manufacturer self-declaration. The cryptographic and data localization requirements reflect China's broader technology sovereignty policies and have no equivalent in R155 or ISO 21434.

Testing and verification follow the procedures defined in GB/T 40856, which specifies both functional verification and adversarial testing. The CCC certification process requires laboratory testing at CATARC or CAERI, followed by factory audit and ongoing surveillance. Certification must be renewed when significant changes are made to the vehicle's cybersecurity architecture or when new threat categories emerge that affect the vehicle type's risk profile.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 5: Organizational Requirements
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40857 Section 5 establishes organizational cybersecurity management requirements for vehicle manufacturers operating in the Chinese market. This section maps directly to UN R155 paragraph 7.2.2.2(a) (CSMS organizational provisions) and 7.2.2.5 (dependency management), and it requires manufacturers to demonstrate a functioning Cybersecurity Management System equivalent to that defined in ISO/SAE 21434 Clauses 5 through 7, adapted to Chinese regulatory expectations.

The organizational requirements begin with governance. The manufacturer must designate a cybersecurity governance structure with named responsible individuals, defined reporting lines, and documented authority to make cybersecurity-related decisions that affect vehicle design, production, and post-market operations. This governance structure must be documented in Chinese and submitted as part of the CCC certification package. MIIT reviewers expect to see an organizational chart showing cybersecurity roles, a responsibility assignment matrix (RACI or equivalent), and evidence that cybersecurity governance has board-level or executive committee visibility.

Competence and training requirements mandate that personnel involved in cybersecurity activities possess demonstrated qualifications. The standard does not prescribe specific certifications, but CATARC and CAERI auditors will evaluate training records, competence assessments, and continuing education programs. For China market compliance, at least key cybersecurity personnel must be able to communicate in Chinese with the type approval authority and testing laboratories. Manufacturers may use third-party cybersecurity engineering services, but must demonstrate oversight and accountability for outsourced activities.

The CSMS documentation package must address cybersecurity policy, risk management approach, threat intelligence processes, vulnerability management procedures, incident response plans, supplier cybersecurity management, and configuration management. China's requirements add data protection governance as a mandatory CSMS component — reflecting the Cybersecurity Law and PIPL obligations that do not appear in the UN R155 CSMS framework. The CSMS must describe how vehicle-collected data is classified, where it is stored (within China), who has access, and how cross-border transfer requests are evaluated against CAC security assessment requirements.

Supplier and dependency management requires the manufacturer to flow down cybersecurity requirements to tier-1 and critical tier-2 suppliers, verify supplier compliance through audits or assessment, and maintain a register of cybersecurity-relevant components with their supply chain provenance. For the China market, this includes verification that suppliers providing connected vehicle components comply with Chinese data localization requirements and use approved cryptographic implementations where applicable.

Continuous improvement is expected. The CSMS must include mechanisms for updating cybersecurity practices based on threat intelligence, vulnerability disclosures, incident lessons learned, and changes in the regulatory landscape. MIIT conducts periodic surveillance audits after initial CCC certification, and manufacturers must demonstrate that their CSMS has evolved in response to the changing threat environment. V2X-related organizational requirements include participation in China's V2X security incident sharing mechanisms and coordination with the national V2X security credential management authority.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 6: Vehicle Development Requirements
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40857 Section 6 defines vehicle development cybersecurity requirements, covering the activities that manufacturers must perform during the design and engineering phases of vehicle programs targeting the Chinese market. This section maps to UN R155 paragraphs 7.3.3 (threat identification), 7.3.4 (mitigation measures), and 7.3.6 (verification and validation), and it corresponds to ISO/SAE 21434 Clauses 9 through 12 covering cybersecurity concept, product development, and cybersecurity validation.

Threat analysis and risk assessment (TARA) is the foundational activity. The manufacturer must perform a structured analysis of cybersecurity threats applicable to the vehicle type, evaluate the risk associated with each threat considering both the likelihood of exploitation and the potential impact on vehicle safety, privacy, financial loss, and operational integrity. GB/T 40857 does not prescribe a specific TARA methodology but requires that the chosen approach be documented, repeatable, and consistent with the threat classification framework used in the manufacturer's CSMS. CATARC and CAERI reviewers will evaluate the TARA for completeness, checking that it covers all external interfaces, wireless communication channels, diagnostic ports, V2X interfaces, and the internal network architecture.

China-specific threat categories must be included in the TARA. These include threats to data localization controls (unauthorized cross-border data exfiltration), threats to V2X communication integrity in the Chinese C-V2X ecosystem, threats targeting Chinese cryptographic implementations (SM2/SM3/SM4 algorithm misuse or key compromise), and threats arising from the vehicle's integration with Chinese intelligent transportation infrastructure. The TARA must also consider threats related to the Chinese Cybersecurity Law and PIPL — specifically, privacy threats where vehicle-collected personal information could be accessed or transferred in violation of Chinese data protection requirements.

Security requirements derived from the TARA must be documented in a security requirements specification and traced to specific design decisions in the technical architecture. Each security requirement must have an associated verification method — analysis, testing, or inspection — and must be validated before vehicle type submission for CCC certification. The requirements specification must cover all electronic control systems connected to external networks and must specifically address gateway security (cross-referencing GB/T 40855), software update security (cross-referencing GB/T 40856), and data security (cross-referencing GB 44495).

Security testing during development must include both verification testing (confirming that security controls work as designed) and validation testing (confirming that the vehicle resists relevant attack scenarios). Testing procedures follow GB/T 40856 and must include fuzz testing of external interfaces, penetration testing of wireless communication channels, and verification of cryptographic implementations. Test reports must be written in Chinese and submitted as part of the CCC certification evidence package to MIIT through the designated testing laboratory.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 7: Technical Security Requirements
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40857 Section 7 specifies the technical security requirements that vehicle electronic systems must satisfy for China market type approval. This section maps to UN R155 paragraphs 7.3.4 (mitigation implementation) and 7.3.8 (data protection and integrity), and it provides the most detailed technical prescriptions in the GB/T 40857 standard. It corresponds to the technical requirements that emerge from the ISO/SAE 21434 cybersecurity concept and product development phases, but adds China-specific mandates for cryptographic algorithm selection, data handling, and V2X security.

Cryptographic requirements are central to Section 7. China mandates that vehicle cybersecurity functions support the national cryptographic algorithms: SM2 (elliptic curve cryptography for digital signatures and key exchange), SM3 (256-bit hash function), and SM4 (128-bit block cipher). These are defined in the GM/T series of standards published by the State Cryptography Administration (SCA). For the China market, manufacturers must implement SM-series algorithms in at minimum the following contexts: firmware signature verification, secure boot chain of trust, TLS/DTLS communication channel protection for V2X and telematics, OTA update package signing, and diagnostic session authentication. International algorithms (AES, RSA, ECDSA, SHA-256) may be used alongside SM-series algorithms in dual-stack configurations but cannot replace them for China market vehicles.

Secure communication requirements extend across all external interfaces. Telematics connections to backend servers must use TLS 1.2 or later with SM cipher suites. V2X communication must implement the security mechanisms defined in China's C-V2X standards, including certificate-based message authentication using SM2 signatures and pseudonym certificate management through the national security credential management system. Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, and UWB interfaces must implement authentication and encryption appropriate to the threat level identified in the TARA, with SM4 as the preferred symmetric cipher.

Data protection requirements in Section 7 implement the technical controls needed to satisfy the Cybersecurity Law, PIPL, and GB 44495. These include data classification enforcement (the vehicle must tag collected data with its classification level), access control (only authorized processes and personnel may access classified data), data minimization (the vehicle must not collect data beyond what is necessary for its stated functions), and audit logging (data access events must be logged for forensic review). Personal information processing must follow PIPL requirements including purpose limitation, explicit consent, and data subject rights enablement.

Hardware security requirements mandate the use of tamper-resistant secure elements or hardware security modules for storing cryptographic keys, performing signature operations, and anchoring the secure boot chain of trust. The HSM or secure element must be certified to at least CC EAL4+ or an equivalent Chinese certification level. Testing and verification of all Section 7 requirements is conducted per GB/T 40856 test procedures at CATARC or CAERI laboratories as part of the CCC certification process.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 8: Post-market Monitoring
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40857 Section 8 defines post-market monitoring requirements for vehicle cybersecurity, mandating that manufacturers maintain active surveillance of cybersecurity threats and vulnerabilities affecting vehicles already sold and operating in the Chinese market. This section maps to the UN R155 requirement for ongoing cybersecurity monitoring and corresponds to ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 13 (cybersecurity monitoring) and Clause 14 (cybersecurity event assessment and vulnerability management).

The monitoring obligation begins at the point of vehicle sale and continues throughout the vehicle's operational life. Manufacturers must establish and operate a cybersecurity monitoring function that continuously collects, analyzes, and acts on information about new threats, vulnerabilities, and attack techniques that could affect their vehicle types. Sources must include public vulnerability databases (CVE, NVD, CNVD — China's National Vulnerability Database), supplier vulnerability notifications, industry threat intelligence sharing organizations, and the manufacturer's own security research activities. China requires specific monitoring of CNVD and the China Information Security Evaluation Center (CNITSEC) publications, as Chinese-language vulnerability disclosures may precede international database entries for domestically discovered issues.

When a relevant vulnerability is identified, the manufacturer must perform a risk assessment to determine whether the vulnerability affects their vehicle types, estimate the severity using CVSS or an equivalent scoring system, and determine the appropriate response. Responses range from documentation-only (for theoretical vulnerabilities with no practical exploitation path) to emergency over-the-air security updates (for actively exploited critical vulnerabilities). The decision process and criteria must be documented in the CSMS and available for MIIT review during surveillance audits.

China-specific monitoring requirements include tracking regulatory changes from MIIT, SAMR, and the CAC that affect vehicle cybersecurity obligations, monitoring for threats specific to the Chinese V2X infrastructure (including attacks on the C-V2X security credential management system), and monitoring data protection incidents that could constitute violations of the Cybersecurity Law or PIPL. The manufacturer must maintain an incident register that records all cybersecurity events, their assessment, and the actions taken.

Reporting obligations under Chinese law require that significant cybersecurity incidents be reported to the relevant regulatory authority. For vehicle cybersecurity, this means notifying MIIT and potentially the CAC if personal data is involved. The notification timeline and content requirements are defined in implementing regulations under the Cybersecurity Law. Manufacturers must have pre-established reporting procedures and designated contacts at both MIIT and their designated testing laboratory.

Post-market monitoring data must comply with data localization requirements. If the manufacturer collects vehicle telemetry or diagnostic data from vehicles in China for monitoring purposes, this data must be stored within Chinese territory. Analysis may be performed by the manufacturer's global cybersecurity team, but the raw data must remain in China unless a CAC cross-border transfer assessment has been completed and approved. Testing of monitoring effectiveness is evaluated during MIIT surveillance audits and may include simulated vulnerability disclosure scenarios to verify the manufacturer's response procedures per GB/T 40856 testing protocols.

mappings.notes: Supports continual monitoring and vulnerability management.
Continual cybersecurity activities alignment.

clauses.title: GB 44495-2024 Vehicle Data Security
clauses.guidance:
GB 44495-2024 is China's mandatory national standard for vehicle data security, published as a compulsory standard (note the absence of "/T" indicating it is not voluntary). It implements the data security requirements of China's Cybersecurity Law (2017), Data Security Law (2021), and Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL, 2021) specifically for the automotive sector. Unlike the GB/T 408xx series which provide technical recommendations, GB 44495 carries the force of law and non-compliance can result in denial of CCC certification, production suspension, or administrative penalties.

The standard defines a comprehensive data governance framework for vehicles. It requires manufacturers to classify all data collected, generated, processed, and transmitted by the vehicle into categories defined by China's data classification scheme: general data, important data, and core data. Important data includes vehicle flow data, geographic mapping data exceeding certain precision thresholds, external environment image data, and any data that could affect national security if aggregated. Core data — rarely generated by individual vehicles but potentially arising from fleet aggregation — is subject to the most restrictive handling requirements.

Personal information protection under GB 44495 implements PIPL requirements in the vehicle context. The standard requires explicit, informed consent for collection of biometric data (facial recognition, fingerprint, voiceprint), location data, driving behavior data, and in-cabin audio or video recording. The vehicle must provide clear notification when data collection is active, must allow data subjects to withdraw consent, and must implement data deletion upon request. For China, these requirements apply regardless of whether the data subject is a Chinese citizen — PIPL has territorial application to data processing activities within China.

Data localization is the standard's most distinctive requirement relative to international frameworks. All personal information and important data collected by vehicles operating in China must be stored on servers physically located within Chinese territory. This applies to telematics data, navigation history, V2X message logs, camera and sensor recordings, diagnostic data, and OTA update telemetry. Cross-border data transfer is permitted only after completing a security assessment by the CAC, entering into standard contractual clauses with the overseas recipient, or obtaining a personal information protection certification. For most manufacturers, this means deploying China-specific data infrastructure.

The standard mandates technical controls including encryption of data in transit and at rest (using SM4 or approved algorithms), access control with role-based permissions, audit logging of all data access events, data anonymization capabilities for analytics use cases, and secure deletion procedures that prevent data recovery. Testing and certification of GB 44495 compliance is conducted alongside GB/T 40857 cybersecurity testing at CATARC or CAERI, with specific test procedures for verifying data classification enforcement, consent mechanisms, localization controls, and deletion completeness. V2X data — including V2X message content and communication metadata — is subject to the same classification and localization requirements as other vehicle data.

clauses.title: China CCC Certification Process
clauses.guidance:
The China Compulsory Certification (CCC) process is the mandatory market access mechanism for vehicles sold in China, administered by the Certification and Accreditation Administration of China (CNCA) with technical execution delegated to MIIT and SAMR. For connected and intelligent vehicles, CCC now includes cybersecurity requirements derived from GB/T 40857, GB/T 40856, GB/T 40855, and GB 44495, making cybersecurity certification a prerequisite for vehicle type approval rather than a separate or optional assessment.

The certification process begins with the manufacturer submitting an application to a designated certification body, accompanied by the complete documentation package. For cybersecurity, this package must include the CSMS documentation (per GB/T 40857 Section 5), threat analysis and risk assessment results (Section 6), technical security architecture documentation (Section 7), post-market monitoring procedures (Section 8), incident response plans (Section 9), software update management system documentation (per GB/T 40856), gateway security specifications (per GB/T 40855), and data security assessment results (per GB 44495). All documentation must be in Chinese.

Technical testing is conducted at MIIT-designated testing laboratories, primarily CATARC (Tianjin) and CAERI (Chongqing). Testing covers functional verification of security controls, penetration testing of external interfaces and communication channels, cryptographic implementation verification (including SM2/SM3/SM4 algorithm correctness), software update process testing, and data security controls verification. The testing phase typically takes three to six months depending on vehicle complexity and the number of findings requiring remediation. Manufacturers should budget for multiple test cycles, as initial submissions frequently generate findings that require design modifications.

Factory audit follows successful laboratory testing. Auditors verify that the production environment implements the cybersecurity controls documented in the certification package — including secure firmware flashing procedures, key provisioning processes, and quality controls for cybersecurity-relevant components. For manufacturers with Chinese joint venture production facilities, the factory audit covers the Chinese facility directly. For imported vehicles, the audit may cover the overseas production facility with ongoing import inspection requirements.

China-specific aspects of the CCC process that distinguish it from UN R155 type approval include the documentation language requirement (Chinese), the mandatory use of Chinese testing laboratories, the requirement to demonstrate compliance with data localization controls during testing, and the ongoing surveillance audit program. CCC certification must be maintained through annual surveillance and renewed when significant changes affect the vehicle type's cybersecurity architecture. V2X-equipped vehicles face additional certification requirements related to C-V2X protocol compliance and integration with China's intelligent transportation infrastructure. Manufacturers already holding UN R155 type approval can accelerate the CCC process by mapping their existing evidence to GB/T requirements, but must produce the China-specific supplements covering cryptographic algorithms, data localization, and V2X security.

clauses.title: Compliance audit evidence package
clauses.guidance:
The compliance audit evidence package for Chinese automotive cybersecurity certification is a structured collection of documents, test reports, and technical artifacts that manufacturers must assemble and submit to demonstrate conformity with GB/T 40857, GB/T 40856, GB/T 40855, and GB 44495. This package serves as the primary basis for CCC certification decisions by MIIT-designated testing laboratories (CATARC and CAERI) and must be comprehensive enough to satisfy both initial certification and ongoing surveillance audit requirements.

The evidence package is organized into five domains aligned with the GB/T 40857 structure: organizational evidence, development evidence, technical evidence, operational evidence, and data security evidence. Organizational evidence includes the CSMS policy document, organizational chart showing cybersecurity governance structure, role and responsibility definitions, training records for cybersecurity personnel, and supplier cybersecurity management procedures. All documents must be in Chinese or accompanied by certified Chinese translations. MIIT reviewers will verify that the organizational evidence reflects actual practice, not just documented intent — expect interview-based verification during factory audits.

Development evidence covers the threat analysis and risk assessment (TARA) methodology and results, security requirements specifications traceable to TARA findings, design documentation showing how security requirements are implemented in the vehicle architecture, verification and validation test plans and results, and evidence of security testing during development. For China market submissions, the TARA must include China-specific threat categories related to data localization, V2X security, and Chinese cryptographic requirements. Test reports must reference GB/T 40856 test procedures and must be issued by or validated by a MIIT-designated laboratory.

Technical evidence encompasses the security architecture document describing all cybersecurity controls, the cryptographic implementation document showing SM2/SM3/SM4 algorithm usage and key management procedures, gateway security specifications per GB/T 40855, software update system architecture and procedures per GB/T 40856, penetration test reports covering all external attack surfaces, and hardware security module certification evidence. For V2X-equipped vehicles, additional technical evidence includes the V2X security architecture, certificate management plan, and evidence of compatibility with China's C-V2X security credential management system.

Operational evidence covers post-market monitoring procedures, incident response plans, vulnerability management procedures, and evidence of integration with Chinese vulnerability databases (CNVD). Data security evidence, required under GB 44495, includes the data classification matrix, data flow diagrams showing where data is collected and stored, consent mechanism implementations, data localization architecture documentation proving that China-collected data remains within Chinese territory, and cross-border transfer assessment results if any data leaves China.

Manufacturers must maintain the evidence package as a living document set, updating it when the vehicle type is modified, when new threats emerge, or when regulatory requirements change. MIIT surveillance audits — conducted annually — will verify that the evidence package remains current and that the manufacturer has acted on any post-certification vulnerability discoveries or regulatory updates. The evidence retention period is the vehicle type's production lifetime plus ten years.

mappings.notes: Evidence pack structure aligns with cybersecurity case expectations.

clauses.title: Chinese Cryptographic Requirements
clauses.guidance:
Chinese cryptographic requirements for automotive cybersecurity derive from the Cryptography Law of the People's Republic of China (2020), the State Cryptography Administration (SCA) regulations, and the specific provisions of GB/T 40857 Section 7. These requirements mandate the use of China's national cryptographic algorithms — SM2, SM3, and SM4 — in vehicle cybersecurity functions for vehicles sold in the Chinese market. The requirements have no direct equivalent in UN R155 or ISO/SAE 21434, which are algorithm-agnostic, making this a significant China-specific compliance obligation.

SM2 is an elliptic curve cryptographic algorithm defined in GM/T 0003 and standardized as GB/T 32918. It provides digital signature and key exchange functionality equivalent to ECDSA and ECDH but uses a different curve (sm2p256v1) and signature scheme. Vehicle applications requiring SM2 include firmware signature verification in the secure boot chain, OTA update package signing, TLS handshake for telematics communication, V2X message signing for C-V2X communication, and diagnostic session authentication for security-critical UDS services.

SM3 is a 256-bit cryptographic hash function defined in GM/T 0004 and standardized as GB/T 32905. It provides integrity verification equivalent to SHA-256 and is used wherever hash computation is required: firmware integrity verification during secure boot, update package integrity checking, certificate fingerprinting, and hash-based message authentication codes (HMAC-SM3). SM3 replaces SHA-256 in China-market implementations wherever the manufacturer's cryptographic architecture allows algorithm substitution.

SM4 is a 128-bit block cipher defined in GM/T 0002 and standardized as GB/T 32907. It provides symmetric encryption equivalent to AES-128 and is used for data encryption at rest and in transit, session key encryption in TLS/DTLS connections, V2X message payload encryption where confidentiality is required, and diagnostic data protection. SM4 supports ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, and CTR modes of operation.

Implementation strategy for manufacturers with existing global cryptographic architectures typically follows a dual-stack approach: the vehicle's hardware security module stores both SM-series and international algorithm keys, and the cryptographic abstraction layer selects the appropriate algorithm based on the communication context. For China-market vehicles, all security-critical functions must use SM-series algorithms as the primary cryptographic mechanism. International algorithms may be retained as secondary options for interoperability with global backend systems, provided that SM-series algorithms are always available and preferred.

Hardware security module (HSM) selection is critical. The HSM must support SM2, SM3, and SM4 in addition to any international algorithms. Several automotive-qualified HSM vendors (Infineon, NXP, Renesas) offer SM-algorithm support in their automotive SoC security subsystems. Chinese HSM vendors (including TASS and Westone) also provide automotive-qualified products with native SM-series support. Testing of cryptographic implementations is conducted at CATARC and CAERI per GB/T 40856 test procedures, with specific test vectors validating correct SM2 signature generation and verification, SM3 hash computation, and SM4 encryption and decryption operations.

clauses.title: Data localization controls
clauses.guidance:
Data localization controls for the Chinese automotive market implement the technical and organizational measures required to comply with China's Cybersecurity Law (2017), Data Security Law (2021), Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL, 2021), and GB 44495-2024. These controls ensure that personal information and important data collected by vehicles operating in China remain stored within Chinese territory unless a regulatory exception has been obtained. For vehicle manufacturers, data localization is not optional — it is a prerequisite for CCC certification and ongoing market access.

The scope of data subject to localization is broad. It includes all personal information as defined by PIPL (data that can identify a natural person, alone or in combination with other data), all location and trajectory data, all external environment image and video data, all in-cabin biometric data, vehicle diagnostic and telemetry data when linked to identifiable vehicle owners, V2X communication logs, OTA update telemetry, and any data classified as "important data" under the Data Security Law. In practice, virtually all data generated by a connected vehicle's telematics, cameras, sensors, and communication systems falls within scope.

Technical implementation requires a China-specific data infrastructure architecture. Manufacturers must deploy telematics backend servers, data storage systems, and analytics platforms within Chinese data centers. Cloud infrastructure must use data centers physically located in mainland China — Hong Kong and Macau do not satisfy the localization requirement. Major cloud providers (Alibaba Cloud, Tencent Cloud, Huawei Cloud, and the China regions of AWS and Azure) offer compliant hosting. The vehicle's telematics control unit (TCU) must be configured to route all data transmissions to Chinese endpoints. DNS, connection failover, and load balancing configurations must ensure that no data is inadvertently routed to servers outside China.

Cross-border data transfer is permitted under specific conditions. The primary mechanism is a security assessment conducted by the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC), required when transferring personal information of more than one million data subjects or important data of any volume. Alternatively, manufacturers may enter into standard contractual clauses (SCCs) with the overseas data recipient or obtain a personal information protection certification from an accredited body. Each mechanism requires documented legal basis, data recipient security commitments, and ongoing compliance monitoring. For most global vehicle manufacturers with Chinese operations, the CAC security assessment is the applicable path.

Organizational controls include appointing a data protection officer or personal information protection responsible person based in China, establishing data classification procedures that apply the Chinese classification scheme, implementing access controls that restrict data access to authorized personnel, maintaining audit logs of all data access and transfer events, and conducting periodic data localization compliance assessments. These controls must be documented and available for MIIT and CAC review during certification and surveillance audits. V2X data presents particular localization challenges because V2X communication inherently involves real-time data exchange between vehicles and roadside infrastructure — manufacturers must ensure that V2X message logging and analytics data remain within China even when the V2X communication itself crosses jurisdictional boundaries.

mappings.notes: Jurisdictional constraints intersect regional implementation boundaries.

clauses.title: Chinese Cybersecurity Testing Requirements
clauses.guidance:
Chinese cybersecurity testing requirements for automotive type approval are defined primarily in GB/T 40856, with additional testing obligations specified in GB/T 40857, GB/T 40855, and GB 44495. Testing is conducted at MIIT-designated laboratories — principally CATARC (China Automotive Technology and Research Center, Tianjin) and CAERI (China Automotive Engineering Research Institute, Chongqing) — and constitutes a mandatory component of the CCC certification process. Testing results must demonstrate compliance with all applicable Chinese national standards before a vehicle type can be approved for sale in China.

The testing framework distinguishes between verification testing and validation testing. Verification testing confirms that individual security controls function as specified in the manufacturer's documentation — it answers "did we build the system right?" Validation testing confirms that the vehicle's cybersecurity posture resists real-world attack scenarios — it answers "did we build the right system?" Both categories are required. GB/T 40856 Section 7 defines functional verification procedures, and Section 8 defines penetration assessment (validation) procedures.

Functional verification testing covers every security control documented in the manufacturer's technical security architecture. For each control, the test laboratory executes defined test cases that verify correct operation under normal conditions, correct behavior under error conditions, and correct handling of boundary cases. Specific test areas include secure boot verification (confirming firmware integrity check at every boot), cryptographic function verification (confirming correct SM2/SM3/SM4 implementation using published test vectors), communication security verification (confirming TLS/DTLS session establishment with correct cipher suites), access control verification (confirming that unauthorized access attempts are rejected and logged), gateway filtering verification (confirming correct message filtering per GB/T 40855), and software update verification (confirming the complete update lifecycle per GB/T 40856).

Penetration testing follows a structured methodology. The test laboratory performs threat modeling based on the manufacturer's TARA, identifies high-priority attack scenarios, and executes them against a production-representative test vehicle. Attack categories include external interface exploitation (cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, USB, OBD-II), network protocol attacks (CAN injection, Ethernet MITM, V2X spoofing), application-layer attacks (infotainment system exploitation, API abuse), and physical attacks (debug port access, chip-level extraction, fault injection). For China specifically, penetration testing must include V2X attack scenarios relevant to the C-V2X ecosystem and verification that data localization controls resist circumvention attempts.

Testing timeline typically requires three to six months from initial vehicle submission to final test report issuance. Manufacturers should plan for iterative testing — initial submissions frequently reveal findings that require remediation and retesting. Scheduling laboratory time at CATARC or CAERI should be initiated six to nine months before the target certification date, as laboratory capacity is limited and demand is increasing as more connected vehicles enter the Chinese market.

clauses.title: Chinese V2X Security Requirements
clauses.guidance:
Chinese V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) security requirements address the cybersecurity of vehicle communication systems within China's national intelligent connected vehicle (ICV) ecosystem. China has committed to the C-V2X (Cellular Vehicle-to-Everything) technology path, based on 3GPP LTE-V2X (PC5 interface) and NR-V2X specifications, rather than the DSRC/802.11p approach used in some other markets. This technology choice drives specific security requirements that differ from those in UN R155 and ISO/SAE 21434, which are technology-neutral on the V2X communication method.

The C-V2X security architecture in China is built on a hierarchical certificate management system. At the top level, the national Root Certificate Authority (Root CA) is operated under the authority of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT). Intermediate CAs issue enrollment certificates to on-board units (OBUs) and roadside units (RSUs), and pseudonym CAs issue short-lived pseudonym certificates that enable vehicles to authenticate V2X messages without revealing their permanent identity. The certificate management system uses SM2 for digital signatures, SM3 for certificate fingerprinting, and the certificate format follows Chinese national standards that extend the IEEE 1609.2 certificate structure with SM-algorithm support.

Security requirements for V2X on-board units include: certificate enrollment and lifecycle management (requesting, installing, renewing, and revoking certificates), pseudonym certificate management (maintaining a pool of pseudonym certificates and rotating them to prevent tracking), message signing and verification (every transmitted V2X message must be signed with the sender's current pseudonym certificate, and every received message must have its signature verified before the application layer processes it), misbehavior detection (identifying V2X participants that send false or manipulated messages), and certificate revocation list management (receiving and applying CRL updates from the certificate authority infrastructure).

V2X data handling falls under China's data localization requirements. V2X communication logs, including Basic Safety Messages (BSMs) and other V2X application data, constitute vehicle telemetry data that must be stored within Chinese territory under the Cybersecurity Law and PIPL. Manufacturers must ensure that V2X data analytics and logging infrastructure is hosted in Chinese data centers. If V2X data is used for fleet analytics, traffic management research, or safety analysis, the data processing must comply with GB 44495 data classification and consent requirements.

Testing of V2X security is conducted as part of the CCC certification process at CATARC and CAERI, following test procedures derived from GB/T 40856 and supplemented by V2X-specific test standards. Test scenarios include certificate validation testing (verifying that OBUs correctly validate certificates and reject invalid or revoked certificates), message integrity testing (verifying signature generation and verification for all V2X message types), pseudonym rotation testing (verifying that OBUs rotate pseudonym certificates at the required interval without disrupting V2X communication), and interoperability testing (verifying that the OBU communicates correctly with China's C-V2X roadside infrastructure). Manufacturers must also demonstrate that the V2X security implementation does not introduce latency that degrades the safety-critical V2X applications, as China's C-V2X deployment roadmap includes collision avoidance and intersection management applications with strict latency requirements.

clauses.title: Overview
clauses.guidance:
ISO 14229 (Unified Diagnostic Services) defines the application-layer diagnostic protocol used by nearly every modern automotive ECU. From a cybersecurity perspective, UDS is one of the most significant attack surfaces in a vehicle because it provides direct read, write, and execution capabilities over the CAN bus, DoIP, and other transport layers. Every service defined in ISO 14229 represents a potential entry point for an attacker who gains access to the vehicle's diagnostic interface, whether through the OBD-II port, a compromised telematics unit, or a connected aftermarket device.

The security model of UDS relies on a layered approach. At the base layer, the diagnostic session (0x10) gates which services are available. Above that, SecurityAccess (0x27) and Authentication (0x29) control whether a client is authorized to invoke privileged services. SecuredDataTransmission (0x84) provides payload-level integrity and confidentiality. This layered model means that a weakness at any single layer can cascade: an attacker who bypasses session control may reach services that assume authentication has already occurred.

ISO 14229-1:2020 introduced the Authentication service (0x29) as a modern replacement for the seed-key mechanism of SecurityAccess (0x27). OEMs should treat this as a migration signal. New ECU designs should implement 0x29 with certificate-based authentication, while continuing to support 0x27 only where backward compatibility is required, and only with hardened seed-key algorithms.

From a regulatory standpoint, UNECE R155 Annex 5 requires OEMs to demonstrate that diagnostic interfaces cannot be exploited to compromise vehicle cybersecurity. ISO 21434 clause 10 (product development at the cybersecurity level) requires that each UDS service be evaluated during the TARA process for its attack feasibility and potential impact. The combination of these regulatory requirements means that every service in this standard must be assessed for cybersecurity risk, not just the obvious security-related services.

Implementors should maintain a diagnostic service allowlist for each ECU, explicitly mapping which services are permitted in which sessions and under which authentication states. Services not required for production operation should be disabled or removed from the ECU firmware entirely. Defense-in-depth measures such as message authentication (SecOC), network segmentation, and intrusion detection complement the service-level controls defined in this standard.

clauses.title: Authentication (0x29)
clauses.guidance:
The Authentication service (0x29) was introduced in ISO 14229-1:2020 as the modern replacement for the legacy SecurityAccess (0x27) seed-key mechanism. It provides certificate-based mutual authentication between diagnostic clients and ECUs, enabling cryptographically strong identity verification that addresses the fundamental weaknesses of symmetric seed-key schemes. For any ECU design starting after 2020, 0x29 should be the primary authentication mechanism, with 0x27 retained only for backward compatibility with legacy tooling under strict deprecation timelines.

The service supports multiple authentication sub-functions, most significantly the APCE (Authentication Protocol with Challenge-response using Extended data) flow. In a typical APCE exchange, the client sends a certificate chain to the ECU, the ECU validates the chain against its trust anchor, and both parties complete a challenge-response handshake. This establishes a session key that can be used for subsequent SecuredDataTransmission (0x84) operations. The protocol supports both unilateral (client authenticates to ECU) and mutual (both parties authenticate) modes.

PKI infrastructure is the backbone of 0x29 implementation. Each OEM must operate or contract a certificate authority hierarchy that issues diagnostic certificates. Short-lived certificates are preferred because they reduce the exposure window if a certificate is compromised. The ECU must store a trust anchor (root CA certificate or its hash) in tamper-resistant storage.  Certificate revocation must be handled through either short certificate lifetimes (hours to days for online testers) or CRL distribution for offline workshop testers. An ECU that cannot verify revocation status should deny authentication rather than assume the certificate is valid.

Hardware security module (HSM) integration is critical for both the ECU side and the tester side. The ECU's private key for mutual authentication must be generated and stored within an HSM or secure element. Key extraction from the ECU must be infeasible. On the tester side, diagnostic tools should use hardware tokens or TPM-backed certificate storage rather than software-only key storage, which is vulnerable to extraction by malware on the diagnostic PC.

Session elevation is a key architectural concept. After successful 0x29 authentication, the ECU grants the client elevated privileges that persist for the duration of the diagnostic session. These privileges should be scoped: a certificate for a workshop technician should grant different access than a certificate for an OEM engineering team. Role-based access control can be encoded in certificate extensions (X.509v3 custom extensions) or in the APCE negotiation parameters. This granularity is a major advantage over 0x27, which typically offers only a binary locked/unlocked state per security level.

The relationship to SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication) matters for in-vehicle deployment. While SecOC authenticates individual CAN frames at the network layer, 0x29 authenticates the diagnostic session at the application layer. Both are needed: SecOC prevents spoofing of normal operational messages, while 0x29 prevents unauthorized diagnostic access. They operate at different layers and are complementary, not redundant.

Common implementation pitfalls include: accepting self-signed certificates during development and failing to enforce chain validation in production; storing the ECU private key in unprotected flash memory; implementing the challenge-response with insufficient randomness in the nonce generation; and failing to bind the authenticated session to a specific transport connection (allowing session hijacking if the attacker can inject messages on the same CAN bus after authentication completes). Each of these represents a CAL-relevant vulnerability under ISO 21434 that must be addressed during the product cybersecurity design phase.

R155 Annex 5 paragraph 7.2.2.2 requires that diagnostic interfaces implement adequate authentication. Deploying 0x29 with a properly managed PKI is currently the strongest available mechanism to satisfy this requirement for new vehicle architectures.

mappings.notes: Supports authenticated diagnostic operations in connected environments.

clauses.title: Communication control (0x28)
clauses.guidance:
The CommunicationControl service (0x28) allows a diagnostic tester to suppress or enable normal communication on specific ECU network interfaces. Typical uses include silencing CAN traffic during reprogramming (to free bandwidth) and disabling NM (Network Management) messages to prevent the ECU from entering sleep mode during extended service procedures. While this service appears operationally benign, it creates significant cybersecurity risks when misused or inadequately protected.

The primary threat is denial of service through communication suppression. An attacker with CAN bus access who can invoke 0x28 without authentication can silence an ECU's normal network participation. In a safety-critical context, this means an ECU might stop transmitting brake status, steering angle, or airbag readiness messages. Other ECUs on the bus that depend on these messages may enter fallback modes, degrade functionality, or trigger diagnostic trouble codes. The cascading effect across a vehicle network can be severe, particularly for ECUs that implement time-critical safety functions.

From an implementation standpoint, CommunicationControl should never be accessible in the Default diagnostic session. It should require at minimum an Extended session, and for safety-critical ECUs, it should require prior SecurityAccess (0x27) or Authentication (0x29) authorization. The ECU should validate the controlType parameter strictly, rejecting any reserved or undefined values rather than defaulting to an enable/disable action.

Network segmentation provides an important layer of defense. If the diagnostic interface is on a gateway-separated segment, an attacker who compromises a single domain (such as infotainment) cannot reach 0x28 on powertrain or chassis ECUs. OEMs should ensure that gateway firewall rules block diagnostic service requests originating from non-diagnostic network segments.

A subtle vulnerability exists with the communicationType sub-parameter, which selects which network interfaces are affected. An implementation that allows suppression of the ECU's entire communication stack (including its security-relevant heartbeat messages) creates a more dangerous attack surface than one that only permits suppression of normal application messages while maintaining safety-relevant transmissions.

ISO 21434 TARA should evaluate CommunicationControl as an attack vector for each ECU that implements it. The threat scenario is straightforward: an attacker uses 0x28 to suppress an ECU's outgoing messages, then exploits the resulting degraded state of other ECUs or the vehicle as a whole. Mitigations include authentication gating, timeout-based auto-recovery (where the ECU resumes normal communication after a defined period even without an explicit re-enable command), and intrusion detection rules that flag unexpected 0x28 requests.

clauses.title: Control DTC setting (0x85)
clauses.guidance:
The ControlDTCSetting service (0x85) allows a diagnostic tester to temporarily suppress or re-enable DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code) recording on an ECU. In normal workshop use, technicians invoke this service before performing operations that would otherwise trigger false fault codes, such as disconnecting sensors during component replacement or flashing new firmware. From a cybersecurity perspective, this service represents a detection-evasion mechanism that an attacker can abuse to blind the vehicle's onboard fault monitoring.

The core threat is that an attacker who can invoke 0x85 to disable DTC recording can then perform malicious operations (memory writes, routine executions, firmware modifications) without leaving diagnostic evidence. DTCs serve as a form of audit trail in automotive systems, and suppressing them is analogous to disabling security logging on an IT system. Any post-incident forensic analysis that relies on DTC history will be incomplete if DTC recording was suppressed during the attack.

To mitigate this risk, ControlDTCSetting should be gated behind authentication. At minimum, it should require an Extended diagnostic session, and the ECU should log the fact that DTC recording was disabled as a separate, non-suppressible event. This meta-event (recording that DTC recording was turned off) should persist in non-volatile memory and be visible to subsequent diagnostic reads, even if the DTCs themselves were suppressed during the window.

OEMs should implement automatic DTC re-enablement after a timeout period. If a tester disables DTC recording and then loses communication (or if an attacker disables recording and walks away), the ECU should automatically resume DTC recording after a configurable period, typically 30-60 seconds. This prevents permanent suppression through a single unauthorized request.

The interaction between 0x85 and session management is important. If the ECU falls back to the Default session (due to session timeout or TesterPresent failure), DTC recording should automatically re-enable. The ECU should not retain a suppressed DTC state across session transitions or power cycles.

R155 does not specifically call out DTC suppression, but the general requirement to detect and respond to cyberattacks (Annex 5, 7.3) implies that an OEM's monitoring capability should not be trivially disabled through a diagnostic service. During ISO 21434 TARA, the scenario where an attacker suppresses DTC recording before executing a payload should be explicitly evaluated, particularly for ECUs in the powertrain and ADAS domains where DTC evidence may be relevant to safety investigations.

clauses.title: DTC reporting and clearing
clauses.guidance:
The DTC reporting (0x19) and clearing (0x14) services provide the primary interface for reading and managing diagnostic trouble codes stored in an ECU. Service 0x19 (ReadDTCInformation) supports multiple sub-functions for querying DTC status, reading snapshot records (freeze frames), and retrieving extended data associated with fault events.
