**Evidence for type approval.** CIAs support the evidence required for R155 paragraphs 7.2.2.5 and 7.3.2 demonstrating supply chain cybersecurity management. Auditors may request to see CIAs for critical components and evidence that supplier deliverables were reviewed against CIA requirements.

clauses.title: Guidelines for impact rating
clauses.guidance:
Annex F provides guidance on using vulnerability scoring systems, primarily CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System), within the automotive cybersecurity context.

**CVSS in automotive context.** CVSS is the industry-standard vulnerability scoring system maintained by FIRST. The base score evaluates: attack vector (network, adjacent, local, physical), attack complexity (low, high), privileges required (none, low, high), user interaction (none, required), and impact on confidentiality, integrity, and availability. While CVSS was designed for IT systems, it can be adapted for automotive use. However, direct application without contextualization can be misleading — a CVSS 9.8 vulnerability in a library used within an ECU that has no network connectivity may present minimal actual risk in the automotive deployment.

**Temporal and environmental scores.** CVSS provides temporal metrics (exploit code maturity, remediation level, report confidence) and environmental metrics (modified base metrics reflecting the specific deployment) that are particularly important for automotive. The environmental score allows adjusting the base score to reflect the actual vehicle context — reduced attack surface, compensating controls, and specific deployment conditions. Organizations should calculate environmental scores rather than relying solely on base scores for risk decisions.

**Integration with TARA.** CVSS scores can be used as an input to the attack feasibility rating (Clause 15.7) and impact rating (Clause 15.5). Map CVSS exploitability metrics to the attack potential factors in Annex G: CVSS attack vector maps to window of opportunity, CVSS attack complexity maps to specialist expertise and elapsed time, and CVSS privileges required maps to knowledge of the item. CVSS impact metrics (confidentiality, integrity, availability) map to the impact categories in Clause 15.5. This mapping must be documented and consistently applied.

**Alternative scoring systems.** Annex F also acknowledges other scoring approaches: CWSS (Common Weakness Scoring System) for design weaknesses rather than specific vulnerabilities, proprietary OEM scoring systems calibrated for automotive risk profiles, and hybrid approaches that combine CVSS with automotive-specific factors. Whichever system is used, it must be documented, consistently applied, and produce results that align with the organization's risk matrix.

**Common pitfalls.** Treating CVSS base scores as absolute risk indicators without environmental contextualization leads to misallocation of remediation effort — fixing a CVSS 9.0 vulnerability in an air-gapped ECU while ignoring a CVSS 5.0 vulnerability in a remotely accessible telematics unit inverts actual risk priorities. Another common error is using CVSS scores from the NVD without verifying they accurately reflect the automotive deployment context. Automated vulnerability scanners that report raw CVSS scores without contextual analysis create noise that overwhelms the vulnerability management process.

**Evidence for type approval.** Vulnerability scoring methodology should be documented as part of the CSMS. For R155 paragraph 7.2.2.2(g), auditors expect to see evidence of structured vulnerability analysis — CVSS scores with environmental adjustments demonstrate a systematic approach.

reference_tables.title: Safety impact rating criteria
reference_tables.description: Safety impact criteria mapped from ISO 26262-3:2018

rows.criteria: S3: Life-threatening injuries (survival uncertain), fatal injuries
S2: Severe and life-threatening injuries (survival probable)
The financial damage leads to catastrophic consequences which the affected road user might not overcome.
The financial damage leads to substantial consequences which the affected road user will be able to overcome.
The financial damage leads to inconvenient consequences which the affected road user will be able to overcome with limited resources.
The financial damage leads to no effect, negligible consequences or is irrelevant to the road user.
Loss or impairment of a core vehicle function. Example: Vehicle not working or unexpected behaviour of core functions such as enabling limp home mode.
Loss or impairment of an important vehicle function. Example: Significant annoyance of the driver.
Partial degradation of a vehicle function. Example: User satisfaction negatively affected.
No impairment or non-perceivable impairment of a vehicle function.
Significant or even irreversible impact to the road user. Information is highly sensitive and easy to link to a PII principal.
Serious impact to the road user. Information is: (a) highly sensitive and difficult to link to a PII principal, or (b) sensitive and easy to link to a PII principal.
Inconvenient consequences to the road user. Information is: (a) sensitive but difficult to link to a PII principal, or (b) not sensitive but easy to link to a PII principal.
No effect, negligible consequences or irrelevant. Information is not sensitive and difficult to link to a PII principal.

clauses.normative_text:
Annex F provides example criteria for impact rating (see 15.5) for damage scenarios involving safety, financial, operational and privacy damage. The tables (Table F.1 through Table F.4) can be used for impact rating.

Considerations on how the scalability of damage (impact to multiple road users in a single damage scenario) modify the impact rating have not been included, but can be added to organization-specific rating criteria.

clauses.title: Guidelines for attack feasibility rating
clauses.guidance:
Annex G defines methods for rating attack feasibility, directly supporting Clause 15.7. This annex is frequently referenced by auditors and is one of the most operationally significant parts of the standard.

**Attack potential method.** The primary method evaluates five factors, each scored on a defined numeric scale: (1) Elapsed time — from less than one day (0 points) to more than six months (19 points), representing the time an attacker needs for research, development, and execution; (2) Specialist expertise — from layperson (0) to multiple experts across different domains (8), representing the skill level required; (3) Knowledge of the item — from public information (0) to critical/restricted information requiring insider access (11), representing what the attacker must know about the target; (4) Window of opportunity — from unlimited/easy access (0) to very difficult single-opportunity access (10), representing access constraints; (5) Equipment — from standard/commodity equipment (0) to multiple bespoke/custom-developed tools (9). The sum of all factor scores produces the attack potential value. Score ranges map to feasibility levels: 0-9 = high feasibility (attack is easy), 10-13 = medium, 14-19 = low, 20-24 = very low, 25+ = infeasible under current assumptions.

**Scoring guidance.** When scoring elapsed time, consider both the development time (creating the attack tool or technique) and the execution time (performing the attack against a target vehicle). For specialist expertise, consider whether the attacker needs automotive-specific knowledge, cryptographic expertise, hardware reverse engineering skills, or RF engineering capabilities. For knowledge of the item, consider whether system documentation is publicly available, whether the ECU firmware can be extracted and analyzed, and whether the network architecture can be inferred from publicly available diagnostic databases. For window of opportunity, consider whether the attack requires proximity to the vehicle, physical access to a port, or can be performed remotely at any time. For equipment, consider whether the attack can be performed with a laptop and off-the-shelf hardware (CAN interfaces, SDR dongles) or requires expensive laboratory equipment (chip probing stations, signal analyzers).

**CVSS-based method.** As an alternative, map CVSS v3.x exploitability metrics to feasibility levels: remote attack vector with low complexity and no privileges required maps to high feasibility; physical attack vector with high complexity and high privileges maps to very low feasibility. This method is simpler but less granular than the attack potential method.

**Common pitfalls.** Inconsistent scoring across analysts is the most common problem. Two analysts rating the same attack scenario may produce scores differing by 10+ points without calibration. Conduct calibration sessions using reference scenarios. Another failure is scoring based on theoretical difficulty rather than demonstrated capability — if an attack technique has been demonstrated at a security conference, the elapsed time and expertise scores should reflect reproduction effort, not original research effort. Over-relying on the window of opportunity factor (assuming physical access is very difficult) ignores insider threats, service access, and scenarios where an attacker has purchased the same vehicle model.

**Evidence for type approval.** Attack feasibility ratings with factor-level scores are required in [WP-15-06]. Auditors will examine scoring rationale, check for consistency, and compare against known automotive attack capabilities.

levels.label: Layman
levels.description: Unknowledgeable compared to experts, with no particular expertise. Example: Ordinary person using step-by-step descriptions of an attack that is publicly available.

levels.label: Proficient
levels.description: Knowledgeable, familiar with the security behaviour of the product or system type. Example: Experienced owner, ordinary technician knowing simple attacks like odometer tuning.

levels.label: Expert
levels.description: Familiar with underlying algorithms, protocols, hardware, structures, security behaviour, principles and concepts, techniques and tools for defining new attacks. Example: Experienced technician or engineer.

levels.label: Multiple experts
levels.description: Different fields of expertise required at expert level for distinct steps of an attack. Example: Multiple highly experienced engineers with expertise in different fields.

reference_tables.title: Knowledge of the item or component

levels.label: Public information
levels.description: Public information concerning the item or component (e.g. as gained from the Internet). Example: Information published on the product homepage or internet forum.

levels.label: Restricted information
levels.description: Restricted information concerning the item or component (e.g. knowledge controlled within developer organization, shared under NDA). Example: Internal documentation shared between manufacturer and supplier.

levels.label: Confidential information
levels.description: Confidential information about the item or component (e.g. knowledge shared between discrete teams, access constrained to team members). Example: Immobilizer-related information, software source code.

levels.label: Strictly confidential information
levels.description: Strictly confidential information (e.g. known by only a few individuals, access very tightly controlled). Example: Customer specific calibrations or memory maps.

levels.label: Unlimited
levels.description: High availability via public/untrusted network without time limitation. Remote access or unlimited physical access. Example: Remote attack via cellular interface, unlimited physical access by owner.

levels.label: Easy
levels.description: High availability and limited access time. Remote access without physical presence. Example: Bluetooth pairing time, remote software update, remote attack requiring vehicle standing still.

levels.label: Moderate
levels.description: Low availability. Limited physical and/or logical access. Physical access to vehicle interior/exterior without special tools. Example: Attacker enters unlocked car, access via OBD port.

levels.label: Difficult
levels.description: Very low availability. Impractical level of access. Example: Decapping an IC, cracking a cryptographic key by brute force faster than key rotation.

levels.label: Standard
levels.description: Readily available to the attacker. Part of the product itself or readily obtained. Example: Laptop, CAN adapter, OBD dongle, screwdriver, soldering iron.

levels.label: Specialized
levels.description: Not readily available but can be acquired without undue effort. Moderate amounts of equipment or extensive attack scripts. Example: Hardware debugging device, HiL test rig, high-grade oscilloscope, signal generator.

levels.label: Bespoke
levels.description: Specially produced, not readily available to the public, or very expensive. Example: Manufacturer-restricted tools, electron microscope.

levels.label: Multiple bespoke
levels.description: Different types of bespoke equipment required for distinct steps of an attack.

reference_tables.title: Attack potential aggregation — numeric values
reference_tables.description: Sum all factor values to get attack potential. Based on ISO/IEC 18045.

reference_tables.title: Attack potential to feasibility mapping
reference_tables.description: Maps sum of attack potential factors to feasibility rating

reference_tables.title: CVSS exploitability to feasibility mapping
reference_tables.description: Maps CVSS exploitability values (E = 8.22 x V x C x P x U) to feasibility
reference_tables.formula: E = 8.22 x V x C x P x U, where V=attack_vector (0.2-0.85), C=attack_complexity (0.44-0.77), P=privileges_required (0.27-0.85), U=user_interaction (0.62-0.85). Range: 0.12-3.89.

reference_tables.title: Attack vector-based feasibility approach
reference_tables.description: Simplified approach suitable for concept phase when insufficient information for detailed attack paths

mapping.description: Potential attack path bound to network stack without limitation. Example: Cellular network making ECU directly accessible on internet.
Attack path bound to network stack but connection limited physically or logically. Example: Bluetooth interface, VPN connection.
Attack path not bound to network stack; requires direct access. Example: USB mass storage device, memory card.
Requires physical access to realize the attack path.

clauses.normative_text:
Annex G provides guidelines on applying three approaches for attack feasibility rating (see 15.7): attack potential-based (G.2), CVSS-based (G.3), and attack vector-based (G.4).

Attack potential is defined in ISO/IEC 18045 as a measure of the effort to be expended in attacking an item or component, expressed in terms of an attacker's expertise and resources. It relies on five core parameters: elapsed time, specialist expertise, knowledge of the item or component, window of opportunity, and equipment.

The sum of all factor scores produces an attack potential value (Table G.6). Ranges map to feasibility levels (Table G.7): 0-9 = High, 10-13 = High, 14-19 = Medium, 20-24 = Low, >=25 = Very low.

The CVSS-based approach uses exploitability metrics: E = 8.22 x V x C x P x U (Table G.8).

The attack vector-based approach is a simplified method suitable for the concept phase when insufficient information is available for detailed attack paths (Table G.9).

clauses.title: Examples of application of TARA methods — headlamp system
clauses.guidance:
Annex H provides example approaches for conducting the TARA methodology, offering practical guidance for organizations implementing Clause 15.

**STRIDE per element.** Apply STRIDE analysis to each element in the system data flow diagram: for every process, data store, data flow, and external entity, systematically consider Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, and Elevation of Privilege threats. This method provides thorough coverage but can generate a large number of threat scenarios for complex systems. Manage this by focusing detailed analysis on elements at trust boundaries (where data crosses from one security domain to another) and grouping similar threats across elements.

**Attack tree analysis.** Build attack trees for each cybersecurity goal or high-value asset. The root node represents the attacker's objective, and child nodes represent sub-goals or alternative approaches. Leaf nodes represent atomic attack steps that can be individually assessed for feasibility. Attack trees provide a visual, structured representation that facilitates discussion with stakeholders and can be maintained and updated as the system evolves. Tools like Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool, ThreatGet, and Ansys medini analyze support automated attack tree generation from system models.

**Kill chain analysis.** Adapt the cyber kill chain model for automotive: (1) reconnaissance — gathering information about the vehicle model, ECU firmware versions, and communication protocols; (2) weaponization — developing attack tools and exploits; (3) delivery — establishing a communication channel to the target (wireless, physical, supply chain); (4) exploitation — executing the attack against a vulnerability; (5) installation — establishing persistent access if needed; (6) command and control — maintaining the ability to issue commands to the compromised system; (7) actions on objectives — achieving the attacker's goal. This framework helps identify detection and disruption opportunities at each stage.

**Hybrid approaches.** In practice, most organizations use a combination: STRIDE per element for systematic identification, attack trees for detailed path analysis of high-priority threats, and kill chain analysis for evaluating multi-stage attacks against connected vehicles. The choice of method should be documented in the cybersecurity plan (Clause 6) and applied consistently across the organization.

**Common pitfalls.** Using a single method exclusively creates blind spots — STRIDE may miss multi-stage attack chains, attack trees may miss threats not related to the explicitly modeled goals, and kill chain analysis may not cover all STRIDE categories. Generating threat scenarios without connecting them to the subsequent TARA steps (impact rating, feasibility rating, risk determination) produces analysis that has no actionable outcome. Teams that treat TARA methods as a one-time exercise rather than a living analysis miss threats introduced by design changes, new components, or evolving attacker capabilities.

**Evidence for type approval.** The TARA methodology must be documented and consistently applied. Auditors expect to see the chosen methods, their application to the specific system, and traceability from identified threats through the complete TARA process to risk treatment decisions.
clauses.normative_text:
EXAMPLE TARA: Headlamp System

Item definition: The headlamp system turns on/off the headlamp in accordance with the switch by demand of the driver. If the headlamp is in high-beam mode, the headlamp system switches automatically to low-beam when an oncoming vehicle is detected, and returns to high-beam when the oncoming vehicle is no longer detected.

Operational environment: Connected to gateway ECU, which connects to navigation ECU. Navigation ECU has Bluetooth and cellular interfaces with firewall. Gateway ECU has OBD-II interface with strong security controls (CAL4).

STEP 1 — Asset Identification (Table H.2):
- Data communication (lamp request): Integrity, Availability. Damage: vehicle cannot be driven at night; front collision with stationary object.
- Data communication (oncoming car info): Integrity, Availability. Damage: oncoming drivers blinded; malfunctioning automatic high beam.
- Firmware of body control ECU: Confidentiality, Integrity.

STEP 2 — Impact Rating (Table H.3):
- Vehicle cannot be driven at night → Operational: Major
- Front collision with stationary object at medium speed → Safety: Severe (S3)
- Malfunctioning automatic high beam → Operational: Moderate

STEP 3 — Threat Scenario Identification (Table H.4):
- Spoofing of lamp request signal → loss of integrity → headlamp turns off unintentionally
- Tampering with signal from body control ECU → loss of integrity → headlamp turns off unintentionally
- DoS of oncoming car information → loss of availability → headlamp always low beam

STEP 4 — Attack Path Analysis (Table H.5):
Path A: Attacker compromises navigation ECU via cellular → malicious control signals → gateway forwards → spoofs lamp request (OFF)
Path B: Attacker compromises navigation ECU via Bluetooth → same chain
Path C: Attacker gets local access to OBD connector → sends malicious signals → gateway forwards → spoofs lamp request (OFF)
Path D: Attacker compromises navigation ECU via cellular → floods communication bus → DoS of oncoming car info
Path E: Attacker attaches BT OBD dongle → compromises smartphone → sends via BT dongle → gateway forwards → floods bus

STEP 5 — Attack Feasibility Rating:
Attack vector-based (Table H.6): Path A (cellular) = High; Path B (Bluetooth) = Medium; Path C (OBD) = Low
Attack potential-based (Table H.7): DoS via cellular: ET=1, SE=8, KoIC=7, WoO=0, Eq=4, Sum=20 → Low; DoS via BT dongle: ET=1, SE=8, KoIC=7, WoO=4, Eq=4, Sum=24 → Low

STEP 6 — Risk Value Determination (Table H.8 risk matrix):
Matrix: Impact(Severe) x Feasibility(High) = Risk 5; Impact(Moderate) x Feasibility(Low) = Risk 2
Results: Spoofing of lamp request (Severe, High) → Risk 5; DoS of oncoming car info (Moderate, Low) → Risk 2
Formula alternative: R = 1 + I x F (where I and F are numerical values from Table H.10)

STEP 7 — Risk Treatment Decision (Table H.11):
- Spoofing of lamp request: Risk 5 → Reducing the risk
- DoS of oncoming car info: Risk 2 → Reducing the risk

rows.damage: Vehicle cannot be driven at night; Front collision with stationary object caused by unintended headlamp off
Oncoming drivers blinded; Malfunctioning automatic high beam always low
Vehicle cannot be driven at night
Front collision with stationary object at medium speed
Malfunctioning automatic high beam always low

reference_tables.title: Numerical values for risk formula R = 1 + I x F

clauses.title: Overview
clauses.guidance:
ISO 24089 defines the engineering requirements for software update management systems (SUMS) in road vehicles. Published as a companion to UN Regulation No. 156, it establishes processes that vehicle manufacturers and their suppliers must follow when designing, implementing, and operating over-the-air (OTA) and workshop-based software update capabilities. The standard covers the complete update lifecycle from organizational governance through package engineering, campaign execution, post-deployment monitoring, and end-of-support planning.

The OTA update architecture addressed by ISO 24089 spans backend distribution infrastructure, in-vehicle update agents, and the communication channels between them. Backend systems must support campaign orchestration, package staging, fleet targeting, and telemetry collection. In-vehicle components must implement secure download, integrity verification, installation sequencing, and rollback mechanisms. The standard requires that this architecture be documented, version-controlled, and subject to change management throughout the vehicle lifecycle.

Version management is central to ISO 24089. Each software component in a vehicle must be uniquely identifiable through version identifiers that trace back to release artifacts, build records, and verification evidence. The standard mandates that the manufacturer maintain a software bill of materials (SBOM) that maps every deployed version to its configuration baseline. This version tracking supports both regulatory compliance under UN R156 and operational troubleshooting when field issues arise.

Update pipeline security receives detailed treatment. All update packages must be cryptographically signed before distribution, and vehicles must verify these signatures before installation. The signing infrastructure must be protected against compromise, with key management procedures that address rotation, revocation, and incident response. Secure boot chains must ensure that only authenticated software executes on target ECUs. These requirements align directly with ISO 21434 Clause 10 on product development and Clause 13 on operations and maintenance, because the update channel is itself an attack surface that requires threat analysis and risk assessment.

UN R156 requires manufacturers to demonstrate a functioning SUMS as a condition of type approval. ISO 24089 provides the engineering detail that fills the process requirements in R156 Paragraph 7, making it the primary implementation guide for meeting regulatory obligations. Organizations that conform to ISO 24089 produce the work products and process evidence that type approval authorities expect when evaluating SUMS applications. This relationship makes ISO 24089 the engineering foundation that translates regulatory intent into repeatable practice.

clauses.title: Deployment safety controls
clauses.guidance:
Clause 10 of ISO 24089 defines deployment safety controls — the requirements that ensure software updates do not create hazardous conditions during installation. Deployment is the most safety-critical phase of the update lifecycle because it involves modifying executable code on systems that may control vehicle dynamics, powertrain, braking, or steering. The standard requires that the deployment process be designed with safety as the primary constraint.

The deployment safety case [UPD-10-01] must document all identified hazards associated with the update installation process and the controls implemented to mitigate them. Hazards include loss of vehicle control during an update that modifies a safety-relevant ECU, loss of propulsion when an update requires a full system restart, loss of lighting or signaling during nighttime installation, and unintended vehicle movement if the update affects park lock or brake hold functions. Each hazard must have documented mitigations that are verified through testing.

Fail-safe behavior during deployment requires that the update agent monitor the installation process and detect failures in real time. If a failure is detected, the system must abort the installation and either roll back to the previous version or place the ECU in a defined safe state. The safe state definition must be derived from the functional safety analysis under ISO 26262 — the update system must know which states are safe for each ECU and must be able to reach those states even when the update process itself has failed.

The update pipeline must enforce deployment sequencing rules. When multiple ECUs must be updated together, the installation order must respect dependency relationships and safety constraints. For example, a gateway ECU that routes update packages to sub-ECUs should not be updated before its downstream targets are prepared for the communication protocol changes. The deployment orchestrator must enforce these sequences and handle partial completion scenarios where some ECUs update successfully while others fail.

UN R156 Paragraph 7 requires that updates be performed safely, and ISO 24089 Clause 10 provides the detailed engineering requirements that demonstrate compliance. The deployment safety controls must be traceable to safety goals from ISO 26262 HARA and to cybersecurity goals from ISO 21434 TARA. A deployment process that is safe against random hardware failures but vulnerable to cyberattack does not meet the combined regulatory expectations. The intersection of deployment safety and update channel security is where ISO 24089, ISO 26262, and ISO 21434 must work together as an integrated system of requirements.

mappings.notes: Addresses safe execution requirements for updates.
Deployment safety intersects with cybersecurity risk controls.

clauses.title: Vehicle state preconditions
clauses.guidance:
Clause 10 also defines vehicle state preconditions — the conditions that must be satisfied before an update installation can begin. These preconditions act as the first line of defense against unsafe deployment by ensuring that the vehicle is in a state where an update can be performed without creating hazardous conditions. The update agent must evaluate all preconditions before proceeding and must refuse to install if any precondition is not met.

Battery state of charge is a fundamental precondition. OTA updates consume significant electrical power during download, verification, and flashing operations. If the battery depletes during installation, the ECU may be left in a partially programmed state that cannot boot. The standard requires a minimum state of charge threshold that accounts for the worst-case energy consumption of the update process plus a safety margin. For battery electric vehicles, the traction battery state of charge may also be relevant if the update affects the battery management system.

Vehicle motion state is a safety-critical precondition. Updates that modify safety-relevant ECUs must not be performed while the vehicle is in motion. The update agent must verify that the vehicle speed is zero, the transmission is in park (or neutral with parking brake engaged), and the ignition state is appropriate for the update type. Some updates to infotainment or telematics systems may be permitted while driving if they do not affect vehicle dynamics, but the precondition logic must enforce this distinction based on the target ECU classification.

Network connectivity preconditions must verify that the communication channel between the backend and the vehicle is stable and has sufficient bandwidth for the download. The system must also verify that the in-vehicle network (CAN, Ethernet, or other bus) is available for inter-ECU communication during the update. If the vehicle is in a degraded network state — for example, after a bus-off event — the update must be deferred until normal communication is restored.

Environmental preconditions may include ambient temperature (flash memory write operations have temperature limits), geographic location (some updates may be market-specific), and time of day (updates may be restricted to hours when the vehicle is typically parked). The precondition evaluation must be logged for audit purposes, supporting the traceability requirements of UN R156. From a cybersecurity perspective per ISO 21434, the precondition check itself must be tamper-resistant — an attacker who can bypass precondition checks could force updates to install in unsafe conditions, creating both safety and security incidents.

mappings.notes: Update preconditions are required for safe deployment.

clauses.title: Campaign KPI monitoring
clauses.guidance:
Clause 11 also addresses campaign KPI monitoring — the quantitative tracking of campaign performance metrics that drive operational decisions. While Clause 11's monitoring and response requirements focus on detecting problems, KPI monitoring provides the broader operational view that measures campaign effectiveness, identifies process improvement opportunities, and generates evidence of SUMS performance for type approval authorities.

Campaign KPIs must cover the full lifecycle of a campaign deployment. Download KPIs include download initiation rate (how many targeted vehicles have started downloading), download completion rate, average download duration, retry rates, and bandwidth utilization. Installation KPIs include installation success rate, average installation duration, rollback frequency, and failure categorization (precondition failures, verification failures, flash failures, post-installation check failures). Adoption KPIs track the percentage of the targeted fleet that has successfully received and retained the update over time.

Quality KPIs measure the update's impact on vehicle health after deployment. These include DTC rates compared to pre-update baselines, warranty claim rates for updated versus non-updated populations, customer complaint rates, and dealer workshop visit rates. Tracking these KPIs over an extended observation window (typically 30 to 90 days post-campaign) provides statistical evidence of update quality that supports continuous improvement.

Anomaly rate monitoring requires defining expected baselines for each KPI and setting threshold values that trigger alerts when observed rates deviate significantly from expectations. For example, if the historical installation failure rate across previous campaigns is 0.3%, a new campaign showing a 2% failure rate should trigger immediate investigation. These thresholds must be calibrated for each vehicle platform, market, and update type because baseline rates vary significantly across these dimensions.

Campaign KPI data feeds directly into the SUMS performance evidence that UN R156 type approval authorities review. Demonstrating consistent campaign success rates, low failure rates, and effective anomaly response across multiple campaigns provides confidence that the manufacturer's update management processes are mature and effective. From an ISO 21434 perspective, KPIs related to security update campaigns — time from vulnerability disclosure to campaign launch, security update adoption rate, mean time to patch across the fleet — are cybersecurity performance indicators that demonstrate the manufacturer's ability to respond to threats in the field.

mappings.notes: Operational monitoring supports SUMS performance evidence.

clauses.title: Monitoring and response
clauses.guidance:
Clause 11 of ISO 24089 establishes requirements for post-deployment monitoring and incident response during and after update operations. Monitoring is not optional or advisory — it is a required element of the SUMS that must operate continuously while campaigns are active and for a defined observation period after campaign completion. The standard recognizes that even thorough pre-deployment testing cannot anticipate every interaction between the update and the diversity of field conditions across a global fleet.

Post-deployment monitoring requires collecting telemetry from updated vehicles that indicates whether the update is functioning as intended. This telemetry includes diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs), error logs, performance metrics, and user complaints routed through dealer networks. The monitoring system must aggregate this data and present it in a form that allows campaign operators to assess fleet health and identify emerging issues. The backend infrastructure must support real-time data ingestion from thousands of vehicles simultaneously, with the analytics capacity to detect anomalous patterns against baseline behavior.

Anomaly detection must distinguish between pre-existing issues, coincidental failures, and update-induced problems. Statistical methods should compare failure rates in updated vehicles against control populations of non-updated vehicles to establish whether observed issues correlate with the update. The monitoring plan must define thresholds for anomaly severity that trigger escalation actions including campaign pause, targeted investigation, or full campaign abort with rollback initiation.

Incident response procedures must be defined before the campaign begins. When monitoring detects a confirmed update-induced issue, the response plan must specify who is notified, what investigation activities begin, what interim mitigations are available (such as disabling an affected feature remotely), and under what conditions a fleet-wide rollback is initiated. The incident response timeline must be aggressive enough to limit the number of affected vehicles when a serious issue is identified.

This clause directly supports both UN R156 and ISO 21434. UN R156 requires ongoing SUMS effectiveness, and monitoring provides the evidence. ISO 21434 Clause 8 on continual cybersecurity activities and Clause 13 on operations and maintenance require that the manufacturer monitor for cybersecurity incidents in deployed products — the monitoring infrastructure for updates serves double duty as part of the cybersecurity monitoring capability. Work product [UPD-11-01] captures the monitoring report that documents what was observed, what actions were taken, and what the outcomes were for each campaign.

mappings.notes: Supports post-deployment control in SUMS.
Supports vulnerability and incident management in CSMS.
Continual cybersecurity activities alignment.

clauses.title: Supplier and interface coordination
clauses.guidance:
Clause 12 of ISO 24089 defines requirements for supplier coordination in the context of software update engineering. Modern vehicles contain ECUs from dozens of Tier-1 and Tier-2 suppliers, each responsible for the software running on their components. When the vehicle manufacturer operates a SUMS that distributes updates to these ECUs, the interfaces between the manufacturer and each supplier must be formally defined, contractually governed, and technically validated.

Supplier interface agreements must specify the update package format, signing requirements, metadata schema, version numbering conventions, and delivery timelines for each updateable component. The agreement must define who is responsible for each activity in the update lifecycle — typically the supplier engineers and tests the update package for their ECU while the manufacturer integrates it into the vehicle-level update campaign, manages distribution, and monitors deployment. Responsibility boundaries must be unambiguous, especially for activities like security signing (does the supplier sign their package, does the manufacturer re-sign it, or both?).

The OTA update architecture must accommodate supplier-specific constraints. Different suppliers may use different flash programming protocols, have different ECU memory layouts, require different installation sequences, or support different rollback mechanisms. The manufacturer's update agent must abstract these differences behind a common interface while correctly implementing supplier-specific procedures for each target ECU. This architectural requirement must be addressed in the SUMS design documentation.

Supply chain security is a critical concern covered by this clause. Update packages received from suppliers must be verified for integrity and authenticity before inclusion in a vehicle-level update campaign. The manufacturer must have processes to detect tampered or unauthorized packages, verify that the package was produced in the supplier's authorized build environment, and confirm that the package has not been modified in transit. This connects to ISO 21434 Clause 7 on distributed cybersecurity activities, which requires that cybersecurity responsibilities in the supply chain be clearly allocated and verified.

Work product [UPD-12-01] captures the supplier update interface agreement. UN R156 holds the vehicle manufacturer responsible for the SUMS regardless of which supplier produced the software being updated. This means the manufacturer must have sufficient visibility into and control over supplier update processes to demonstrate compliance. The supplier coordination framework in this clause provides the structure for achieving that visibility while respecting the supplier's engineering autonomy for their specific components.

mappings.notes: Supports supply-chain process assurance.
Distributed cybersecurity activities alignment.

clauses.title: Third-party dependency control
clauses.guidance:
Clause 12 also addresses third-party dependency control for update packages and the broader update supply chain. Modern automotive software increasingly relies on third-party libraries, open-source components, and commercially licensed middleware. When these dependencies are included in update packages distributed through the SUMS, they introduce risks that must be managed through specific governance processes.

Dependency management requires maintaining an accurate software bill of materials (SBOM) for every update package. The SBOM must list all third-party components, their versions, their licenses, and their known vulnerability status at the time of package release. When a new vulnerability is disclosed in a third-party component, the manufacturer must be able to quickly identify which update packages contain the affected component and which vehicles have received those packages. This traceability is essential for responding to vulnerability disclosures with targeted update campaigns.

Supply chain provenance verification ensures that third-party components included in update packages are authentic and have not been tampered with. This includes verifying that open-source components were obtained from authoritative repositories, that commercial components were delivered through secure channels with integrity verification, and that the build process that incorporated these components into the final package is reproducible and auditable. Dependency confusion attacks — where a malicious package with the same name as a legitimate internal dependency is substituted — must be addressed through build pipeline controls.

Version pinning and dependency lock files must be used to ensure that builds are reproducible. Floating dependency versions (such as "latest" or version ranges) are prohibited in production builds because they make it impossible to guarantee that two builds of the same source code produce identical output. This reproducibility requirement supports both quality assurance and forensic investigation of field issues.

From a cybersecurity perspective per ISO 21434, third-party dependencies are part of the attack surface of the update package. The TARA for the update system must include threat scenarios related to compromised dependencies, and mitigations must include dependency scanning, SBOM maintenance, and vulnerability monitoring. UN R156 does not directly address software dependencies, but the manufacturer's obligation to ensure that updates do not introduce vulnerabilities implicitly requires dependency management. CAL-relevant classifications apply when third-party components execute in safety-relevant or security-relevant contexts, elevating the rigor of dependency verification from routine quality assurance to formal security assessment.

mappings.notes: Supply-chain dependency control supports cybersecurity risk management.

clauses.title: Audit retention controls
clauses.guidance:
Clause 13 also defines audit retention controls — the policies and mechanisms that determine how long update-related records are preserved and how they are made available for audit. Retention is a regulatory obligation: type approval authorities can audit the SUMS at any time during the production lifecycle, and the manufacturer must be able to produce complete records for any update campaign, any vehicle, and any time period within the retention window.

Retention scope covers all artifacts in the update lifecycle: requirement documents, engineering change records, test plans and results, approval records, package build logs, signing certificates, campaign plans, deployment telemetry, monitoring reports, incident records, rollback logs, and user notification records. The retention policy must define minimum retention periods for each artifact category, taking into account regulatory requirements (UN R156 references national type approval retention periods, typically 10 years beyond production end), contractual obligations, and the manufacturer's own quality and litigation needs.

Storage and access controls for retained records must ensure integrity, availability, and confidentiality. Records must be stored in tamper-evident systems where modifications are detectable. Access must be controlled so that only authorized personnel can retrieve records, with access logs that track who viewed what and when. The retention system must support efficient retrieval — an auditor requesting records for a specific campaign or VIN must receive complete, organized documentation within a reasonable timeframe, not a disorganized archive that requires manual assembly.

Digital signatures and timestamps on retained records provide non-repudiation — proof that a specific record existed at a specific time and has not been altered since. This is particularly important for approval records and signing certificates, where the integrity of the historical record has direct regulatory significance. If a question arises about whether a specific update was properly approved before deployment, the retained records must provide definitive evidence.

UN R156 Paragraph 9 explicitly addresses conformity of production, requiring that the manufacturer maintain evidence of ongoing SUMS compliance. ISO 24089 Clause 13 retention controls produce this evidence systematically rather than reconstructing it on demand when an audit is announced. From an ISO 21434 perspective, retained records support cybersecurity incident investigation — when a vulnerability is discovered, investigators may need to review update records spanning years to understand the deployment history of affected components and assess the fleet-wide exposure.

mappings.notes: Conformity-of-production audits need retained evidence.

clauses.title: Traceability and documentation
clauses.guidance:
Clause 13 of ISO 24089 establishes documentation and traceability requirements for the entire update lifecycle. Documentation is not a bureaucratic exercise — it is the mechanism through which the manufacturer demonstrates to type approval authorities, auditors, and internal stakeholders that the SUMS operates as designed and that every update distributed to the fleet was developed, tested, and deployed through controlled processes.

Traceability must connect requirements to implementation to verification to deployment. For each update package, the documentation must trace the requirement that motivated the update (defect report, feature request, security vulnerability, regulatory change) through the engineering changes made, the test cases executed and their results, the approval decision and its basis, the campaign plan and execution, and the post-deployment monitoring outcomes. This end-to-end traceability chain is work product [UPD-13-01] — the update evidence package.

Version management documentation must maintain a complete history of software configurations deployed to the fleet. For each vehicle identification number (VIN), the manufacturer must be able to determine what software versions are currently installed, what versions were previously installed, when each update was applied, and whether any rollbacks occurred. This fleet-wide software ledger is a core UN R156 requirement and must be maintained for the lifetime of the vehicle type plus any applicable retention period.

Update pipeline documentation must describe the technical architecture of the distribution system including backend components, communication protocols, security mechanisms, and in-vehicle update agents. Changes to the update infrastructure must be documented with the same rigor as changes to vehicle software. The signing infrastructure documentation must include key management procedures, certificate chains, and the HSM configuration used for package signing.

ISO 21434 Clause 6 requires a cybersecurity case that documents the cybersecurity argument for the product. The update system documentation must contribute to this cybersecurity case by demonstrating that the update channel is secure, that packages are integrity-protected, that the distribution infrastructure resists relevant threat scenarios, and that monitoring detects anomalies. UN R156 Paragraph 7 requires comprehensive documentation as evidence for type approval, and Paragraph 9 extends this to conformity of production audits where the authority may review documentation at any time during the production lifecycle. Without the documentation framework established in this clause, the manufacturer cannot sustain its type approval status.

mappings.notes: Documentation required for type approval and conformity assessments.
Cybersecurity case and evidence linkage.

clauses.title: End-of-support and decommissioning
clauses.guidance:
Clause 14 of ISO 24089 addresses end-of-support and decommissioning planning for update-capable products. Every vehicle with OTA update capability has a finite support horizon during which the manufacturer commits to providing software updates. This clause requires that the support horizon be defined during product planning, communicated to customers at purchase, and managed through formal processes as the end-of-support date approaches.

The end-of-support policy [UPD-14-01] must define the manufacturer's commitments regarding update availability for each vehicle model. This includes the minimum support duration (often driven by regulatory requirements and market expectations), the types of updates that will continue during the support period (safety, security, regulatory, functional), and the notice period before support ends. The policy must also address what happens to vehicles after support ends — whether they continue to operate with their last received software or whether certain features are deprecated.

Support horizon communication must be proactive and multi-channel. Vehicle buyers must be informed at the point of sale about the expected update support period. Approaching end-of-support notifications must begin well before the actual date, giving owners time to make informed decisions about vehicle retention, sale, or upgrade. The notification system described in Clause 8 must support these lifecycle communications in addition to individual update notifications.

The OTA distribution infrastructure must support end-of-support scenarios. This includes decommissioning campaign targeting for end-of-support models, archiving telemetry and configuration data for vehicles leaving active support, and potentially distributing a final update that configures the vehicle for unsupported operation (for example, ensuring that the update agent stops presenting update notifications for updates that will never come).

ISO 21434 Clause 14 addresses end of cybersecurity support and requires that the manufacturer consider the cybersecurity implications of ending software updates. A vehicle that no longer receives security updates accumulates known vulnerabilities over time, creating increasing risk. The manufacturer must assess whether the residual risk is acceptable and communicate it to vehicle owners. This cybersecurity end-of-support assessment must be coordinated with the general end-of-support process defined in ISO 24089. UN R156 addresses production-discontinued scenarios in Paragraph 11, and ISO 24089 Clause 14 provides the engineering process that implements these obligations.

mappings.notes: Links to production-discontinued and support lifecycle obligations.
End of cybersecurity support alignment.

clauses.title: Sunset execution strategy
clauses.guidance:
Clause 14 also covers sunset execution strategy — the operational process of transitioning fielded vehicles from active support to end-of-support status. While the end-of-support policy defines the rules, sunset execution implements those rules across the fleet in a controlled, transparent, and traceable manner. This is not simply turning off a server — it requires careful coordination across engineering, operations, customer relations, and regulatory affairs.

The sunset execution plan must define the timeline and milestones for transitioning each vehicle model out of active support. This timeline must account for regulatory obligations that may extend beyond the manufacturer's standard support period — for example, safety-related recalls may require updates to be distributed even after the general support period has ended. The plan must identify which obligations persist after general support ends and ensure that the infrastructure and processes needed to fulfill those obligations remain available.

Fleet monitoring during the sunset period requires tracking which vehicles have received their final updates and confirming that those updates installed successfully. Vehicles that fail to receive the final update before support infrastructure is decommissioned may be left in an undesirable software state. The sunset plan must define procedures for handling these vehicles, which may include extending the support infrastructure temporarily or providing workshop-based update options as a fallback.

The OTA distribution infrastructure decommissioning must follow a controlled process. Backend servers, update repositories, signing infrastructure, and monitoring systems associated with the sunset model must be decommissioned in a defined sequence that ensures no active vehicles lose connectivity before receiving their final updates. Signing keys and certificates associated with the model must be archived according to the retention policy in Clause 13 rather than destroyed, because they may be needed for forensic investigation or regulatory inquiry years after support ends.

From a security perspective, sunset execution must address the vulnerability gap that begins when security updates stop. ISO 21434 requires that the manufacturer assess residual cybersecurity risk for unsupported vehicles and communicate this risk transparently. The sunset plan should include a final security assessment that documents known unpatched vulnerabilities, their severity, and any available mitigations that vehicle owners can apply independently (such as disabling connected features that increase attack surface). UN R156 holds the manufacturer accountable for update management throughout the vehicle's lifecycle, and sunset execution must demonstrate that this accountability was discharged responsibly even as active support ends.

mappings.notes: Support sunset planning aligns with production-discontinued obligations.

clauses.title: Organizational governance
clauses.guidance:
Clause 5 of ISO 24089 establishes the organizational governance framework for software update engineering. It requires the vehicle manufacturer to define and maintain policies that govern how software updates are developed, approved, distributed, and monitored across the vehicle fleet. These policies must assign clear roles and responsibilities, including an accountable owner for the SUMS who has authority over release decisions and the organizational resources to enforce update process compliance.

The governance structure must define decision gates for update approval, including criteria for who can authorize a release, what evidence must be reviewed, and what conditions must be met before deployment begins. This includes establishing a change advisory board or equivalent body that evaluates the safety, security, and functional impact of proposed updates before they enter the distribution pipeline. The board must have representation from functional safety, cybersecurity, quality, and operations functions to ensure that cross-domain risks are identified and addressed.

OTA update architecture governance requires that the backend infrastructure, communication protocols, and in-vehicle update agents operate under documented configuration management. Changes to the update distribution system itself must follow the same controlled process as changes to vehicle software. This prevents situations where infrastructure modifications introduce vulnerabilities or compatibility issues that affect the entire fleet.

Alignment with ISO 21434 is explicit at the governance level. The cybersecurity management system (CSMS) required by ISO 21434 Clause 5 must integrate with the SUMS governance framework. The cybersecurity policy must address update-specific risks including unauthorized package injection, man-in-middle attacks on distribution channels, and compromise of signing keys. Joint governance ensures that cybersecurity considerations are embedded in update decisions rather than treated as an afterthought.

UN R156 Paragraph 7.1 requires the manufacturer to demonstrate organizational processes for managing software updates. ISO 24089 Clause 5 provides the detailed governance requirements that satisfy this obligation. Type approval evidence includes the governance policy, role definitions, decision authority matrices, and evidence of management review. Without this governance foundation, subsequent clauses on planning, engineering, and deployment lack the organizational authority needed for consistent execution across programs and platforms.

mappings.notes: Directly supports SUMS governance expectations.
Organizational cybersecurity governance alignment.

clauses.title: Project planning and responsibilities
clauses.guidance:
Clause 6 of ISO 24089 covers project planning and lifecycle responsibilities for software update engineering. It requires that each vehicle program with update capability have a documented update lifecycle plan that defines the scope of updateable components, the planned update cadence, resource allocation, and the interfaces between development, operations, and supplier organizations. This plan must be established during the concept phase and maintained throughout the vehicle's production and post-production life.

Planning must address the OTA distribution infrastructure required for the program, including bandwidth requirements, server capacity, content delivery network design, and redundancy provisions. The plan must account for fleet size projections, concurrent download limits, and geographic distribution of vehicles to ensure that the infrastructure can support campaign execution without degradation. Workshop update infrastructure planning must address diagnostic tool compatibility, workshop network requirements, and dealer training needs.

Version management planning requires defining the versioning scheme for all updateable components, establishing baseline configurations for each vehicle variant, and documenting the dependency relationships between software components. The plan must specify how version conflicts are detected and resolved, how configuration drift is identified, and how the manufacturer maintains an accurate view of the software state across the fleet. This version management strategy feeds directly into the campaign targeting logic covered in Clause 8.

Rollback planning is a required element. The update lifecycle plan must define which components support rollback, how rollback is triggered (automatic or manual), what the expected rollback duration is, and how rollback events are reported and investigated. Not all components can support rollback equally — the plan must identify constraints such as hardware dependencies, calibration data incompatibilities, or safety implications that limit rollback options for specific ECUs.

The planning clause establishes the bridge between ISO 24089 and ISO 21434 Clause 6 on project-dependent cybersecurity management. The update lifecycle plan must reference the cybersecurity plan and identify where update-related cybersecurity activities (threat analysis of the update channel, security testing of update packages, key management planning) integrate with the overall project timeline. UN R156 requires evidence of planned processes, and this clause produces the work products — specifically the update lifecycle plan [UPD-06-01] — that demonstrate planning rigor to type approval authorities.

mappings.notes: SUMS planning and process evidence.
Project-level cybersecurity management alignment.

clauses.title: Delta update strategies
clauses.guidance:
Clause 7 also addresses delta and differential update strategies, which reduce the data volume transferred during OTA campaigns. Instead of distributing complete firmware images, delta updates contain only the differences between the currently installed version and the target version. This approach significantly reduces bandwidth consumption, download time, and cellular data costs — factors that directly affect campaign completion rates and user acceptance.

The standard requires that delta update generation follow a controlled process. The delta algorithm must be validated to produce correct output for every supported source-target version pair. This means maintaining a compatibility matrix that tracks which delta paths have been tested and approved. If a vehicle's current version falls outside the tested delta paths, the system must fall back to a full image update rather than attempting an untested delta. The version management system must track which vehicles are candidates for delta versus full updates based on their reported software baseline.

Delta packages introduce unique integrity challenges. The standard requires that both the delta package itself and the resulting installed image be integrity-verified. Post-installation verification must confirm that the reconstructed image matches the expected hash of the target version, not just that the delta was applied without errors. This two-stage verification prevents scenarios where a corrupted source image combined with a valid delta produces an incorrect result that passes delta-level checks but fails image-level validation.

Rollback considerations for delta updates require careful engineering. If a delta update fails during application, the system must be able to restore the previous version. This may require maintaining a backup of the pre-update image or implementing an A/B partition scheme where the new version is written to an inactive partition while the current version remains intact on the active partition. The standard requires that the rollback mechanism be tested for every delta update scenario.

From a security perspective, delta updates must receive the same signing and verification treatment as full updates. An attacker who can predict the delta algorithm might craft a malicious delta that, when applied to a known source version, produces compromised firmware. The signing process must therefore operate on the final delta package, and the vehicle must verify this signature before applying the delta. This aligns with ISO 21434 threat analysis requirements — the delta update mechanism itself must be included in the TARA scope for the update system. UN R156 does not distinguish between delta and full updates; both must meet the same integrity and traceability requirements.

mappings.notes: Contributes to controlled and efficient software update management.

clauses.title: Update package engineering
clauses.guidance:
Clause 7 of ISO 24089 addresses update package engineering, covering how software updates are composed, protected, validated, and prepared for distribution. This clause is where the technical substance of update security meets practical implementation. Each update package must have a defined structure that includes the payload, metadata describing the target component and version, dependency declarations, installation instructions, and integrity protection mechanisms.

Package composition must follow a controlled process where source artifacts are traced from version control through build pipelines to final release packages. The build environment must be reproducible, and the relationship between source code commits, build outputs, and released packages must be auditable. This traceability supports both quality assurance and incident investigation — when a field issue is reported, engineers must be able to reconstruct exactly what was built and deployed.

Cryptographic signing is mandatory for all update packages. The standard requires that packages be signed using asymmetric cryptography with key lengths and algorithms appropriate to the vehicle's expected lifetime. The signing process must occur in a controlled environment, with private keys stored in hardware security modules (HSMs) and access restricted to authorized personnel. Key management procedures must address key generation, distribution to vehicles, rotation schedules, revocation mechanisms, and compromise response. These requirements connect directly to ISO 21434 Clause 10, which requires secure implementation practices for cybersecurity-relevant components.

Integrity verification at the vehicle side must validate the package signature before installation begins. The vehicle's secure boot chain must ensure that the verification code itself has not been tampered with. Failed verification must block installation and generate a diagnostic event. The standard also addresses partial download recovery, requiring that interrupted transfers can resume without restarting from scratch while maintaining integrity guarantees.

Package engineering must account for UN R156 requirements on software identification. Each package must carry a software identification number (RXSWIN or equivalent) that the type approval authority can use to verify the approved software version. The package metadata must enable the backend to track which vehicles have received which versions, supporting the fleet-wide software configuration management that R156 requires manufacturers to maintain. Work product [UPD-07-01] captures the package specification that documents these requirements for each updateable component.

mappings.notes: Supports secure update preparation and execution controls.
Product development and secure implementation linkage.

clauses.title: Campaign planning and rollout
clauses.guidance:
Clause 8 of ISO 24089 defines campaign management — the process of planning, targeting, executing, and monitoring the distribution of software updates across a vehicle fleet. A campaign is the operational unit that connects a validated update package to its intended recipients. Campaign management transforms engineering artifacts into fleet-wide deployments, making it the operational heart of the software update management system.

Campaign planning requires defining the scope (which vehicles, which regions, which variants), the timeline (start date, rollout phases, completion target), and the success criteria (target installation rate, maximum acceptable failure rate, escalation thresholds). The campaign plan must identify any prerequisites such as minimum battery state, required network connectivity, or geographic restrictions (for example, regulatory approval may not yet cover all markets). These prerequisites feed directly into the vehicle state preconditions covered in Clause 10.

Staged rollout is a required practice. The standard mandates that campaigns begin with a controlled pilot phase targeting a small subset of the fleet before expanding to broader populations. The pilot phase must include defined observation periods during which telemetry data is analyzed for anomalies before proceeding to the next stage. Stage gates define the criteria for advancement, pausing, or aborting the campaign. This approach limits blast radius when an update causes unexpected behavior in the field.

Fleet monitoring during campaign execution requires real-time visibility into download progress, installation success rates, failure categories, and vehicle health indicators. The backend infrastructure must collect and aggregate telemetry from participating vehicles, presenting campaign operators with dashboards that support informed decisions about campaign progression. Anomaly detection should identify patterns such as unusual failure clustering by vehicle variant, geographic region, or hardware revision that may indicate a systematic issue requiring campaign suspension.

The campaign management process must integrate with ISO 21434 operations and maintenance activities (Clause 13). Security-critical updates — those addressing known vulnerabilities or responding to active exploits — may require accelerated campaign timelines that compress or adapt the standard staged rollout. The campaign plan must define expedited procedures for security updates while maintaining minimum safety verification gates. UN R156 requires that the manufacturer demonstrate the ability to manage update campaigns effectively, and ISO 24089 Clause 8 provides the process framework that produces the campaign plan [UPD-08-01] and execution evidence for type approval review.

mappings.notes: Campaign planning and user communication expectations.
Operations and maintenance execution context.

clauses.title: User notification design
clauses.guidance:
Clause 8 also addresses user notification design, establishing requirements for how vehicle owners and operators are informed about software updates before, during, and after installation. User notification is not merely a convenience feature — UN R156 explicitly requires that users be informed about pending updates and that their consent be obtained where applicable. ISO 24089 translates this regulatory requirement into specific engineering guidance for notification system design.

The notification system must deliver clear, accurate information about what the update contains, why it is being offered, what the user needs to do, and how long the process will take. Notifications must distinguish between mandatory updates (safety or security fixes that the user cannot decline) and optional updates (feature enhancements where user consent is required). The notification content must be localized for each market and written in language that a non-technical user can understand without ambiguity.

Pre-update notifications must inform the user of any preconditions such as minimum battery level, vehicle stationary requirements, or expected downtime. The system must provide the user with the ability to schedule the update at a convenient time or to proceed immediately if all preconditions are met. For updates that require the vehicle to be stationary, the notification must clearly communicate that the vehicle will not be driveable during installation and must include an estimated duration.

During-update notifications must communicate progress status and set expectations about remaining time. If the update encounters an error or needs to roll back, the notification must explain the situation and provide clear guidance on what the user should do next. Post-update notifications must confirm successful installation, describe any changes the user will notice, and provide a channel for reporting issues.

The notification architecture must account for multiple communication channels including the vehicle's head unit display, companion mobile applications, email, and SMS. The OTA infrastructure must support notification delivery through these channels with appropriate fallback logic when a primary channel is unavailable. From a security perspective, notification messages must be authenticated to prevent spoofing attacks where a malicious actor sends fake update notifications to trick users into performing unsafe actions. This connects to ISO 21434 threat analysis — social engineering through fake update notifications is a documented attack vector that must be addressed in the update system TARA.

mappings.notes: Directly aligns with user information expectations.

clauses.title: Rollback validation
clauses.guidance:
Clause 9 includes specific requirements for rollback validation — testing and confirming that the rollback mechanism works correctly before an update is approved for deployment. Rollback is the safety net that protects vehicles when an update fails during installation or causes problems after completion. If rollback fails, a vehicle may be left in an inoperable state requiring physical dealer intervention, which is both a safety concern and a significant operational cost.

Rollback validation must cover multiple failure scenarios. These include interrupted downloads (network loss, vehicle power loss), failed installation (signature verification failure, insufficient storage, hardware incompatibility), post-installation functional failure (the update installs successfully but causes incorrect behavior), and user-initiated rollback (where supported). For each scenario, the validation must confirm that the vehicle returns to its pre-update software configuration and that all functionality is restored to its previous state.

The A/B partition architecture, commonly used for OTA updates, requires specific rollback validation. The validation must confirm that partition switching works correctly, that the boot loader selects the correct partition after a failed update, and that the inactive partition's content is preserved during the update attempt. For ECUs that do not support A/B partitioning, the validation must confirm that backup mechanisms (such as storing a pre-update image in reserved flash) operate correctly under all tested failure conditions.

Version management interacts with rollback in complex ways. A rollback does not just restore previous software — it must also restore the vehicle's reported software version to match the pre-update baseline. The fleet management backend must correctly reflect the rollback, updating its records to show the vehicle's actual running version rather than the intended target version. Campaign monitoring must track rollback events as a distinct category from installation failures, because rollbacks indicate that the vehicle is functional but did not receive the intended update.

From a security perspective, rollback must not create a vulnerability window. If a security update is rolled back, the vehicle returns to a state with the known vulnerability. The standard requires that this scenario be addressed in the campaign design — for security-critical updates, the campaign plan must define what happens when rollback occurs, including whether a retry is mandatory or whether the vehicle must be flagged for dealer intervention. This connects to ISO 21434 incident response requirements and to UN R156's expectation that the manufacturer can demonstrate control over the software state of vehicles in the field.

mappings.notes: Supports safe execution and recovery controls.

clauses.title: Verification and validation
clauses.guidance:
Clause 9 of ISO 24089 establishes verification and validation requirements for software updates before they are approved for fleet deployment. This clause ensures that every update package has been rigorously tested to confirm it meets its intended function, does not introduce safety regressions, maintains security properties, and performs acceptably on target hardware. Verification and validation (V&V) is the quality gate between engineering and operations — no update may enter a campaign without passing this gate.

Verification activities confirm that the update package meets its specification. This includes functional testing to confirm that new features or fixes work as intended, regression testing to confirm that existing functionality is preserved, integration testing to confirm compatibility with other ECU software versions in the vehicle, and performance testing to confirm that resource consumption (memory, CPU, storage) remains within acceptable bounds. The verification scope must cover all vehicle variants and hardware revisions targeted by the update.

Validation confirms that the update achieves its intended purpose in a representative vehicle environment. This includes hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing, vehicle-level integration testing, and where appropriate, real-world driving tests. For safety-relevant updates, validation must demonstrate that the update does not violate any safety goals established under ISO 26262. For cybersecurity-relevant updates, validation must confirm that security controls remain effective and that the update does not introduce new vulnerabilities.

Update pipeline security is a V&V concern. The verification process must confirm that the package signing was performed correctly, that the vehicle's verification logic correctly accepts valid signatures and rejects invalid ones, and that the secure boot chain remains intact after installation. Test cases must cover negative scenarios including tampered packages, expired certificates, revoked keys, and replay attacks where a previously valid but superseded package is re-presented.

ISO 21434 Clause 11 requires cybersecurity validation at the vehicle level. ISO 24089 Clause 9 extends this to the update mechanism itself — the V&V process must treat the update channel as a cybersecurity-relevant component and include test cases derived from the TARA for the update system. UN R156 requires evidence that updates have been verified before deployment; work product [UPD-09-01] captures the verification report that constitutes this evidence. The V&V process must also validate the rollback mechanism, confirming that rollback procedures execute correctly and that the vehicle returns to a known-good state — this aspect is detailed further in the rollback validation subclause.

mappings.notes: Evidence for update approval and technical service review.
Cybersecurity validation alignment.

clauses.title: Overview
clauses.guidance:
ISO 26262 is the primary international standard for functional safety of electrical and electronic systems in road vehicles. Published in its second edition in 2018, it provides a structured lifecycle approach to identifying hazards, assessing risks, and implementing safety measures across all phases of automotive product development from concept through decommissioning. The standard defines the Automotive Safety Integrity Level (ASIL) classification scheme, which assigns safety requirements at four levels (ASIL A through ASIL D, with QM for non-safety-relevant functions) based on severity, probability of exposure, and controllability.

The standard spans twelve parts. Part 1 provides vocabulary and overview. Part 2 covers management of functional safety. Part 3 addresses the concept phase including item definition and hazard analysis and risk assessment (HARA). Parts 4 through 6 address system-level, hardware-level, and software-level product development respectively. Part 7 covers production, operation, service, and decommissioning. Part 8 defines supporting processes. Part 9 provides guidance on ASIL-oriented and safety-oriented analyses. Part 10 offers informative guidelines. Part 11 addresses semiconductor-specific considerations. Part 12 covers adaptation for motorcycles.

For cybersecurity practitioners, ISO 26262 is important because cybersecurity attacks can cause safety violations. When a cyberattack compromises an electronic control unit, it may trigger hazardous behavior that was analyzed under HARA. ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 15 requires coordination between TARA and HARA, ensuring that cybersecurity attack scenarios that lead to safety-relevant failures are captured and mitigated. The ASIL assigned to a safety goal under ISO 26262 influences the rigor of cybersecurity countermeasures when the Cybersecurity Assurance Level (CAL) from ISO 21434 intersects with a safety-relevant function.

Organizations implementing both standards must establish a joint safety-cybersecurity management structure. The functional safety manager (Part 2) and cybersecurity manager (ISO 21434 Clause 5) must coordinate on hazard and threat co-analysis, shared requirement traceability, and integrated verification planning. Dependent failure analysis under Part 9 must account for common-cause failures that originate from cybersecurity vulnerabilities, such as a compromised communication bus affecting multiple safety-relevant ECUs. The safety case (Part 2, Clause 6.4.6) should reference relevant cybersecurity evidence to demonstrate that safety arguments remain valid under foreseeable attack conditions.
