clauses.title: Freedom from interference
clauses.guidance:
Freedom from interference is a foundational concept in ISO 26262 that addresses how software or hardware elements of different ASIL levels can coexist on shared resources without higher-ASIL elements being corrupted by lower-ASIL or QM elements. Part 6 Clause 7.4.4 and Part 11 address this concept for software and semiconductor elements respectively. When a system contains mixed-criticality components, the design must demonstrate that a fault in a lower-integrity element cannot propagate to compromise a higher-integrity element.

Freedom from interference covers three primary interference types: interference through shared memory (spatial interference), interference through shared processor time (temporal interference), and interference through shared communication channels. For spatial interference, hardware memory protection units (MPUs) or memory management units (MMUs) partition address spaces so that QM software cannot write to ASIL D data regions. For temporal interference, watchdog mechanisms and execution-time monitoring ensure that a runaway QM task cannot starve an ASIL D task of CPU cycles. For communication interference, message authentication and end-to-end protection prevent corrupted or spurious messages from reaching safety-critical consumers.

The cybersecurity dimension adds a critical layer to freedom-from-interference analysis. A cyberattack that bypasses memory protection, manipulates scheduling priorities, or injects malicious messages on an internal bus can violate all three interference categories simultaneously. ISO 21434 Clause 9 (cybersecurity concept) must identify attack paths that could defeat freedom-from-interference mechanisms. For example, if an attacker gains code execution on a QM-rated infotainment ECU that shares a CAN bus with an ASIL D braking controller, the lack of bus authentication becomes both a cybersecurity vulnerability and a safety interference path.

When performing dependent failure analysis under Part 9, engineers must evaluate whether cybersecurity countermeasures (firewalls, HSMs, SecOC) form part of the freedom-from-interference argument. If they do, those countermeasures inherit the ASIL level of the safety function they protect and must be developed, verified, and maintained accordingly. The CAL assigned under ISO 21434 TARA should be consistent with the ASIL level that depends on the cybersecurity mechanism for interference protection.

ASIL decomposition (Part 9 Clause 5) interacts with freedom from interference because decomposed elements on separate channels must each demonstrate independent freedom from interference. If two ASIL B(D) elements share a resource, the decomposition argument is invalid unless interference is excluded.

clauses.title: Functional safety audit preparation
clauses.guidance:
Functional safety audits and assessments are defined in Part 2 Clause 6.4.7 and Part 8 Clause 13. An audit evaluates whether the processes defined in the safety plan were followed, while an assessment evaluates the functional safety achieved by the product. Both require structured preparation to ensure that evidence is traceable, complete, and accessible to independent assessors.

Audit preparation begins with the functional safety plan (Part 2 Clause 6.4.2), which defines the safety lifecycle activities, responsibilities, milestones, and work products for a specific project. The plan must identify the safety manager, the confirmation measures (review, audit, assessment), and the independence requirements for each confirmation measure based on ASIL level. For ASIL C and D, independent assessment by a party outside the project team is required. For ASIL A and B, internal review may suffice, though many OEMs require independent assessment regardless.

The evidence package for an audit typically includes the item definition, HARA with ASIL assignments, functional safety concept, technical safety concept, system and component safety requirements with bidirectional traceability, verification reports (reviews, analyses, tests), safety analyses (FMEA, FTA, FMEDA), and the safety case. Each work product must be version-controlled and traceable to the safety plan that required it.

From a cybersecurity-safety interaction perspective, audit preparation must demonstrate that the TARA from ISO 21434 was coordinated with the HARA. Assessors will look for evidence that cybersecurity threats that could cause safety violations were identified, that safety mechanisms are not undermined by cybersecurity weaknesses, and that the dependent failure analysis considered cybersecurity-caused common-cause failures. If the safety case relies on cybersecurity controls (such as SecOC for message authentication or HSM-backed secure boot), those controls must have their own evidence of adequate design and verification, traceable to both the cybersecurity assurance case and the functional safety case.

Tool qualification evidence (Part 8 Clause 11) must be prepared for all tools in the safety lifecycle that could introduce or fail to detect errors. This includes static analysis tools, test automation frameworks, and requirement management tools. The tool confidence level (TCL) depends on the tool impact and error detection capability, and maps to qualification method requirements.

clauses.title: HARA traceability model
clauses.guidance:
Hazard analysis and risk assessment (HARA) is defined in Part 3 Clause 7 and produces the foundational safety artifact from which all downstream safety requirements flow. The HARA traceability model defines how hazards, operational situations, safety goals, ASIL classifications, and functional safety requirements maintain bidirectional traceability throughout the product lifecycle.

The HARA process begins with the item definition (Part 3 Clause 5), which describes the item's functionality, interfaces, operating environment, and known similar items. From the item definition, the safety team identifies malfunctioning behaviors at the vehicle level (hazardous events) in the context of operational situations. Each hazardous event is classified for severity (S0-S3), probability of exposure (E0-E4), and controllability (C0-C3). The combination yields an ASIL from QM to D, or for certain combinations, no ASIL assignment (S0 or C0 events).

Safety goals are top-level safety requirements derived from hazardous events. Each safety goal inherits the ASIL of its associated hazardous event. The functional safety concept (Part 3 Clause 8) derives functional safety requirements from safety goals, allocating them to architectural elements. These flow into the technical safety concept (Part 4), system safety requirements, hardware safety requirements (Part 5), and software safety requirements (Part 6), maintaining bidirectional traceability at every level.

The traceability model intersects with cybersecurity through ISO 21434 Clause 15, which mandates coordination between HARA and TARA. When a TARA damage scenario aligns with a HARA hazardous event, the cybersecurity goal from the TARA must be traceable to the corresponding safety goal. If a cyberattack on the steering ECU causes unintended lateral movement (a hazardous event with S3, E4, C2 = ASIL D), the cybersecurity requirement to prevent that attack path must support the ASIL D safety goal.

Traceability tools must maintain these cross-standard links. The requirement management system should support linking TARA attack trees to HARA hazardous events, linking cybersecurity requirements to safety requirements they support, and generating impact analyses when either a safety or cybersecurity requirement changes. Loss of traceability between HARA and TARA artifacts is a finding in both functional safety assessments and cybersecurity assessments and indicates a process gap that could result in unmitigated attack-induced hazards.

mappings.notes: Traceability parallels between HARA and TARA artifact structures.

clauses.title: Part 10: Guidelines
clauses.guidance:
Part 10 of ISO 26262 provides informative guidelines and examples to assist practitioners in applying the normative requirements of Parts 2 through 9. Although Part 10 is informative rather than normative, it is regularly referenced during safety assessments and audits to clarify the intent of normative clauses. It covers topics including ASIL decomposition examples, dependent failure analysis methods, safety element out of context (SEooC) development, integration of safety and cybersecurity analyses, and interpretation of confidence levels for hardware metrics.

Part 10 Clause 9 addresses ASIL decomposition with worked examples. It shows how a safety function allocated to a single ASIL D element can be decomposed across two independent channels, each developed to ASIL B(D), provided that the independence of the channels is demonstrated through analysis of dependent failures. The guidelines make clear that ASIL decomposition reduces the development rigor applied to each channel but does not reduce the overall safety requirement. The sufficiency argument for independence must address common-cause failures, common-mode failures, and cascading failures between the channels.

For cybersecurity-safety interaction, Part 10 provides guidance on how to handle situations where a cybersecurity vulnerability introduces a dependent failure between otherwise independent channels. If both decomposed channels share a communication bus that lacks message authentication, an attacker who gains bus access can manipulate both channels simultaneously, defeating the decomposition argument. The guidelines direct engineers to either add cybersecurity controls that restore independence or re-evaluate the decomposition with the shared vulnerability treated as a dependent failure.

Part 10 also provides guidance on the safety element out of context (SEooC) development model, where a component is developed without a specific vehicle integration context. SEooC developers must make and document safety assumptions that the integrator must validate. For components with cybersecurity relevance (such as microcontrollers with HSM capabilities), the SEooC assumptions should include cybersecurity assumptions — for example, that the integrator will enable secure boot and protect debug interfaces. These assumptions bridge ISO 26262 and ISO 21434 for component suppliers.

The guidelines on probabilistic metric for random hardware failures (PMHF) and single-point fault metric (SPFM) in Part 10 help hardware engineers understand how to account for security-relevant hardware features in their diagnostic coverage calculations. An HSM that provides key storage and cryptographic operations for SecOC may also serve as a safety mechanism if it detects message corruption, and its failure rate must be included in the hardware metric calculations.

clauses.title: Part 11: Semiconductor guidance
clauses.guidance:
Part 11 of ISO 26262 was introduced in the 2018 second edition to address the specific challenges of applying functional safety to semiconductor components, including microcontrollers, sensors, power devices, and application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs). Semiconductors present unique challenges because they contain millions of transistors with failure modes that differ from discrete electronic components, and because they are often developed as safety elements out of context (SEooC) by silicon vendors who do not control the final vehicle integration.

Part 11 provides guidance on semiconductor-specific failure modes, including single-event upsets (SEUs) caused by cosmic radiation, electromigration, time-dependent dielectric breakdown, and systematic failures in digital logic. For hardware safety metrics (SPFM, LFM, PMHF from Part 5), Part 11 explains how to account for the transistor-level failure distributions and how built-in self-test (BIST), error-correcting codes (ECC), and lockstep architectures contribute to diagnostic coverage.

The cybersecurity relevance of Part 11 centers on hardware security modules (HSMs) embedded in automotive microcontrollers. Modern automotive MCUs (such as Infineon AURIX, NXP S32, or Renesas RH850) integrate HSMs that provide secure key storage, cryptographic acceleration, and secure boot capabilities. When an HSM is used to implement safety-relevant functions — for example, authenticating safety-critical CAN messages via SecOC — it becomes a safety mechanism whose failure must be analyzed under Part 5 hardware metrics and Part 11 semiconductor guidance.

If the HSM fails (either through hardware fault or cybersecurity compromise), the safety function that depends on authenticated messages may receive unauthenticated or forged messages. This means the HSM's random hardware failure rate contributes to the PMHF budget of the safety function, and a cybersecurity attack that compromises the HSM's key material is a systematic failure that must be addressed through the cybersecurity development process under ISO 21434.

Part 11 also addresses multi-core processors and the freedom-from-interference challenges they present. When safety-critical and non-safety-critical software share a multi-core processor, the semiconductor must provide hardware mechanisms (core isolation, memory protection, bus arbitration) to prevent interference. These same mechanisms must be evaluated from a cybersecurity perspective because an attacker who compromises a non-safety core could attempt to access safety-critical memory regions or disrupt scheduling on safety-critical cores.

clauses.title: Part 2: Management of functional safety
clauses.guidance:
Part 2 of ISO 26262 defines the organizational and project-level management framework for functional safety. It covers overall safety management (Clause 5), project-dependent safety management (Clause 6), and safety management after start of production (Clause 7). The organizational requirements establish that companies performing safety-relevant work must have a functional safety management system, a safety culture, competence management for safety personnel, a quality management system, and processes for managing safety anomalies.

At the project level, Part 2 requires the creation of a functional safety plan that defines the safety lifecycle tailored to the specific project. The plan must identify the safety manager, define the safety activities for each lifecycle phase, specify the work products to be produced, and define the confirmation measures (reviews, assessments, audits) including the required level of independence. For ASIL D, the assessor must be from an organization independent of the development organization. For ASIL C, a person independent of the project is sufficient. For ASIL A and B, the person performing the activity may also review it, though this is increasingly challenged by assessors.

The safety plan must coordinate with the cybersecurity plan required by ISO 21434 Clause 6. Both plans should reference the same project milestones, define the interaction points between safety and cybersecurity activities, and establish the escalation path when a cybersecurity finding affects safety or vice versa. Key coordination points include the concept phase (where HARA and TARA should be performed concurrently or with explicit cross-references), the system design phase (where safety and cybersecurity requirements must be allocated without conflict), and the verification phase (where combined safety-cybersecurity testing may be required).

Part 2 also requires the development interface agreement (DIA) when safety work is distributed across multiple organizations (such as OEM and tier-1 supplier). The DIA must define responsibilities for each ISO 26262 activity and work product. When cybersecurity is involved, the DIA should also reference ISO 21434 Clause 7 (distributed cybersecurity activities) and define which party performs the TARA, which party implements cybersecurity controls, and how vulnerability monitoring responsibilities are allocated post-production. The ASIL level and CAL level should both be specified in the DIA to ensure consistent rigor across organizational boundaries.

clauses.title: Part 3: Concept phase
clauses.guidance:
Part 3 of ISO 26262 covers the concept phase of the safety lifecycle, which includes item definition (Clause 5), initiation of the safety lifecycle (Clause 6), and hazard analysis and risk assessment (Clause 7). The concept phase produces the foundational safety artifacts from which all subsequent safety work derives, making it the most impactful phase for cybersecurity-safety coordination.

The item definition describes the item (a system or combination of systems that implements a vehicle-level function) including its functionality, interfaces with other items and elements, operating conditions, and the legal and regulatory context. For items with cybersecurity relevance, the item definition should identify communication interfaces that could be exposed to external attackers (V2X, OBD-II, Bluetooth, cellular), processing elements that handle both safety and non-safety functions, and any dependence on external infrastructure (cloud services, backend systems).

HARA (Clause 7) is the central activity of the concept phase. The safety team identifies malfunctioning behaviors at the vehicle level, determines the hazardous events in operational situations, and classifies each hazardous event using the three parameters — severity (S0-S3), probability of exposure (E0-E4), and controllability (C0-C3) — to assign an ASIL. Safety goals are formulated for each ASIL-classified hazardous event, expressing the top-level safety requirement (for example, "unintended acceleration shall not occur" for an ASIL D hazardous event).

The functional safety concept (Clause 8) derives functional safety requirements from the safety goals and allocates them to preliminary architectural elements. Each functional safety requirement inherits the ASIL of the parent safety goal unless ASIL decomposition is applied (Part 9). The concept must define the safe states, fault-tolerant time intervals, and warning/degradation concepts.

ISO 21434 Clause 15 requires that the cybersecurity concept phase (TARA, cybersecurity goals, cybersecurity concept) be coordinated with the safety concept phase. Practically, this means that every TARA damage scenario must be evaluated for safety impact. If an attacker can cause a vehicle-level hazardous event (for example, by sending spoofed torque requests to the EPS ECU), the TARA damage scenario maps to a HARA hazardous event, and the cybersecurity goal must be consistent with the safety goal. The CAL assigned to the cybersecurity goal should reflect the ASIL of the corresponding safety goal to ensure proportionate rigor in cybersecurity treatment.

mappings.notes: Concept phase coordination between safety and cybersecurity analyses.

clauses.title: Part 4: System-level product development
clauses.guidance:
Part 4 of ISO 26262 defines the requirements for system-level product development, spanning the initiation of system design (Clause 5), specification of the technical safety concept (Clause 6), system design (Clause 7), item integration and testing (Clause 8), safety validation (Clause 9), and functional safety assessment (Clause 10). This part bridges the concept phase (Part 3) with the detailed hardware and software development (Parts 5 and 6).

The technical safety concept (TSC) translates functional safety requirements into technical safety requirements allocated to hardware, software, and system architecture elements. Each technical safety requirement must maintain the ASIL traceability from the safety goal and must specify the safety mechanism or design measure that addresses it. Safety mechanisms at the system level include redundancy, monitoring, voter logic, plausibility checks, and safe-state transitions. The TSC must define the fault detection time interval, fault reaction time interval, and fault-tolerant time interval for each safety function.

System architecture design under Part 4 must demonstrate that the architecture supports the required level of fault tolerance and diagnostic coverage. For mixed-criticality systems, the architecture must ensure freedom from interference between elements of different ASIL levels (linking to the Part 6 and Part 11 requirements). The system design must also define the hardware-software interface (HSI), which specifies the assumptions that software makes about hardware behavior and vice versa.

The cybersecurity-safety interaction is most visible at the system level because this is where communication architectures, ECU partitioning, and gateway functions are defined. A system architect must ensure that cybersecurity controls (firewalls, intrusion detection systems, SecOC-authenticated messages, domain isolation) do not conflict with safety timing requirements. For example, adding cryptographic message authentication to a safety-critical CAN message increases the latency and bus load, potentially violating the fault reaction time interval. Conversely, omitting authentication to meet timing creates a cybersecurity gap that could be exploited to cause a safety violation.

Dependent failure analysis at the system level (referenced from Part 9) must consider cybersecurity-caused common-cause failures. If multiple safety-relevant ECUs share an Ethernet backbone and a single compromised gateway can forward malicious traffic to all of them, that gateway becomes a single point of failure from both safety and cybersecurity perspectives. The system architecture must either prevent this scenario through network segmentation and authentication, or the dependent failure analysis must document it as an accepted residual risk with appropriate mitigation.

mappings.notes: System development integrates safety and cybersecurity requirements.

clauses.title: Part 5: Hardware-level product development
clauses.guidance:
Part 5 of ISO 26262 defines requirements for hardware-level product development, covering hardware safety requirements specification (Clause 6), hardware design (Clause 7), evaluation of the hardware architectural metrics (Clause 8), evaluation of safety goal violations due to random hardware failures (Clause 9), and hardware integration and testing (Clause 10). This part introduces quantitative metrics that distinguish it from other parts of the standard.

The three primary hardware safety metrics are the single-point fault metric (SPFM), the latent fault metric (LFM), and the probabilistic metric for random hardware failures (PMHF). ASIL D requires SPFM greater than or equal to 99%, LFM greater than or equal to 90%, and PMHF less than 10^-8 per hour. ASIL B requires SPFM greater than or equal to 90%, LFM greater than or equal to 60%, and PMHF less than 10^-7 per hour. These metrics drive the selection of safety mechanisms such as dual-channel sensing, lockstep cores, and diagnostic monitoring.

Hardware safety analysis methods include FMEA (failure modes and effects analysis), FMEDA (failure modes, effects, and diagnostic analysis), FTA (fault tree analysis), and DFMEA (design FMEA). FMEDA is the workhorse method for computing diagnostic coverage and failure rate distributions. Each hardware component is analyzed for its failure modes, the effect of each failure on the safety function, and whether existing diagnostic mechanisms detect the failure.

From a cybersecurity perspective, hardware components that implement security functions must be analyzed for both random hardware failures and systematic failures. A hardware security module (HSM) that provides message authentication for safety-critical communications is a safety mechanism if the safety argument depends on authenticated messages. Its failure rate (random hardware failures of cryptographic cores, key storage memory, random number generators) must be included in the PMHF calculation. If the HSM fails to authenticate a message, the safety function must have a fallback (for example, transitioning to a safe state after a timeout).

Hardware-level cybersecurity attacks, such as fault injection (voltage glitching, electromagnetic interference, laser fault injection), can cause deterministic hardware failures that bypass diagnostic coverage. These attack methods are addressed in ISO 21434's threat analysis and in semiconductor-specific evaluation standards. When evaluating hardware metrics under Part 5, engineers should consider whether the diagnostic coverage claimed for a safety mechanism remains valid if an attacker can influence the hardware operating conditions. If voltage monitoring detects undervoltage faults but an attacker can inject precisely calibrated glitches below the detection threshold, the diagnostic coverage for that failure mode is reduced.

clauses.title: Part 6: Software-level product development
clauses.guidance:
Part 6 of ISO 26262 defines requirements for software-level product development, covering software safety requirements (Clause 6), software architectural design (Clause 7), software unit design and implementation (Clause 8), software unit testing (Clause 9), software integration and testing (Clause 10), and software verification (Clause 11). The ASIL level determines the rigor of methods required at each stage — for example, ASIL D requires structural coverage at MC/DC level for unit testing, while ASIL A requires only statement coverage.

The software architecture must demonstrate properties including modularity, encapsulation, restricted complexity, and absence of unintended functionality. Part 6 Clause 7.4.4 requires freedom from interference when software of different ASIL levels coexists on the same hardware. Software partitioning, using either operating-system-level mechanisms (memory protection, time partitioning) or separate hardware, must prevent lower-ASIL software from corrupting higher-ASIL software's memory, timing, or communication.

Software safety requirements are derived from the technical safety concept (Part 4) and the hardware-software interface specification. They define the behavior that software must exhibit (including safe behavior under fault conditions), performance constraints, and data integrity requirements. Each requirement inherits the ASIL of the parent technical safety requirement.

The cybersecurity-safety interaction in software development is substantial. ISO 21434 Clause 10 requires secure software development practices, and when safety-relevant software is also cybersecurity-relevant, the development process must satisfy both standards simultaneously. Specific areas of overlap include input validation (safety requires range checking of sensor inputs; cybersecurity requires validation of all external inputs to prevent injection attacks), memory safety (safety requires absence of dangling pointers and buffer overflows; cybersecurity requires the same to prevent exploitation), and code review (safety requires review against coding guidelines such as MISRA C; cybersecurity requires review for common weakness enumerations such as CWE entries for automotive software).

ASIL decomposition at the software level allows two independent software paths, each developed to a lower ASIL, to jointly achieve a higher ASIL safety goal. The independence argument must demonstrate that the two paths do not share common code, data, or failure modes. From a cybersecurity perspective, independence also requires that an attacker who compromises one path cannot use that access to compromise the other. If both paths use the same cryptographic library for secure communication, a vulnerability in that library is a common-mode failure that invalidates the decomposition. The dependent failure analysis must explicitly consider software supply chain risks and shared components as potential common causes.

mappings.notes: Secure software and safe software engineering must be co-managed.

clauses.title: Part 7: Production, operation, service and decommissioning
clauses.guidance:
Part 7 of ISO 26262 defines safety requirements for production (Clause 5), operation, service, and decommissioning (Clause 6). Unlike Parts 3 through 6 which focus on development, Part 7 extends safety obligations across the entire product lifetime, from manufacturing through end-of-life. This temporal scope aligns closely with ISO 21434's post-development cybersecurity requirements (Clauses 12 and 13).

Production safety requirements ensure that manufacturing processes do not introduce faults that compromise safety. This includes end-of-line testing procedures that verify safety-critical functions (such as airbag deployment circuits, braking system calibration, and sensor alignment), configuration management to prevent wrong software variants from being flashed to ECUs, and process controls for safety-critical assembly steps. Each production step that could affect a safety function must be defined in the production control plan with pass/fail criteria.

For cybersecurity-safety interaction during production, the manufacturing environment must ensure that cryptographic keys, certificates, and secure boot configurations are provisioned correctly. A mis-provisioned HSM (wrong key, missing certificate) can prevent SecOC from authenticating safety-critical messages, creating both a cybersecurity failure and a safety failure. The production control plan should include verification that cryptographic provisioning was successful, either through automated end-of-line tests or through secure manufacturing protocols that log provisioning status.

Operational safety requirements address the vehicle's behavior in the field. Part 7 requires that the user be informed of maintenance schedules that affect safety (for example, sensor recalibration intervals), that diagnostic functions support field monitoring, and that modification or repair of safety-critical systems follows defined procedures. The interaction with cybersecurity is significant because over-the-air (OTA) software updates can modify safety-critical software. ISO 24089 and UNECE R156 govern the software update management system, but from a Part 7 perspective, any update to safety-relevant software must maintain the safety qualification of the software. This means that the update process must verify the updated software against the safety requirements, that rollback capability exists if the update fails, and that the ASIL classification of the update process itself is appropriate.

Decommissioning requirements ensure that safety-relevant data and configurations are handled appropriately when a vehicle reaches end of life. For cybersecurity, this includes secure erasure of cryptographic keys and personal data. For safety, it includes ensuring that decommissioned ECUs cannot be reused in safety-critical applications without requalification.

mappings.notes: Operational safety and operational cybersecurity coordination.

clauses.title: Part 8: Supporting processes
clauses.guidance:
Part 8 of ISO 26262 defines supporting processes that apply across all lifecycle phases, including interfaces within distributed developments (Clause 5), specification and management of safety requirements (Clause 6), configuration management (Clause 7), change management (Clause 8), verification (Clause 9), documentation (Clause 10), tool confidence (Clause 11), qualification of software components (Clause 12), and evaluation of hardware elements (Clause 13).

Configuration management (Clause 7) requires that all safety-related work products are version-controlled, that the configuration of the item at any point in time can be reconstructed, and that the relationship between safety requirements, design artifacts, test results, and released versions is traceable. For cybersecurity-safety integration, the configuration management system must also track cybersecurity artifacts (TARA, cybersecurity requirements, penetration test results) and maintain cross-references between safety and cybersecurity work products that share dependencies.

Change management (Clause 8) defines how changes to safety-relevant elements are assessed for their safety impact. Every change request must be evaluated against the existing safety analysis — if the change could introduce new hazards or invalidate existing safety arguments, the HARA and downstream requirements must be updated. The cybersecurity-safety interaction here is that a cybersecurity patch (for example, updating a cryptographic library to address a vulnerability) is a change to a safety-relevant software element that must undergo safety change impact analysis. If the patch modifies timing behavior, memory usage, or interface behavior, it could affect the software safety verification results.

Tool confidence (Clause 11) requires that software tools used in the safety lifecycle are qualified to a tool confidence level (TCL) commensurate with the ASIL level of the safety function they support. A TCL is determined by the tool impact (TI) — whether the tool can introduce or fail to detect errors — and the tool error detection capability (TD) — whether downstream activities would catch tool errors. TCL 3 (TI1, TD1) requires the most rigorous qualification, including validation of the tool's output against independently computed results. For tools that serve both safety and cybersecurity purposes (such as static analysis tools that check both MISRA compliance and CWE coverage), a single qualification that covers both use cases is efficient and recommended.

Qualification of software components (Clause 12) addresses how to use software not developed under ISO 26262 (such as COTS operating systems, open-source libraries, or legacy code) in safety-relevant applications. The qualification process includes analysis of the component's development process, safety-relevant requirements, and testing. From a cybersecurity perspective, qualified software components must also be assessed for known vulnerabilities (CVEs) and must be included in the software bill of materials (SBOM) for ongoing monitoring under ISO 21434 Clause 13.

clauses.title: Part 9: ASIL-oriented and safety-oriented analyses
clauses.guidance:
Part 9 of ISO 26262 defines ASIL-oriented and safety-oriented analyses, with its primary contribution being the rules and methods for ASIL decomposition (Clause 5), dependent failure analysis (Clause 7), and safety analyses (Clause 8). ASIL decomposition is a design strategy that allows a safety requirement with a given ASIL to be allocated to redundant elements, each developed to a lower ASIL, provided that the elements are sufficiently independent.

The decomposition rules are: ASIL D can decompose to ASIL D(D) and QM(D), ASIL C(D) and ASIL A(D), or ASIL B(D) and ASIL B(D). ASIL C can decompose to ASIL C(C) and QM(C), ASIL B(C) and ASIL A(C). ASIL B can decompose to ASIL B(B) and QM(B), or ASIL A(B) and ASIL A(B). The notation ASIL X(Y) means "developed to ASIL X requirements, with ASIL Y being the original requirement before decomposition." Each decomposition requires a rigorous demonstration that the decomposed elements are sufficiently independent.

Dependent failure analysis (DFA) is the method used to demonstrate this independence. DFA evaluates three categories of dependent failures: common-cause failures (same root cause affecting both channels, such as a shared power supply), common-mode failures (same failure mode occurring independently in both channels due to similar design weaknesses), and cascading failures (failure of one channel causing failure of the other). Each dependent failure must be either eliminated by design, detected and mitigated by a safety mechanism, or demonstrated to have sufficiently low probability.

The cybersecurity dimension adds a fourth category to dependent failure analysis: adversarial common-cause failures. An attacker who identifies a vulnerability present in both redundant channels (for example, a shared software library with a buffer overflow, or a shared communication protocol without authentication) can exploit both channels simultaneously with a single attack method. Unlike random hardware failures, cybersecurity attacks are intentional and systematic, meaning probability-based arguments are insufficient. The DFA must demonstrate that cybersecurity controls make the exploitation of both channels simultaneously infeasible, not merely improbable.

When the CAL from ISO 21434 TARA intersects with the ASIL from HARA for the same function, the dependent failure analysis must address both random hardware failures (Part 5 metrics) and deliberate attacks (cybersecurity threat scenarios). If ASIL decomposition relies on network segmentation for channel independence, a cybersecurity assessment must confirm that the segmentation resists foreseeable attack methods, not just accidental faults. The bridge between ASIL and CAL is strongest in Part 9's dependent failure analysis, where the two frameworks share the most analytical common ground.

clauses.title: Safety case integration
clauses.guidance:
The safety case is the structured argument, supported by evidence, that demonstrates a product achieves functional safety for its intended use. ISO 26262 Part 2 Clause 6.4.6 requires the safety case to be developed throughout the lifecycle and finalized before start of production. The safety case collects and structures all safety work products — HARA, safety concepts, requirements, architecture descriptions, analyses, verification reports, and assessment findings — into a coherent argument that the safety goals are met.

A typical safety case structure follows the Goal Structuring Notation (GSN) or a Claims-Arguments-Evidence (CAE) model. The top-level claim is that the item is acceptably safe for its intended use. This decomposes into sub-claims for each safety goal, each supported by evidence that the safety requirements derived from that goal have been correctly specified, implemented, verified, and validated. Counterarguments and assumptions are explicitly documented. The safety case is a living document that must be maintained throughout the product's operational lifetime as changes, field data, and new hazard information emerge.

For cybersecurity-safety integration, ISO 21434 Clause 6.4.8 defines a separate cybersecurity assurance case. The two cases must be integrated where cybersecurity controls support safety arguments. Specific integration points include shared evidence (for example, a penetration test report that demonstrates a safety-critical ECU resists network-based attacks supports both the cybersecurity assurance case and the freedom-from-interference argument in the safety case), dependency declarations (where the safety case explicitly depends on a cybersecurity control, the cybersecurity assurance case must provide the evidence, and the dependency must be documented so that changes to the cybersecurity control trigger a review of the safety argument), and residual risk coordination (the safety case accepts residual risk based on ASIL classification, and the cybersecurity case accepts residual risk based on CAL classification; where both apply to the same function, the residual risk acceptance must be consistent).

Assessors evaluating the safety case will examine whether cybersecurity threats that could cause safety violations are addressed. A safety case that claims freedom from interference based on network segmentation but provides no evidence that the segmentation resists cyber attacks has a gap. The integration of safety and cybersecurity cases is an area of increasing regulatory focus, particularly under UNECE R155 which requires ongoing cybersecurity management, and any cybersecurity finding that affects safety must be reflected in the safety case update process.

mappings.notes: Safety case and cybersecurity case should be aligned for assurance.

clauses.title: Safety mechanism allocation
clauses.guidance:
Safety mechanism allocation is the process of assigning specific safety mechanisms to architectural elements to detect, control, or mitigate faults that could lead to violation of safety goals. Part 4 Clause 6 defines the technical safety concept where safety mechanisms are allocated to the system architecture, and Parts 5 and 6 refine the allocation to hardware and software levels respectively.

Safety mechanisms fall into three categories: fault prevention mechanisms that eliminate fault occurrence by design (such as using ECC memory to prevent single-bit errors), fault detection mechanisms that identify when a fault has occurred (such as watchdog timers, plausibility checks, and cross-comparison of redundant channels), and fault reaction mechanisms that transition the system to a safe state when a fault is detected (such as disabling an actuator, activating a backup system, or alerting the driver). The effectiveness of a safety mechanism is quantified as its diagnostic coverage — the percentage of safety-relevant faults it can detect — which directly feeds into the SPFM and LFM calculations of Part 5.

When allocating safety mechanisms, the architect must ensure that each safety goal is addressed by a chain of detection-reaction mechanisms that can execute within the fault-tolerant time interval (FTTI). The FTTI is the time from fault occurrence to potential hazardous event, minus the time needed for the driver to regain control. Each safety mechanism in the chain has a fault detection time interval and a fault reaction time interval that must fit within the FTTI budget.

The cybersecurity-safety interaction in safety mechanism allocation is bidirectional. First, cybersecurity controls may serve as safety mechanisms. If message authentication (SecOC) prevents spoofed messages from reaching a safety-critical actuator, that authentication is a safety mechanism with an ASIL inheritance from the safety function it protects. It must be developed, verified, and maintained to the ASIL level, and its failure must be included in hardware metric calculations. Second, safety mechanisms may have cybersecurity implications. A diagnostic function that reads safety-critical data from an ECU via UDS (ISO 14229) could be exploited by an attacker to extract calibration data or inject malicious diagnostic commands. The safety mechanism allocation must be reviewed from a TARA perspective to ensure that safety diagnostic channels do not create cybersecurity attack surfaces.

The relationship between ASIL and CAL affects the rigor of safety mechanism development. An ASIL D safety function protected by a cybersecurity control that was developed under CAL 2 has a potential gap — the cybersecurity control may not have been verified with sufficient rigor to support an ASIL D argument. The allocation process must flag these gaps and either upgrade the cybersecurity development rigor or add additional safety mechanisms that do not depend on cybersecurity.

clauses.title: SAE J3061 Overview
clauses.guidance: SAE J3061 (2016) was the first automotive cybersecurity standard, predating ISO 21434. Key concepts: (1) Cybersecurity lifecycle - concept, development, production, operation, decommissioning; (2) Feature-based development - integrates with existing automotive processes; (3) Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) - originated many concepts later refined in ISO 21434; (4) Guiding principles - defense in depth, least privilege, fail-safe. While superseded by ISO 21434 for new programs, J3061 is still referenced in legacy contracts and provides good introductory material. Many J3061 concepts map directly to ISO 21434.

clauses.title: Supporting Processes
clauses.guidance: Organizational processes supporting cybersecurity. Includes: (1) Cybersecurity governance - organizational structure, responsibilities; (2) Cybersecurity culture - awareness, training; (3) Tool qualification - security of development tools; (4) Supplier management - cybersecurity requirements for suppliers. Similar to ISO 21434 Clauses 5 and 7. J3061 was less prescriptive about organizational requirements than ISO 21434.

clauses.title: Governance and process assurance
clauses.guidance: Defines governance, oversight, and process assurance mechanisms across the cybersecurity lifecycle.

mappings.notes: Governance baseline aligns with organizational cybersecurity management.

clauses.title: Metrics and continuous improvement
clauses.guidance: Defines cybersecurity metric collection and continuous improvement loops for engineering effectiveness.

clauses.title: Guiding Principles
clauses.guidance: Foundational security principles for vehicle cybersecurity. Principles: (1) Defense in depth - multiple layers of security controls; (2) Least privilege - minimal access rights for functions; (3) Separation of domains - isolate critical from non-critical systems; (4) Fail-safe design - secure defaults, graceful degradation; (5) No security by obscurity - don't rely on hidden designs; (6) Simplicity - reduce attack surface through simple designs. These principles remain valid and should guide all automotive security architecture decisions.

clauses.title: Concept Phase
clauses.guidance: Initiates cybersecurity for a vehicle feature. Activities: (1) Item definition - scope, boundaries, interfaces; (2) Cybersecurity goals - high-level security objectives; (3) Preliminary TARA - initial threat identification; (4) Cybersecurity concept - high-level security architecture. Similar to ISO 21434 Clause 9 but with different terminology. J3061 uses 'feature' where ISO 21434 uses 'item'. The outputs feed into detailed design phase.

clauses.title: Development Phase
clauses.guidance: Detailed cybersecurity engineering during product development. Key activities: (1) System-level TARA - detailed threat analysis; (2) Security requirements - derived from TARA and cybersecurity goals; (3) Security architecture - detailed security design; (4) Security implementation - coding standards, secure coding; (5) Security testing - verification and validation. J3061 introduced the concept of security testing levels analogous to ISO 26262 ASIL decomposition.

clauses.title: Production Phase
clauses.guidance: Cybersecurity during manufacturing. Focus areas: (1) Secure manufacturing - protect keys, certificates, software; (2) Secure flashing - authenticated software installation; (3) Production security controls - physical and logical access; (4) End-of-line testing - verify security features work. Similar to ISO 21434 Clause 12 but less detailed. Key concern: protecting cryptographic material during production.

clauses.title: Operation and Maintenance Phase
clauses.guidance: Cybersecurity for vehicles in the field. Key activities: (1) Vulnerability monitoring - track new vulnerabilities; (2) Incident response - detect and respond to attacks; (3) Security updates - patch vulnerabilities; (4) Field monitoring - collect security telemetry. J3061 was early in recognizing the need for post-production security monitoring. Maps to ISO 21434 Clause 8 (continual activities) and Clause 13 (operations).

clauses.title: TARA Method Examples
clauses.guidance: Provides example TARA methods. Methods described: (1) EVITA - uses attack trees and attack potential calculation; (2) HEAVENS - threat modeling with security levels; (3) TVRA - ETSI method adapted for vehicles; (4) Attack trees - hierarchical threat decomposition; (5) Misuse cases - extend use cases with attacker perspective. These methods influenced ISO 21434 Annex H. Choose a method that fits your organization's risk assessment experience.

mappings.notes: Cybersecurity case structure similarity.

mappings.notes: Operations and maintenance phase response alignment.
