clauses.title: TISAX Overview and Assessment Levels
clauses.guidance:
TISAX (Trusted Information Security Assessment Exchange) is the automotive industry's standardized information security assessment mechanism, governed by the ENX Association and built on the VDA Information Security Assessment (ISA) catalog. It exists because automotive OEMs — particularly VW Group, BMW, Mercedes-Benz, and their joint ventures — require every supplier handling confidential data or prototypes to demonstrate information security maturity through a recognized, repeatable process rather than ad-hoc questionnaires.

TISAX defines three assessment levels. AL1 (normal protection) permits self-assessment only and is rarely accepted by OEMs. AL2 (high protection) requires a remote assessment by an accredited audit provider and covers the "Info high" scope label for confidential business information. AL3 (very high protection) demands an on-site audit and applies to "Info very high" (strictly confidential data), "Proto" (prototype protection), and "Vehicle" (test vehicle handling) scope labels. Most supplier engagements require AL2 or AL3.

Implementation begins with scoping. Identify which sites, departments, and data flows fall within the assessment boundary. Register on the ENX portal, select an accredited audit provider (such as TUV, DEKRA, or Bureau Veritas), and define scope labels. Allow six to nine months from registration to assessment readiness. The VDA ISA catalog structures requirements across information security policies, organization, human resources, asset management, access control, cryptography, physical security, operations security, communications security, supplier relationships, incident management, business continuity, compliance, and — for AL3 — prototype protection.

TISAX maps closely to ISO 27001:2022. An existing ISO 27001 certification covers roughly 70-80 percent of TISAX requirements, but gaps typically appear in prototype protection, vehicle handling, and automotive-specific supplier flow-down obligations. ISO 21434 (cybersecurity engineering) complements TISAX by addressing product-level cybersecurity, while TISAX focuses on organizational information security. Suppliers working on safety-relevant ECUs or connected vehicle components often need both.

Common audit findings include incomplete risk assessments that fail to address automotive-specific threats, missing or outdated topic-specific policies (especially for mobile devices and remote work), insufficient supplier security flow-down documentation, and weak prototype protection measures at AL3. Auditors also frequently cite inadequate business continuity testing and poor evidence of management review.

Evidence needed for assessment includes the ISMS documentation package, a current risk assessment with treatment plan, the Statement of Applicability mapping VDA ISA controls, policy documents with version control and approval records, training records, supplier security assessments, incident response logs, business continuity test results, and internal audit reports. For prototype protection scopes, additional evidence covers physical security measures, NDA registers, photo and device policies, and camouflage documentation. Results are valid for three years and shared with OEM customers through the ENX portal, eliminating the need for repeated assessments by different customers.

clauses.title: Information Security Policies
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 1 requires a documented, management-approved information security policy framework that establishes the organization's commitment to protecting information assets and sets the direction for all subsequent controls. This is the foundation the TISAX assessor evaluates first, because without clear policy authority the remaining controls lack governance backing.

The top-level information security policy must state the organization's objectives for information security, commit to satisfying applicable requirements (legal, regulatory, contractual, and OEM-specific), and commit to continual improvement of the ISMS. It must be signed by top management — typically the managing director or board member responsible for IT and security. Assessors verify that the signature is current and that the policy has been reviewed within the last twelve months.

Below the top-level policy, topic-specific policies must exist for at least: access control, cryptographic controls, acceptable use of assets, mobile devices and teleworking, supplier relationships, data classification, backup, and information transfer. For organizations seeking prototype protection labels, a dedicated prototype protection policy is required. Each policy must identify its owner, review cycle, and distribution scope.

Implementation in an automotive supplier context means aligning policy language with OEM expectations. BMW's TISAX scope requires explicit reference to handling of "vertraulich" (confidential) and "streng vertraulich" (strictly confidential) classifications. VW Group expects supplier policies to reference VDA ISA control objectives by number. Integrate your ISO 21434 cybersecurity policy as a referenced sub-policy — this demonstrates that product cybersecurity governance and organizational information security governance are coordinated.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping is direct: Clause 5.2 (Information security policy) and Annex A control A.5.1 (Policies for information security). ISO 21434 Clause 5 (Organizational cybersecurity management) overlaps where cybersecurity policy intersects with information security policy. An integrated policy document satisfies both standards if the scope statement covers both organizational IT and product development environments.

Common audit findings include policies that have not been reviewed since initial certification, policies that lack version control or approval signatures, topic-specific policies that exist as templates but have not been tailored to the organization's actual operations, and policies that are not communicated to employees beyond a signature on their employment contract. Assessors expect evidence that employees can locate and understand the policies that affect their daily work.

Evidence needed includes the approved information security policy with signature and date, all topic-specific policies with version history, records of management review meetings where policies were discussed, communication records (email distribution, intranet publication, training session attendance), and a policy review schedule showing planned and actual review dates. For AL3 assessments, assessors may interview staff to verify policy awareness.

clauses.title: Supplier Relationships
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 10 governs security in supplier and third-party relationships, a high-priority area in the automotive supply chain where data and components flow through multiple tiers. TISAX assessors pay close attention to this chapter because a supplier's information security is only as strong as its weakest sub-supplier.

The supplier information security policy must define the security requirements that apply to any third party accessing, processing, storing, or transmitting the organization's information. These requirements must be proportional to the classification of the data involved. For automotive suppliers, this means flowing down OEM-specific classification and handling requirements to sub-suppliers. If your customer classifies data as "strictly confidential," your sub-supplier handling that data must meet equivalent protection standards.

Supplier agreements must contain explicit information security clauses covering data classification handling, access control, incident notification, audit rights, return and destruction of data at contract end, and compliance with applicable regulations (GDPR, industry-specific requirements). TISAX assessors check whether contracts contain these clauses — a generic "comply with applicable laws" clause is insufficient. For suppliers with significant outsourcing (IT managed services, cloud hosting, engineering services), the security requirements must be formally defined before the service begins and monitored throughout the relationship.

Ongoing supplier monitoring and review must be performed at planned intervals. This includes verifying supplier security posture through questionnaires, audit reports, certifications (ISO 27001, TISAX), or on-site assessments for critical suppliers. The organization must maintain an approved supplier list that records the security assessment status of each supplier. Changes in supplier arrangements (new sub-contracting, changes in service scope, personnel changes) must trigger a security reassessment.

The ICT supply chain requires specific attention — security of hardware and software procurement, including integrity verification of delivered components and secure delivery channels. For automotive suppliers procuring embedded software or IP cores, this means verifying the provenance and integrity of delivered code.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.5.19 (Information security in supplier relationships), A.5.20 (Addressing information security within supplier agreements), A.5.21 (Managing information security in the ICT supply chain), A.5.22 (Monitoring, review and change management of supplier services), and A.5.23 (Information security for use of cloud services). ISO 21434 Clause 7 (Distributed cybersecurity activities) requires formal cybersecurity interface agreements with suppliers involved in vehicle cybersecurity, which should be coordinated with TISAX supplier requirements.

Common audit findings include security requirements not defined before supplier onboarding, contracts missing specific security clauses, no documented supplier security assessments beyond collecting an ISO 27001 certificate, critical suppliers not re-assessed when their service scope changed, sub-supplier chains not identified or assessed, and cloud service providers used without formal risk assessment or contractual security requirements.

Evidence needed includes the supplier security policy, contract templates showing security clauses, the approved supplier register with assessment dates and status, supplier assessment questionnaires and results, sub-supplier identification and flow-down documentation, cloud service risk assessments, and records of periodic supplier reviews.

clauses.title: Incident Management
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 11 addresses information security incident management — the capability to detect, report, assess, respond to, and learn from security incidents. In the automotive sector, incident management must account for the cascading impact of security breaches across the supply chain, including potential effects on vehicle safety and OEM production schedules.

The organization must have a documented incident management process that defines incident categories, severity levels, escalation paths, roles and responsibilities, and communication procedures. TISAX assessors expect a distinction between security events (observable occurrences) and security incidents (events that actually compromise confidentiality, integrity, or availability). The process must define clear criteria for when an event becomes an incident and who makes that determination.

Reporting obligations are critical in the automotive context. Employees must know how to report suspected security incidents — a defined reporting channel (hotline, email, ticketing system) must be in place and communicated. Reporting must be encouraged through a no-blame culture for good-faith reports. For OEM-related incidents, the process must include notification procedures aligned with contractual obligations — most OEM contracts require notification within 24-72 hours of confirming an incident affecting their data. GDPR data breach notification (72 hours to the supervisory authority) may also apply.

Incident response must follow a structured methodology: identification, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident review. Containment actions must be predefined for common scenarios (ransomware, data exfiltration, unauthorized access, lost device). The incident response team must be identified, trained, and available. For automotive suppliers with 24/7 production operations, out-of-hours incident response capability is expected.

Post-incident review (lessons learned) must be performed for all significant incidents. The review must identify root causes, assess whether existing controls failed, and generate improvement actions that are tracked to completion. TISAX assessors look for evidence that lessons learned from incidents have actually driven changes to controls, procedures, or training.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.5.24 (Information security incident management planning and preparation), A.5.25 (Assessment and decision on information security events), A.5.26 (Response to information security incidents), A.5.27 (Learning from information security incidents), and A.5.28 (Collection of evidence). ISO 21434 Clause 13 (Cybersecurity incident response) addresses product-level incident response, including vulnerability disclosure and coordination with OEMs and authorities — the TISAX organizational incident process should interface with the ISO 21434 product incident process.

Common audit findings include incident management procedures that exist on paper but have never been tested through exercises or tabletop simulations, no defined severity classification, missing escalation criteria for OEM notification, incident logs that lack sufficient detail for forensic analysis, post-incident reviews that are not performed or are performed but do not generate tracked improvement actions, and no evidence of periodic incident response training or drills.

Evidence needed includes the incident management procedure, incident report forms or ticketing system configuration, incident log with response records, evidence of employee awareness of reporting procedures, post-incident review reports with improvement actions, training and drill records, communication templates for OEM and regulatory notification, and evidence chain-of-custody procedures for forensic evidence.

clauses.title: Business Continuity
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 12 addresses business continuity management as it relates to information security, requiring organizations to maintain the availability of information processing facilities and business processes during and after disruptive events. For automotive suppliers operating in just-in-time and just-in-sequence production environments, even brief disruptions can halt OEM assembly lines and trigger contractual penalties.

The organization must integrate information security requirements into its business continuity management system. This begins with a business impact analysis (BIA) that identifies critical business processes, their dependencies on information systems, and the maximum tolerable downtime for each. Recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) must be defined for all critical systems. In automotive contexts, production-supporting systems (MES, ERP, EDI) typically require RTOs measured in hours, while engineering systems may tolerate longer recovery periods.

Business continuity plans must document the steps for maintaining or restoring information processing capabilities after a disruption. Plans must cover scenarios relevant to the organization's risk profile: ransomware attacks, data center failures, network outages, natural disasters, and supplier failures. Each plan must identify the responsible team, activation criteria, communication procedures (internal and external, including OEM notification), and recovery steps.

Redundancy and resilience measures must be implemented proportional to system criticality. This includes redundant infrastructure (failover servers, replicated databases, diverse network paths), backup data centers or cloud-based disaster recovery, and alternative processing arrangements. For IT systems supporting production, automatic failover or rapid manual switchover must be documented and tested.

Testing and exercising is where TISAX assessors focus most heavily. Business continuity plans must be tested at planned intervals — annually at minimum. Tests must include tabletop exercises, technical recovery tests (restoring from backup to alternate infrastructure), and, where practical, full failover tests. Test results must be documented, including identified gaps and corrective actions. Plans must be updated based on test findings and changes to the organization's systems and processes.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.5.29 (Information security during disruption) and A.5.30 (ICT readiness for business continuity), plus Clause 8.1 (Operational planning and control) for the integration of continuity requirements. ISO 21434 does not directly address business continuity but assumes it as a prerequisite for sustained cybersecurity operations, particularly for post-production vulnerability monitoring and incident response.

Common audit findings include business impact analyses that have not been updated to reflect current system dependencies, business continuity plans that cover natural disasters but not cyber incidents (ransomware, data destruction), plans that have never been tested beyond a document review, backup restoration tests that demonstrate technical recovery but not business process recovery, missing or unrealistic RTO/RPO definitions, and no consideration of supply chain disruption (critical IT service provider failure).

Evidence needed includes the business impact analysis, business continuity plans covering information security scenarios, redundancy and failover architecture documentation, test plans and test result reports, corrective actions from test findings, communication plans for disruption notification (including OEM contacts), and evidence of plan maintenance and update cycles.

clauses.title: Compliance
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 13 addresses compliance with legal, regulatory, contractual, and organizational requirements related to information security. For automotive suppliers operating across multiple jurisdictions and serving OEMs with diverse contractual requirements, compliance management is operationally demanding and a frequent source of TISAX findings.

The organization must identify all applicable legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements related to information security and maintain a register of these obligations. This includes data protection regulations (GDPR in the EU, state-level laws in the US), intellectual property protections, export control regulations (dual-use goods and technology), sector-specific regulations (automotive type-approval, product liability), and contractual requirements from OEM customers. The register must be reviewed periodically and updated when new requirements are identified.

Data protection and privacy require specific attention. Under GDPR, the organization must have a documented basis for processing personal data, a data protection officer (DPO) where required, data processing agreements with processors, privacy impact assessments for high-risk processing, and breach notification procedures. TISAX assessors verify that data protection is integrated into the ISMS rather than operating as a separate compliance function. Records of processing activities must be maintained and available for inspection.

Intellectual property protection is critical in the automotive context. OEM designs, specifications, and production processes constitute trade secrets that must be protected through technical controls (access restrictions, DRM, watermarking), legal controls (NDAs, license agreements), and organizational controls (classification, need-to-know access). The organization must also ensure that its own IP is protected and that it respects third-party IP rights in its products and processes.

Cryptographic controls are subject to export regulations in many jurisdictions. Organizations must verify that their use and transfer of cryptographic technology complies with applicable export control regulations, particularly when exchanging encrypted data or security solutions with entities outside the EU or across sanctioned jurisdictions.

Internal compliance verification through independent review (internal audit) must be performed at planned intervals. The ISMS audit program must cover all TISAX control areas over a defined cycle and must be performed by auditors independent of the areas being reviewed. Audit findings must be documented, corrective actions assigned, and closure verified.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.5.31 (Legal, statutory, regulatory and contractual requirements), A.5.32 (Intellectual property rights), A.5.33 (Protection of records), A.5.34 (Privacy and protection of PII), A.5.35 (Independent review of information security), A.5.36 (Compliance with policies, rules and standards), and A.8.24 (Use of cryptography — export aspects). ISO 21434 Clause 5.2 requires identification of applicable cybersecurity regulations, which overlaps with TISAX compliance identification for product-related requirements.

Common audit findings include incomplete compliance registers that miss contractual OEM requirements, data protection documentation that is not integrated with the ISMS, missing or outdated data processing agreements with sub-processors, internal audit programs that do not cover all TISAX control areas, internal auditors who are not independent of the areas they audit, and audit findings that remain open beyond their agreed remediation dates.

Evidence needed includes the compliance obligations register, data protection documentation (processing records, DPIAs, DPO appointment), IP protection procedures, export control compliance evidence, the internal audit program and schedule, internal audit reports with findings and corrective actions, management review records covering compliance status, and evidence of periodic compliance register reviews.

clauses.title: Identity and Access Governance
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 14 (referenced in ISA 6.0 extended controls) addresses identity and access governance at a strategic level, building on the tactical access control requirements of Chapter 5. This chapter focuses on the governance framework that ensures access rights across all systems remain aligned with business needs, organizational roles, and regulatory requirements over time.

Identity governance requires a centralized identity management approach — whether through a dedicated IAM (Identity and Access Management) platform or through documented processes that achieve equivalent control. Every digital identity (employee, contractor, service account, machine identity) must have a defined lifecycle: creation tied to a verified business need, periodic recertification, and prompt deactivation when the need ends. For automotive suppliers, this lifecycle must account for project-based access: engineers are assigned to OEM programs with specific data classification, and their access rights must change when they move between programs.

Access governance mandates periodic access certification campaigns where system owners and managers review and confirm (or revoke) access rights for all users on their systems. These campaigns must occur at defined intervals — at least annually, with more frequent reviews for high-privilege accounts and systems processing strictly confidential data. The organization must define clear ownership for every system and dataset, because without ownership, no one can authorize or review access.

Role-based access control (RBAC) governance requires that roles are defined, documented, and periodically reviewed to prevent role proliferation and privilege accumulation. Role definitions must reflect actual job functions, and exceptions (users who need access beyond their role) must be documented with business justification and time-limited where possible. Segregation of duties must be enforced through role design — conflicting permissions (such as creating and approving purchase orders, or developing and deploying to production) must not exist within a single role.

Privileged identity management (PIM) requires elevated controls for administrative accounts: just-in-time access elevation, session recording, separate credentials from daily-use accounts, and break-glass procedures for emergency access with post-use review. Service accounts and API credentials must be inventoried, owned, and rotated on a defined schedule.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.5.15 through A.5.18 (Access control policy, identity management, authentication, access rights) and A.8.2 through A.8.5 (Privileged access, information access restriction, source code access, secure authentication). ISO 21434 Clause 5.4.6 addresses access control for cybersecurity-related tools and repositories, which must be included in the identity governance scope.

Common audit findings include identity lifecycle processes that do not cover contractor and temporary worker accounts, access certification campaigns that are performed but generate no revocations (indicating rubber-stamping), role definitions that have not been reviewed since initial creation, service accounts with static credentials that have never been rotated, and privileged access without session recording or time-limited elevation.

Evidence needed includes the identity governance policy, IAM system configuration or process documentation, access certification campaign records with reviewer actions, role definitions and review records, privileged access management procedures and session logs, service account inventory with rotation records, and segregation of duties matrix with conflict resolution documentation.

mappings.notes: Identity controls contribute to organizational cybersecurity posture.

clauses.title: Cryptographic Control Expectations
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 15 (part of ISA 6.0 extended controls) defines expectations for cryptographic controls at a governance level, extending the operational requirements of Chapter 6 with strategic oversight, lifecycle management, and supply chain considerations for cryptographic technology.

The cryptographic control framework must establish governance over the selection, deployment, management, and retirement of cryptographic mechanisms across the organization. This goes beyond listing approved algorithms — it requires a documented decision-making process for selecting cryptographic solutions, assigning ownership of cryptographic assets (keys, certificates, tokens), and planning for cryptographic agility (the ability to transition to new algorithms when current ones become deprecated or compromised).

Cryptographic standards management requires the organization to maintain an approved cryptographic standards document that specifies minimum algorithm strengths, approved cipher suites for each use case (TLS, disk encryption, email, code signing, database encryption), and prohibited algorithms with migration timelines for any legacy deployments. The standards document must be reviewed at least annually and updated when industry guidance changes — for example, when NIST deprecates algorithm variants or when post-quantum cryptography standards mature.

Key management governance covers the entire key lifecycle across the organization: generation using approved methods and entropy sources, secure distribution (never in plaintext via email or chat), storage in dedicated key management systems (HSMs, cloud KMS, or software vaults with appropriate protection), usage monitoring and rotation schedules, revocation procedures when compromise is suspected, and secure destruction when keys reach end of life. Key management procedures must address both symmetric and asymmetric keys, and must cover operational IT keys (TLS, disk encryption) as well as product-related keys (firmware signing, secure boot) where applicable.

Certificate management must ensure that all X.509 certificates are inventoried, monitored for expiration, and renewed before they lapse. Certificate authorities (internal CAs, public CAs) must be documented, and certificate issuance must follow a defined approval process. Wildcard certificate usage must be controlled and justified. Certificate transparency logs should be monitored for unauthorized certificate issuance for the organization's domains.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping centers on Annex A control A.8.24 (Use of cryptography), with governance aspects touching A.5.31 (Legal and regulatory requirements, particularly export controls on cryptography) and A.5.37 (Documented operating procedures for key management). ISO 21434 Clauses 8 and 9 address cryptographic requirements for vehicle cybersecurity, and the organizational cryptographic framework must ensure alignment between IT cryptography and product cryptography.

Common audit findings include no formal process for selecting or approving cryptographic solutions (teams choose independently), cryptographic standards documents that reference outdated guidance, key management procedures that exist only for production PKI but not for operational IT (VPN keys, database encryption keys), certificates discovered expired in production causing service outages, and no roadmap for post-quantum cryptographic migration.

Evidence needed includes the cryptographic governance policy, approved standards document with review dates, key management procedures covering the full lifecycle, key and certificate inventory, HSM or KMS deployment documentation, certificate monitoring system evidence, export control compliance assessment for cryptographic technology, and cryptographic agility roadmap.

mappings.notes: Implementation controls and crypto governance alignment.

clauses.title: Audit Program Management
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 16 (part of ISA 6.0 extended controls) establishes requirements for the internal audit program as it relates to information security, expanding on the compliance audit requirements of Chapter 13 with detailed expectations for audit planning, execution, reporting, and follow-up.

The audit program must be formally documented with a multi-year plan that ensures all VDA ISA control areas, information security processes, and critical systems are audited within a defined cycle — typically no longer than three years, with high-risk areas audited annually. The program must be risk-based: areas with higher inherent risk, recent incidents, or significant changes must be audited more frequently. The audit program must be approved by management and allocated adequate resources (auditor time, tools, budget).

Auditor competence and independence are fundamental requirements. Internal auditors must be trained in information security auditing (ISO 27001 Lead Auditor certification or equivalent is expected) and must be independent of the area they audit. For small organizations where full independence is difficult to achieve, compensating measures must be documented — such as using external auditors for areas where internal independence cannot be guaranteed, or cross-auditing between departments.

Audit execution must follow a structured methodology: planning (scope, objectives, criteria, schedule), evidence gathering (document review, interviews, observation, technical testing), finding classification (nonconformity, observation, opportunity for improvement), reporting, and follow-up. Audit findings must be documented with sufficient detail to enable the audited area to understand the issue and take corrective action. Finding classifications must be consistent and calibrated — assessors look for evidence that audit findings reflect actual control weaknesses rather than trivial documentation gaps.

Corrective action management requires that all nonconformities identified through internal audits are assigned to owners, given deadlines, and tracked to closure. Root cause analysis should be performed for significant findings. The effectiveness of corrective actions must be verified — not just that the action was taken, but that the underlying control weakness has been resolved. Assessors check for patterns: recurring findings in the same area across multiple audit cycles indicate ineffective corrective action.

Management reporting must ensure that audit results reach top management through defined reporting channels. The management review process (per ISO 27001 Clause 9.3) must include audit results as an input, and management decisions or actions resulting from audit findings must be documented.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Clauses 9.2 (Internal audit) and 9.3 (Management review), plus Annex A control A.5.35 (Independent review of information security). ISO 21434 Clause 5.4.4 requires cybersecurity audit capability, and the TISAX audit program should include cybersecurity process audits where the organization performs automotive cybersecurity engineering.

Common audit findings include audit programs that are not risk-based (every area audited with equal frequency regardless of risk), auditors who lack formal training or certification, audit reports that contain only document review findings without technical verification, corrective actions that are closed without verification of effectiveness, management reviews that mention audit results but generate no actions, and audit programs that do not cover third-party or cloud service provider controls.

Evidence needed includes the audit program plan with risk-based scheduling, auditor qualification records (certifications, training), audit reports with finding classifications, corrective action log with ownership, deadlines, and closure evidence, root cause analysis for significant findings, management review minutes showing audit input and resulting actions, and evidence of corrective action effectiveness verification.

clauses.title: Prototype and Asset Protection
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 17 covers prototype and pre-production asset protection, a TISAX-specific requirement that goes beyond standard ISO 27001 controls. This chapter applies to organizations with the "Proto" (prototype protection) or "Vehicle" (test vehicle handling) scope labels and is assessed at AL3, requiring an on-site audit. It is one of the most distinctive aspects of TISAX and an area where many suppliers initially fail their assessment.

Prototype classification requires that all prototype components, vehicles, and related information be classified according to the OEM's protection requirements. OEMs typically define prototype classification levels (e.g., VW uses "VP" for Vorprototyp and "P" for Prototyp; BMW uses tiered protection levels). The supplier must map these classifications to internal handling rules and ensure that all staff handling prototypes understand the applicable classification and its implications.

Physical protection of prototypes requires dedicated, access-controlled areas for storing and working with prototype components and vehicles. These areas must have reinforced access controls (badge plus PIN or biometric), CCTV coverage with adequate retention, visitor management with escort requirements, and window treatments or opaque coverings to prevent visual access from outside. For test vehicles, designated parking areas with physical barriers, covers or camouflage, and access restrictions must be provided. Vehicle movements (deliveries, test drives, transport to events) must be planned, authorized, and documented.

Photography and recording device restrictions are a key requirement. Personal mobile phones, cameras, and other recording devices must be prohibited in prototype areas. Technical enforcement (phone lockers, signal blocking where legal, camera detection) is preferred over policy-only controls. Exceptions for work-related photography must be individually authorized, documented, and the resulting images must be classified and controlled.

Digital prototype data — CAD models, simulation results, photographs, specifications — must be protected with the same rigor as physical prototypes. Access controls, encryption, watermarking, and transfer restrictions must be applied. Digital transfer of prototype data to external parties must be individually authorized and use approved secure channels. Data loss prevention (DLP) controls should detect unauthorized transmission of prototype data.

Camouflage and concealment requirements apply to prototype vehicles that must be moved outside secure areas (for testing on public roads or transport between facilities). The organization must follow OEM-specific camouflage requirements, which may include vinyl wraps, body cladding, or false panel shapes. Camouflage application, removal, and disposal must be documented.

ISO 27001:2022 does not have direct equivalents for prototype protection — this is an automotive-specific extension. However, the underlying controls map to Annex A physical security (A.7.x), asset management (A.5.9-A.5.14), and information transfer (A.5.14) controls. ISO 21434 addresses protection of cybersecurity work products but not physical prototype protection.

Common audit findings include prototype areas that lack dedicated access control (accessible through general factory access), personal phones permitted in prototype areas without technical enforcement, prototype vehicle movements that are not documented or authorized, digital prototype data stored on general-purpose file servers without additional access restrictions, missing or inconsistent camouflage on prototype vehicles, and staff unfamiliar with the applicable prototype classification levels and their handling requirements.

Evidence needed includes prototype protection procedures, prototype area access control logs, CCTV system documentation for prototype areas, phone and device restriction enforcement evidence, prototype vehicle movement logs with authorization records, digital prototype data access controls and DLP evidence, camouflage application and removal records, NDA registers for staff and visitors with prototype access, and OEM-specific prototype protection requirement acknowledgments.

clauses.title: Cloud and Hosted Service Controls
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 18 (part of ISA 6.0 extended controls) addresses the security requirements for cloud services and hosted infrastructure, reflecting the automotive industry's increasing adoption of cloud platforms for engineering collaboration, data analytics, supply chain management, and production support systems.

Cloud service governance begins with a risk assessment before adopting any cloud service. The assessment must evaluate data classification alignment (can this cloud service handle "confidential" or "strictly confidential" data according to your classification scheme and OEM requirements?), data residency (where will data be stored and processed, and does this comply with contractual and regulatory requirements?), provider security posture (what certifications does the provider hold — ISO 27001, SOC 2, C5?), and exit strategy (how will data be extracted and the service decommissioned?). Some OEMs contractually prohibit storage of their data outside the EU/EEA or on specific cloud providers.

Cloud security architecture must ensure that the organization's security requirements are met in the cloud environment. This covers identity and access management (federation with enterprise IAM, MFA enforcement, privileged access controls), network security (virtual network isolation, network security groups, private endpoints where available), data protection (encryption at rest and in transit, key management including customer-managed keys for classified data), logging and monitoring (cloud audit logs forwarded to the organization's SIEM, alerting on security events), and configuration management (infrastructure as code, security baseline compliance, drift detection).

Shared responsibility model understanding is fundamental. The organization must document which security controls are the provider's responsibility and which are the organization's responsibility for each cloud service used. This documentation must be specific to the actual services deployed (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS each have different responsibility boundaries) and must be reviewed when services change.

Cloud service agreements must include security requirements beyond the provider's standard terms. For automotive suppliers, this typically means data processing agreements (GDPR Article 28), confidentiality clauses aligned with OEM requirements, audit rights (direct or through third-party attestation), incident notification timelines, and data deletion guarantees at contract termination. Multi-tenant risks must be assessed — for strictly confidential OEM data, dedicated or isolated tenancy may be required.

Backup and continuity for cloud services must be addressed. Organizations must verify that provider SLAs meet their RTO/RPO requirements, understand the provider's disaster recovery capabilities, and implement independent backup for critical data (not relying solely on provider-side redundancy).

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A control A.5.23 (Information security for use of cloud services), plus A.5.19-A.5.22 (supplier relationship controls applied to cloud providers) and A.8.20-A.8.22 (network security in cloud contexts). ISO 21434 does not specifically address cloud security but assumes secure infrastructure for cybersecurity engineering activities — cloud-hosted development environments and CI/CD pipelines must meet the same security requirements as on-premises infrastructure.

Common audit findings include cloud services adopted without formal risk assessment, data residency requirements not verified or enforced, cloud IAM not integrated with enterprise identity management (local admin accounts used), encryption at rest using provider-managed keys without assessment of key management adequacy, cloud audit logs not collected or monitored, shared responsibility model not documented for services in use, and cloud service agreements that rely on standard terms without supplementary security requirements.

Evidence needed includes the cloud service register with risk assessment results, data residency documentation, cloud security architecture diagrams, IAM configuration evidence (federation, MFA), encryption configuration (at rest, in transit, key management), cloud audit log integration evidence, shared responsibility documentation per service, cloud service agreements with security clauses, and cloud-specific business continuity and backup documentation.

clauses.title: Organization of Information Security
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 2 addresses how information security is organized within the company, covering internal organization, roles and responsibilities, segregation of duties, contact with authorities and special interest groups, and information security in project management. The assessor evaluates whether security governance is embedded in the organizational structure rather than existing only on paper.

The primary requirement is a defined information security organization with clear roles. An Information Security Officer (ISO) or equivalent must be formally appointed, with documented authority, reporting lines, and sufficient resources. For automotive suppliers, the ISO role must have direct access to top management — TISAX assessors verify this through organizational charts and meeting minutes. Segregation of duties must prevent any single individual from controlling an entire critical process, particularly in IT administration, change management, and access provisioning.

Implementation for automotive suppliers should establish an information security committee or steering group that includes representatives from IT, engineering, quality, and management. Project security must be addressed from the outset — integrate security gates into your APQP (Advanced Product Quality Planning) or project management methodology. Every customer project that handles classified OEM data must have a security classification assessment at project kickoff. Mobile and remote working arrangements require explicit risk assessment and policy coverage, which became a heightened focus area after widespread adoption of remote work.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Clauses 5.1 (Leadership and commitment), 5.3 (Organizational roles), and Annex A controls A.5.2 (Information security roles and responsibilities), A.5.3 (Segregation of duties), A.5.4-A.5.6 (Management responsibilities, contact with authorities and special interest groups), and A.5.8 (Information security in project management). ISO 21434 Clause 5.4 specifies cybersecurity-specific roles including the cybersecurity manager, which should be cross-referenced in the TISAX organizational structure.

Common audit findings include an ISO that reports too far down the hierarchy (buried under IT operations rather than reporting to management), missing evidence of resource allocation for information security activities, project management procedures that lack security considerations, and mobile device management policies that do not reflect actual working practices. Assessors also find missing or outdated contact lists for authorities (data protection authorities, law enforcement, sector CERTs like Auto-ISAC).

Evidence needed includes the organizational chart showing the ISO role and reporting lines, the appointment letter or job description for the ISO, minutes from information security committee meetings, project management procedures showing security integration, a register of contacts with authorities and industry groups, mobile and remote working policies with risk assessments, and evidence of resource allocation (budget, headcount, training time) for information security. For AL3, assessors verify that the ISO has genuinely participated in management meetings and has documented escalation authority.

clauses.title: Human Resource Security
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 3 covers human resource security across three phases: before employment, during employment, and at termination or change of employment. The automotive supply chain depends on people who handle classified OEM designs, prototype specifications, and production data, making personnel security controls a high-priority assessment area.

Before employment, the organization must conduct background verification checks appropriate to the role and the classification level of information the person will access. For roles involving prototype access or strictly confidential OEM data (AL3 scope), this means verifiable identity checks, criminal background screening where legally permitted, and qualification verification. Employment contracts and NDAs must include information security responsibilities, confidentiality obligations specific to automotive OEM data, and obligations that continue after employment ends. Temporary staff and contractors require equivalent contractual coverage.

During employment, ongoing security awareness and training is mandatory. TISAX assessors expect more than a one-time onboarding session — they look for regular, role-appropriate training that covers current threats (phishing, social engineering, insider threats), data classification handling, prototype protection rules (if in scope), and incident reporting procedures. Training must be documented with attendance records and content summaries. A disciplinary process must exist for information security violations, and employees must be aware of it.

At termination or role change, the organization must have documented procedures for revoking access, returning assets (including mobile devices, tokens, and badges), and reminding departing staff of continuing confidentiality obligations. For automotive suppliers, this is critical because engineers frequently move between tier-one suppliers — knowledge of competitor OEM programs must be protected through enforceable post-employment obligations.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.6.1 (Screening), A.6.2 (Terms and conditions of employment), A.6.3 (Information security awareness, education and training), A.6.4 (Disciplinary process), A.6.5 (Responsibilities after termination or change of employment). ISO 21434 Clause 5.4.3 requires competence in cybersecurity roles, which aligns with TISAX training requirements for staff involved in product development.

Common audit findings include background checks that are not performed for contractors or temporary workers, training records that show completion of generic IT security modules without automotive-specific content, missing or unsigned NDAs for staff who joined before the ISMS was established, termination checklists that do not cover all access systems (physical badges, VPN, cloud services, OEM portals), and no evidence of periodic security awareness refresher training. Assessors also flag cases where the disciplinary process is defined but has never been applied or communicated.

Evidence needed includes screening procedures and records (redacted as necessary for privacy), employment contract templates showing security clauses, NDA templates and signed copies, training plans and attendance records, training content materials, the disciplinary process documentation, termination and role-change checklists with completion records, and evidence of continuing obligation reminders sent to departing employees.

clauses.title: Asset Management
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 4 addresses asset management, requiring organizations to identify, classify, and protect information assets throughout their lifecycle. In the automotive supply chain, assets include CAD files, simulation data, tooling specifications, ECU software, calibration data, test results, and prototype photographs — all of which carry OEM-specific classification requirements.

The first requirement is a comprehensive asset inventory. Every information asset must be identified, assigned an owner, and recorded in a register. Asset types include digital (documents, databases, source code, firmware), physical (servers, laptops, removable media, prototype parts), and service-based (cloud platforms, SaaS tools, OEM collaboration portals). Each asset must have an assigned classification level. TISAX uses three classification tiers aligned with VDA: "normal" (internal use), "confidential" (vertraulich), and "strictly confidential" (streng vertraulich). The classification drives handling, storage, transfer, and disposal requirements.

Implementation requires a data classification scheme that maps to OEM expectations. Most German OEMs define their own classification levels and expect suppliers to map these to their internal scheme. BMW uses "BMW Confidential" and "BMW Strictly Confidential" markings. VW Group uses "K" (Konfidentiell) and "GK" (Geheim-Konfidentiell). Your asset management procedure must document how incoming OEM-classified data is mapped to your internal classification and how the required protections are applied. Removable media (USB drives, external hard drives) must be controlled — many automotive suppliers enforce technical USB blocking on all production and engineering workstations, with exceptions managed through a formal request process.

Acceptable use policies must define what employees may and may not do with information assets, covering personal use of company devices, use of personal devices for work (BYOD), cloud storage, email forwarding, and printing of classified documents. Asset return procedures at end of use, project completion, or employee departure must be documented and enforced.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.5.9 (Inventory of information and other associated assets), A.5.10 (Acceptable use of information), A.5.11 (Return of assets), A.5.12 (Classification of information), A.5.13 (Labelling of information), and A.5.14 (Information transfer). ISO 21434 Clause 5.4.5 references management of cybersecurity-relevant work products as controlled assets.

Common audit findings include asset inventories that are incomplete or outdated, missing or inconsistent classification labels on documents and emails, removable media policies that exist on paper but are not technically enforced, no documented process for reclassifying assets when project sensitivity changes, and inadequate disposal procedures for physical media containing classified data (hard drives not degaussed or physically destroyed).

Evidence needed includes the asset inventory with owners and classifications, the data classification scheme, acceptable use policies, removable media control procedures and exception logs, asset return checklists, media disposal records with certificates of destruction, and evidence of periodic asset inventory reviews.

clauses.title: Access Control
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 5 covers access control — the policies, procedures, and technical measures that restrict access to information and systems based on business need and classification level. In automotive supplier environments, access control is high-scrutiny because multiple OEM programs run in parallel, and cross-contamination of classified data between competing customers must be prevented.

The access control policy must define the principles governing access: need-to-know, least privilege, and segregation of duties. It must cover both logical access (systems, applications, networks) and physical access (buildings, server rooms, prototype areas). User access management requires formal processes for registration, provisioning, privilege escalation, and de-provisioning. Every access right must be traceable to a business justification, and a periodic access review must verify that rights remain appropriate. For automotive suppliers, this means project-based access reviews when engineers move between OEM programs.

User authentication must be appropriate to the classification level. For systems handling "confidential" data, strong passwords with defined complexity and rotation are the minimum. For "strictly confidential" data and AL3 scope, multi-factor authentication is required. Privileged access (administrator accounts) must be tightly controlled with separate credentials, session logging, and time-limited elevation where possible. Service accounts and API keys must be inventoried and managed with the same rigor as user accounts.

Application and system access control must enforce authorization at the application level, not just network perimeter. Source code repositories, PLM systems (Teamcenter, Windchill), and file shares containing OEM data must implement role-based access control (RBAC) aligned with project assignments. Network segregation must separate OEM program environments where contractually required — some OEMs mandate dedicated network segments for their data.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.5.15 (Access control), A.5.16 (Identity management), A.5.17 (Authentication information), A.5.18 (Access rights), A.8.2 (Privileged access rights), A.8.3 (Information access restriction), A.8.4 (Access to source code), and A.8.5 (Secure authentication). ISO 21434 Clause 5.4.6 addresses access control for cybersecurity work products and development tools.

Common audit findings include stale user accounts (employees who left but retain system access), excessive administrator privileges granted for convenience, missing or incomplete access reviews, shared accounts in production or engineering environments, lack of MFA on critical systems despite AL3 scope, and no formal process for granting temporary or emergency access. OEM portal access (BMW Connected Drive Partner Portal, VW Group Partner Portal) is also frequently found without proper joiners-movers-leavers processes.

Evidence needed includes the access control policy, user provisioning and de-provisioning procedures, access review records with dates and reviewer names, privileged account inventories, MFA deployment records, network segmentation documentation, RBAC configuration evidence from key systems, and logs showing access review actions taken (rights revoked, accounts disabled).

clauses.title: Cryptography
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 6 establishes requirements for the use of cryptographic controls to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of information. In the automotive supply chain, cryptography protects data in transit between suppliers and OEMs, data at rest on engineering workstations and file servers, and the integrity of software and firmware delivered as part of vehicle systems.

A cryptographic policy must define the organization's approach to encryption, including approved algorithms, minimum key lengths, key management procedures, and use cases where encryption is mandatory. For data classified as "confidential" or "strictly confidential," encryption in transit (TLS 1.2 or higher) and encryption at rest are expected. Email encryption (S/MIME or PGP) must be available for exchanging classified documents with OEM partners. File-level or volume-level encryption must protect classified data on laptops and mobile devices.

Key management is a critical sub-requirement. The organization must document the full key lifecycle: generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction. Cryptographic keys must be protected against unauthorized access — hardware security modules (HSMs) or equivalent key vaults are expected for high-value keys. Certificate management for TLS endpoints, code signing, and email encryption must be tracked with expiry monitoring and renewal procedures.

Implementation in an automotive context must address specific scenarios: encrypted transfer of CAD files and simulation data to OEM portals, encrypted backup storage, full-disk encryption on all laptops (enforced via MDM), and encrypted VPN connections for remote access to engineering networks. For suppliers involved in ECU development, ISO 21434 and UNECE R155 impose additional cryptographic requirements for secure boot, firmware signing, and V2X communication — the TISAX cryptographic policy should reference these product-level requirements even though TISAX itself focuses on organizational IT.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.8.24 (Use of cryptography) and A.8.25 (Secure development lifecycle). ISO 21434 Clauses 8 and 9 address cryptographic measures in product cybersecurity, and the TISAX assessment may probe whether organizational and product cryptographic controls are coordinated.

Common audit findings include reliance on outdated algorithms (SHA-1, 3DES, RSA below 2048-bit), missing or informal key management procedures, expired TLS certificates on internal services, laptops without enforced full-disk encryption, email encryption capability that is deployed but not used for classified exchanges, and no documented process for cryptographic algorithm review when standards evolve. Assessors at AL3 may specifically probe the use of encryption on removable media used for prototype data.

Evidence needed includes the cryptographic policy, approved algorithm and key length list, key management procedures, certificate inventory with expiry dates, MDM configuration showing enforced disk encryption, TLS configuration records for external and internal services, email encryption deployment evidence, and records of cryptographic standard reviews.

clauses.title: Physical and Environmental Security
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 7 covers physical and environmental security, addressing the protection of facilities, equipment, and information from unauthorized physical access, damage, and interference. For automotive suppliers, this extends beyond standard office security to include prototype workshops, testing facilities, paint shops, and secure storage areas for OEM components.

Secure areas must be defined and protected with layered physical controls. The perimeter must prevent unauthorized entry through walls, locked doors, reception staffing, and surveillance. Within the perimeter, zones of increasing security must be established: general office areas, restricted IT areas (server rooms, network closets), and high-security zones (prototype areas, calibration labs, test tracks). Each zone must have access controls proportional to the sensitivity of the work performed there. For AL3 prototype protection scope, dedicated prototype rooms with reinforced access control (badge plus PIN, biometric, or mantrap) are expected.

Equipment security requires protection against environmental threats (fire, flood, power failure, temperature extremes) and unauthorized access. Server rooms must have climate control, fire suppression (gas-based systems for electronics), uninterruptible power supply (UPS), and monitored access logs. Cabling security must prevent interception or damage — sensitive network cabling should be routed through protected conduits. Equipment maintenance must be performed by authorized personnel, and maintenance records must be retained.

Off-site security covers the protection of equipment and media outside the organization's premises. Laptops taken off-site must have full-disk encryption, remote wipe capability, and screen privacy filters for use in public spaces. Equipment disposal must ensure that all data-bearing media are sanitized or destroyed before leaving the organization's control — certificates of destruction are expected.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.7.1 (Physical security perimeters), A.7.2 (Physical entry), A.7.3 (Securing offices, rooms and facilities), A.7.4 (Physical security monitoring), A.7.5 (Protecting against physical and environmental threats), A.7.6 (Working in secure areas), A.7.7 (Clear desk and clear screen), A.7.8 (Equipment siting and protection), A.7.9 through A.7.14 (covering maintenance, disposal, utilities, cabling, and off-premises). ISO 21434 does not directly address physical security but assumes it as a prerequisite for secure development environments.

Common audit findings include server rooms without environmental monitoring or alerting, missing visitor logs or unescorted visitor access to restricted areas, tailgating into badge-controlled areas without mantrap or turnstile enforcement, prototype areas accessible through unsealed secondary doors or windows, clear desk policy violations (classified documents left on desks overnight), equipment disposal without documented sanitization, and CCTV systems with insufficient retention or gaps in coverage.

Evidence needed includes site security plans with zone maps, physical access control system logs, visitor management records, CCTV system documentation and retention settings, environmental monitoring records for server rooms, equipment maintenance logs, media disposal certificates, clear desk audit results, and prototype area access logs.

clauses.title: Operations Security
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 8 addresses operations security — the procedures and controls that ensure information processing facilities operate correctly, securely, and resiliently. This covers change management, capacity planning, malware protection, backup, logging, vulnerability management, and audit considerations for production IT environments.

Documented operating procedures must exist for all critical IT systems, covering startup, shutdown, backup, error handling, and escalation. Change management must ensure that changes to production systems, applications, and infrastructure are assessed for security impact, tested, approved, and documented before implementation. In automotive supplier environments, change management must coordinate with OEM requirements — some OEMs mandate notification of changes to systems that process their classified data.

Malware protection must be deployed across all endpoints (workstations, laptops, servers) and at network boundaries (email gateways, web proxies). Anti-malware solutions must be centrally managed with current signatures and real-time scanning. Engineering workstations that connect to vehicle networks or ECU test benches require particular attention because they may run specialized software that conflicts with standard endpoint protection — documented exceptions and compensating controls are expected.

Backup procedures must ensure that information can be recovered after incidents. Backup scope, frequency, retention, and restoration testing must be documented. Restoration tests must be performed regularly and results recorded — assessors consistently check whether backup restoration has actually been tested, not just scheduled.

Logging and monitoring must capture security-relevant events across systems: authentication attempts, access to classified data, privilege escalation, system changes, and security alerts. Logs must be protected against tampering and retained for a defined period. Clock synchronization across all systems is required for forensic correlation. Vulnerability management must include regular scanning, patch management with defined timelines, and documented risk acceptance for patches that cannot be applied immediately.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.5.37 (Documented operating procedures), A.8.6 (Capacity management), A.8.7 (Protection against malware), A.8.8 (Management of technical vulnerabilities), A.8.9 (Configuration management), A.8.13 (Information backup), A.8.15 (Logging), A.8.16 (Monitoring activities), A.8.17 (Clock synchronization), and A.8.19 (Installation of software on operational systems). ISO 21434 Clause 8 addresses operational cybersecurity for product development environments, including CI/CD pipeline security and build environment integrity.

Common audit findings include outdated or missing operating procedures, change management processes that are bypassed for "urgent" changes without retrospective review, malware protection gaps on engineering workstations or Linux servers, backup restoration tests that have never been performed, log retention periods that are too short for investigation needs, vulnerability scans that are performed but not acted upon within defined timelines, and missing patch management metrics.

Evidence needed includes documented operating procedures, change management records with approval evidence, anti-malware deployment reports and exception documentation, backup procedures and restoration test records, log management architecture documentation, sample log entries showing required events, vulnerability scan reports with remediation tracking, and patch management status reports.

clauses.title: Communications Security
clauses.guidance:
VDA ISA Chapter 9 covers communications security — the controls protecting information in networks and information transfer between organizations. In the automotive supply chain, where CAD data, calibration files, test reports, and prototype images move constantly between OEMs, tier-one suppliers, and sub-suppliers, secure communication is a baseline expectation.

Network security management requires segmented network architecture with defined trust zones. Production networks, engineering networks, office networks, guest networks, and OT (operational technology) networks must be separated through firewalls, VLANs, or physical isolation. Network services must be identified, agreed upon, and monitored. Firewalls must enforce least-privilege rules — "allow all" rules are an immediate audit finding. Intrusion detection or prevention systems (IDS/IPS) should monitor traffic at network boundaries, particularly between segments handling different OEM programs.

For organizations with manufacturing operations, the IT/OT boundary requires particular attention. Manufacturing execution systems (MES), SCADA, and programmable logic controllers must be isolated from the corporate network with controlled, monitored crossing points. This is increasingly relevant as TISAX assessors evaluate the risk of lateral movement from compromised IT systems to production control systems.

Information transfer policies must govern how classified information is sent to and received from external parties. Approved transfer mechanisms must be defined for each classification level — for example, OEM portals for CAD data, encrypted email (S/MIME) for documents, SFTP for large datasets, and physical courier with chain-of-custody documentation for prototype parts. NDAs and confidentiality agreements must be in place before any classified transfer occurs. Agreements must specify permitted transfer methods, handling requirements, and breach notification obligations.

Electronic messaging security covers email, instant messaging, and collaboration platforms. Email must support encryption and digital signatures for classified content. Collaboration platforms (Teams, Slack, SharePoint) must enforce access controls aligned with data classification — public channels must not contain classified OEM data. Automated DLP (Data Loss Prevention) rules should detect and block unauthorized transmission of classified content through email and web channels.

ISO 27001:2022 mapping covers Annex A controls A.8.20 (Networks security), A.8.21 (Security of network services), A.8.22 (Segregation of networks), A.5.14 (Information transfer), and A.8.12 (Data leakage prevention). ISO 21434 Clause 7 addresses secure communication within distributed development environments, which aligns with TISAX network security requirements for engineering networks.

Common audit findings include flat network architectures without meaningful segmentation, firewall rules that have accumulated over years without review, missing or unenforced information transfer policies, classified documents sent via unencrypted email, file sharing through unapproved cloud services (shadow IT), and no DLP controls despite handling strictly confidential OEM data.

Evidence needed includes network architecture diagrams with zone definitions, firewall rule sets with review dates, information transfer policies and approved channel lists, NDAs with external parties, email encryption deployment evidence, DLP policy configurations, and network monitoring or IDS/IPS deployment documentation.

clauses.title: Prototype Protection
clauses.guidance:
The TISAX Prototype Protection module is a standalone assessment objective, separate from (but complementary to) the information security chapters. It applies when an organization carries the "Proto" scope label, indicating it handles OEM prototype components, pre-series vehicles, or related design data. Assessment is always at AL3 (on-site). This module represents one of the most automotive-specific aspects of TISAX and is where suppliers from non-automotive backgrounds most frequently encounter assessment failures.

Prototype protection is structured around three pillars: physical protection, organizational measures, and digital data protection. Physical protection encompasses the secure facilities, access controls, and environmental measures that prevent unauthorized visual or physical access to prototype assets. Organizational measures cover the policies, procedures, roles, and contractual arrangements that govern prototype handling. Digital data protection addresses the technical controls for electronic prototype information — CAD models, design documents, photographs, test data, and simulation results.

OEM-specific requirements form a layer above the VDA ISA baseline. Each OEM issues its own prototype protection requirements, and the supplier must map these to its internal processes. BMW's "Anforderungen Prototypenschutz," VW Group's "Prototypenschutz Anforderungen," and Mercedes-Benz's prototype requirements each contain specific obligations regarding facility classification, camouflage standards, transport procedures, and reporting. The organization must maintain a register of applicable OEM-specific requirements and demonstrate compliance with each.

The designated prototype protection officer (or equivalent role) must have sufficient authority and organizational visibility to enforce prototype protection requirements across all departments involved. This person must oversee NDA management, visitor screening for prototype areas, exception handling for photography, transport authorization, and incident reporting specific to prototype breaches.

Transport and logistics for prototype parts and vehicles require documented procedures covering route planning, vehicle concealment during transport, authorized transport providers, chain-of-custody documentation, and delivery confirmation. For international transport, customs documentation must not reveal prototype details unnecessarily. Prototype vehicles on public roads must be camouflaged according to OEM specifications, with camouflage application and removal documented.

Photography and media control in prototype areas requires a comprehensive approach: personal device prohibition with technical enforcement (phone lockers, detection systems), authorized photography with individual work orders specifying what may be photographed, secure handling and storage of authorized images, and procedures for detecting and responding to unauthorized photography (including social media monitoring).

Visitor management for prototype areas requires advance authorization, NDA execution before entry, escort throughout the visit, device surrender (phones, cameras, smartwatches), and documented entry and exit times. Visitors must not be permitted unescorted access under any circumstances.

ISO 27001:2022 does not address prototype protection directly — these are automotive-specific requirements without ISO equivalent. However, the underlying control mechanisms map to ISO 27001 physical security (A.7.x), asset management (A.5.9-A.5.14), and human resource security (A.6.x) controls. ISO 21434 addresses protection of cybersecurity work products, which may overlap with digital prototype data protection for connected vehicle components.

Common audit findings in prototype protection include inadequate physical separation of prototype areas from general production, personal devices found in prototype zones (policy exists but technical enforcement is absent), prototype vehicle movements without documented authorization, camouflage that does not meet OEM specifications, digital prototype data accessible to users who do not need it for their current project, visitor NDAs not executed before prototype area entry, and missing or incomplete prototype asset tracking.

Evidence needed includes the prototype protection policy and procedures, prototype protection officer appointment, OEM-specific requirement register with compliance mapping, prototype area access control system logs and CCTV records, device prohibition enforcement evidence, transport authorization and chain-of-custody records, camouflage documentation (application, specification compliance, removal), visitor management records for prototype areas, NDA register, authorized photography log, and social media monitoring procedures.

clauses.title: Network layer timing profiles
clauses.guidance: Defines timing and latency profile expectations for stable OBD communication across tool and vehicle variants.

mappings.notes: OBD timing behavior depends on transport-layer timing semantics.

clauses.title: Service mode access control
clauses.guidance: Defines policy controls and authorization conditions for access to sensitive OBD service modes.

mappings.notes: Service-mode access decisions should align with diagnostic security access controls.

clauses.title: Diagnostic gateway interoperability
clauses.guidance: Defines interoperability behavior when OBD services are mediated by Ethernet or zonal diagnostic gateways.

mappings.notes: Gateway routing constraints affect external OBD tool behavior.

clauses.title: Emissions data retention governance
clauses.guidance: Defines retention, traceability, and evidence expectations for emissions diagnostics data histories.

mappings.notes: Diagnostics evidence governance is aligned with update documentation obligations.

clauses.title: Cable locking safety interlocks
clauses.guidance: Defines connector lock-state controls and safety interlock behavior for conductive charging sessions.

mappings.notes: Physical lock behavior affects safe operation controls.

clauses.title: Insulation coordination testing
