mappings.notes: Insulation monitoring requirements complement conductive safety validation.

clauses.title: Charger-vehicle handshake safety
clauses.guidance: Defines safety preconditions and sequencing constraints during charger-vehicle session establishment.

mappings.notes: Session establishment safety and cybersecurity conditions are interdependent.

clauses.title: Environmental boundary conditions
clauses.guidance: Defines environmental operating boundaries and degraded-mode handling for conductive charging safety.

mappings.notes: Boundary-condition assumptions should be traceable in system-level safety design.

clauses.title: Certificate-based access for RMI
clauses.guidance: Defines certificate-based identity and trust requirements for role-based RMI access.

mappings.notes: Identity and trust controls align with cybersecurity concept and implementation controls.

clauses.title: Dataset integrity and provenance
clauses.guidance: Defines integrity verification and provenance metadata for published repair datasets.

mappings.notes: Artifact integrity and provenance expectations mirror update package governance.

clauses.title: Independent operator access governance
clauses.guidance: Defines governance controls ensuring fair and traceable independent operator access workflows.

mappings.notes: Access governance obligations intersect supplier security governance practices.

clauses.title: Service portal availability management
clauses.guidance: Defines resilience and availability expectations for digital service portals delivering RMI content.

mappings.notes: Service platform availability governance principles are shared.

clauses.title: Semantic version compatibility policy
clauses.guidance: Defines version compatibility policies for signal trees to prevent downstream integration breakage.

mappings.notes: Signal schema evolution should align with extended vehicle API governance.

clauses.title: Digital twin governance profiles
clauses.guidance: Defines governance profiles for digital twin synchronization and authoritative signal ownership.

mappings.notes: VSS twin models complement ExVe service-layer twin handling.

clauses.title: Data contract conformance testing
clauses.guidance: Defines conformance testing profiles for signal contracts consumed by cloud and diagnostics services.

mappings.notes: Conformance quality rules are needed for reliable cross-tool interpretation.

clauses.title: Signal deprecation lifecycle policy
clauses.guidance: Defines signal deprecation and retirement lifecycle to preserve platform stability and traceability.

mappings.notes: Deprecation records should be governed similarly to update release records.

clauses.title: Audit trail requirements
clauses.guidance: Defines immutable audit trail requirements for cross-organization data access and operations.

mappings.notes: Methodology controls should materialize as service-level audit implementations.

clauses.title: Data access revocation flow
clauses.guidance: Defines revocation sequence handling for third-party access rights and delegated service credentials.

mappings.notes: Methodology revocation controls require service implementation support.

clauses.title: Interface contract stability
clauses.guidance: Defines stability requirements and backward-compatibility constraints for external interface contracts.

mappings.notes: Signal schema governance contributes to interface contract stability.

clauses.title: Zero-trust endpoint posture
clauses.guidance: Defines endpoint posture and continuous verification expectations for connected platform actors.

mappings.notes: Endpoint trust posture supports vehicle cybersecurity controls.

clauses.title: Safety validation planning
clauses.guidance:
Safety validation planning is defined in Part 4 Clause 9 and addresses the systematic confirmation that the safety goals are achieved at the vehicle level under real-world conditions. While verification (Part 8 Clause 9) confirms that work products are correct with respect to their inputs, validation confirms that the item achieves its intended safety in its operational environment. Validation is the final check before start of production and must demonstrate that the item is safe in the context of the complete vehicle.

The safety validation plan defines the validation objectives (derived from safety goals), the validation environment (test vehicles, test tracks, simulation environments), the test cases (derived from HARA operational situations and hazardous events), the pass/fail criteria, and the responsible parties. For each safety goal, the validation plan must identify how the goal will be demonstrated — through testing, analysis, simulation, or a combination. ASIL D safety goals typically require more extensive validation, including fault injection testing where faults are deliberately introduced to verify that safety mechanisms respond correctly.

Safety validation interacts with SOTIF (ISO 21448) validation because some hazardous events arise not from system faults but from functional insufficiencies or misuse — scenarios that ISO 26262 does not fully cover. The validation plan should be coordinated with the SOTIF validation plan to avoid gaps. For autonomous driving features, both random hardware failures (ISO 26262) and triggering conditions (ISO 21448) must be validated in the same operational design domain.

From a cybersecurity perspective, safety validation planning must include scenarios where cybersecurity attacks could cause safety violations. This does not mean every penetration test is a safety validation test, but it means that attack scenarios identified in the TARA that map to HARA hazardous events should be represented in the safety validation plan. For example, if the TARA identifies that spoofed radar objects could cause the automated emergency braking system to brake unexpectedly (an ASIL D hazardous event), the safety validation plan should include a test case that verifies the system's response when spoofed radar data is injected.

Fault injection testing under the validation plan should include cybersecurity-relevant faults: corrupted messages (without authentication), delayed messages (denial-of-service conditions), and manipulated sensor data. These fault injection tests validate that the safety mechanisms allocated during the design phase are effective under conditions that include both random faults and adversarial inputs. The validation report must demonstrate that safety goals remain achieved under the specified attack conditions, or document the residual risk and its acceptance rationale.

mappings.notes: Functional safety and SOTIF validation plans must be harmonized for ADS features.

clauses.title: Tool qualification evidence
clauses.guidance:
Tool qualification under ISO 26262 Part 8 Clause 11 ensures that software tools used in the functional safety lifecycle do not introduce undetected errors that could compromise safety. Every tool that processes safety-relevant work products — requirements management tools, modeling tools, code generators, compilers, static analyzers, test frameworks, and simulation environments — must be evaluated for its potential impact on safety and qualified accordingly.

The qualification process uses two parameters to determine the tool confidence level (TCL). Tool Impact (TI) assesses whether the tool can introduce or fail to detect errors in safety work products: TI1 means the tool can introduce errors, TI2 means it cannot introduce errors but may fail to detect them. Tool Error Detection (TD) assesses whether errors introduced by the tool would be detected by subsequent lifecycle activities: TD1 means high confidence in downstream detection, TD2 means moderate, TD3 means low. The combination determines the TCL: TCL 1 (lowest risk, no qualification required) through TCL 3 (highest risk, rigorous qualification required).

TCL 3 tools (TI1 and TD3) require the most effort: the user must validate the tool's output through an independent method, perform tool operational requirements testing, and document the tool qualification results. TCL 2 tools require either increased confidence from use (tool operational requirements, established use history) or validation of the tool's output. TCL 1 requires no additional qualification beyond the normal development process.

The cybersecurity-safety interaction in tool qualification has two aspects. First, tools themselves are attack surfaces. A compromised code generator or compiler can inject malicious code into safety-critical software without the developer's knowledge (a supply-chain attack). ISO 21434 Clause 7 requires cybersecurity considerations for development tools and infrastructure. The tool qualification process should include integrity verification of the tool installation (hash verification, signed binaries), access controls on the tool environment, and monitoring for unauthorized tool modifications.

Second, cybersecurity analysis tools may need to be qualified if they produce evidence used in the safety case. If a static analysis tool is used to verify both MISRA C compliance (safety) and CWE coverage (cybersecurity), and the results are cited in the safety case, the tool must be qualified for its safety-relevant use. Conversely, if a penetration testing tool's results are referenced to support a freedom-from-interference argument (for example, demonstrating that network attacks cannot reach a safety-critical ECU), the tool's limitations and coverage should be documented to the same rigor as safety test tool qualification. This ensures that cybersecurity evidence used in safety arguments meets the same integrity standard as other safety evidence.

mappings.notes: Tool assurance and governance evidence can be reused across safety and cybersecurity activities.

clauses.title: Operational monitoring assumptions
clauses.guidance:
Operational monitoring assumptions define the conditions and constraints under which the safety argument remains valid during the vehicle's operational life. Part 7 Clause 6 and Part 3 Clause 5 (item definition) establish the operational context assumptions, while Part 4 Clause 6 (technical safety concept) defines how monitoring mechanisms verify that these assumptions hold during operation.

Safety arguments typically depend on assumptions about the operating environment: temperature ranges within which sensors maintain accuracy, road surface conditions under which braking systems achieve specified deceleration, driver attentiveness levels that support controllability assessments, and maintenance schedules that ensure safety-critical components remain within specification. When these assumptions are violated, the safety argument may not hold, and the system must detect the violation and respond appropriately (typically by transitioning to a degraded mode or safe state).

Operational monitoring mechanisms include sensor plausibility checks (comparing multiple sensors measuring the same physical quantity), environmental monitoring (detecting conditions outside the operational design domain), system health monitoring (battery voltage, processor temperature, memory utilization), and driver monitoring (attention detection for partially automated systems). Each monitoring mechanism has detection coverage, a response time, and an associated ASIL level.

The cybersecurity-safety interaction in operational monitoring is significant because cyberattacks can systematically violate safety assumptions in ways that monitoring mechanisms may not detect. For example, if the safety argument assumes that wheel speed sensor data is accurate and the monitoring mechanism cross-checks left and right wheel speeds for plausibility, an attacker who can manipulate both sensors simultaneously (through a compromised sensor gateway ECU) can provide plausible but incorrect data that defeats the monitoring mechanism.

ISO 21434 Clause 13 requires continuous cybersecurity monitoring during operations, including vulnerability monitoring, threat intelligence, and incident response. These cybersecurity monitoring activities should be coordinated with safety monitoring assumptions. When a new vulnerability is discovered that could affect a safety monitoring mechanism, the safety impact assessment must determine whether the safety argument is still valid. If a CVE affects the firmware of a sensor used in a safety-critical plausibility check, the safety team must evaluate whether the vulnerability could lead to undetected safety assumption violations.

The SOTIF standard (ISO 21448) extends the concept of operational monitoring assumptions to include triggering conditions — operational situations that cause functional insufficiencies in the intended functionality. The combination of ISO 26262 fault monitoring, ISO 21434 cybersecurity monitoring, and ISO 21448 triggering condition monitoring creates a comprehensive monitoring framework where each standard contributes assumptions that the others must consider.

mappings.notes: SOTIF assumptions should be explicitly aligned with safety monitoring assumptions.

clauses.title: Hardware-software interface safety
clauses.guidance:
The hardware-software interface (HSI) specification is defined in Part 4 Clause 7 and Part 6 Clause 6.4.3 as a critical work product that bridges hardware design (Part 5) and software design (Part 6). The HSI defines the agreements between hardware and software regarding shared resources, timing constraints, register interfaces, interrupt behavior, memory layout, diagnostic capabilities, and operating modes. A correct and complete HSI is essential because misunderstandings at this boundary are a common source of systematic failures.

The HSI must specify for each hardware-software interaction point the hardware resource (register, memory region, I/O pin, peripheral), the access method (direct register access, DMA, driver API), the timing constraints (maximum latency, minimum sampling frequency, interrupt response time), the data format and range (resolution, endianness, valid ranges), the error behavior (what happens when hardware reports an error flag, timeout, or illegal value), and the initialization sequence (power-up defaults, required configuration steps before safety-critical operation begins).

For safety mechanisms that depend on hardware features, the HSI must explicitly define the diagnostic capability. For example, if the safety concept relies on the microcontroller's memory protection unit (MPU) to enforce freedom from interference, the HSI must specify which MPU regions are configured, what access permissions apply, and how MPU violation exceptions are handled by the software. If the safety concept relies on a watchdog timer, the HSI must define the timeout period, the refresh mechanism, and the hardware response to a timeout event.

The cybersecurity-safety interaction at the HSI is direct and consequential. Hardware security features — HSM access, secure boot status registers, cryptographic accelerator interfaces, debug port lock status — must be specified in the HSI when they affect safety-relevant functions. If software assumes that secure boot has verified firmware integrity before safety-critical functions execute, this assumption must be documented in the HSI and verified during system integration testing. If the HSM provides key material for SecOC message authentication, the HSI must define the key access API, the latency of cryptographic operations, and the error behavior when the HSM is unavailable.

ASIL-relevant HSI items inherit the ASIL level of the safety function they support. An HSI specification for a register that controls an ASIL D safety mechanism must be verified to ASIL D rigor — meaning that the specification must be reviewed by an independent reviewer (for ASIL C/D), and the hardware and software implementations must both be tested against the HSI specification. When cybersecurity features form part of the HSI for a safety function, the cybersecurity development process under ISO 21434 must produce evidence at a level consistent with the ASIL. A CAL 1 cybersecurity assessment of an HSM interface that supports an ASIL D safety function represents a gap that the safety assessor will identify.

mappings.notes: Security hardening at interfaces should not violate safety interface assumptions.

clauses.title: Service authentication profiles
clauses.guidance:
Service authentication profiles in AUTOSAR define standardized patterns for authenticating and authorizing service-oriented communication between ECUs across trust domains. As vehicles transition from signal-based CAN communication to service-oriented Ethernet architectures, authentication must shift from per-message (SecOC) to per-service and per-session models.

Profile Taxonomy. Authentication profiles are categorized by strength and mechanism. Profile None provides no authentication — suitable only for non-security-relevant services within a single trust domain behind firewall protection. Profile PreSharedKey uses symmetric keys shared between service provider and consumer for lightweight mutual authentication — appropriate for intra-domain services with moderate security requirements. Profile Certificate uses X.509 certificate-based mutual TLS authentication — required for cross-domain services and any service accessible from external interfaces. Profile Token uses short-lived authentication tokens issued by a central authentication service — applicable in AUTOSAR Adaptive platforms with centralized identity management.

Profile Assignment Methodology. Each service in the vehicle service catalog should be assigned an authentication profile based on the ISO 21434 TARA results. Services that affect vehicle dynamics (brake, steering, propulsion) require Certificate or Token profiles regardless of network topology. Services carrying personal data (location, driver behavior) require encrypted channels with mutual authentication. Infotainment services within an isolated domain may use PreSharedKey or None with compensating network controls. The profile assignment should be documented as part of the cybersecurity specifications and reviewed during cybersecurity assessment.

Cross-Domain Authentication. When a service call crosses domain boundaries (e.g., an infotainment application requesting vehicle speed data from the powertrain domain), the gateway must enforce the authentication profile defined for that cross-domain service. The caller presents credentials appropriate to the profile, the gateway verifies them (using CSM for cryptographic operations and KeyM for certificate validation), and only then forwards the request to the target domain. Failed authentication attempts should be logged to IdsM as potential unauthorized access attempts.

Platform Integration. In AUTOSAR Adaptive, service authentication integrates with the Identity and Access Management (IAM) framework. The ara::iam service manages authentication state and issues authorization decisions for each service invocation. In AUTOSAR Classic, service authentication is implemented through custom BSW modules or SecOC-based per-PDU authentication on the service transport layer. The choice depends on platform capabilities and performance constraints. R155 evidence should include the service authentication profile mapping and verification test results demonstrating that unauthenticated access to protected services is rejected.

mappings.notes: Service authentication controls should align with external API authorization principles.

clauses.title: Secure diagnostics proxy patterns
clauses.guidance:
Secure diagnostics proxy patterns in AUTOSAR define security controls for routed diagnostic access in architectures where diagnostic requests traverse proxy ECUs before reaching target ECUs. This pattern is common in zonal E/E architectures where a zone controller acts as a diagnostic proxy for ECUs in its zone, and in connected vehicle architectures where remote diagnostics passes through telematics and gateway modules.

Proxy Security Functions. The diagnostics proxy performs several security-critical functions. Authentication enforcement: the proxy verifies the diagnostic client's credentials before forwarding requests to target ECUs, preventing unauthenticated access to ECUs that may lack their own authentication capability. Authorization filtering: the proxy applies policy rules that restrict which diagnostic services and target ECUs a given client can access — remote diagnostic clients may be limited to read-only operations, while workshop clients with physical presence have broader access. Request sanitization: the proxy validates diagnostic request parameters before forwarding, preventing malformed requests that could exploit parsing vulnerabilities in target ECU diagnostic stacks.

Routing Security in Zonal Architectures. In zonal architectures, each zone controller proxies diagnostic access to sensors, actuators, and low-capability ECUs within its zone. The zone controller terminates the external diagnostic session (DoIP/TLS from the diagnostic tester or remote server), authenticates the client, and establishes separate internal diagnostic sessions (CAN UDS) to target ECUs within its zone. This two-stage model means the zone controller holds both the external TLS context and internal CAN authentication state, making it a high-value target. Compromise of a zone controller's diagnostic proxy function could provide an attacker with diagnostic access to all ECUs in that zone.

Remote Diagnostics via SOVD. ISO 17978 (SOVD) introduces service-oriented diagnostic access where diagnostic operations are exposed as REST-like services. When SOVD access passes through a diagnostic proxy, the proxy must enforce SOVD-specific security: OAuth-based token validation for API access, per-operation authorization checks against the SOVD capability model, and rate limiting to prevent resource exhaustion on target ECUs. The proxy must preserve the security context — including caller identity and authorization scope — through the routing chain so that downstream components can make accurate access control decisions.

Audit and Monitoring. All diagnostic proxy operations should be logged with sufficient detail for forensic analysis: timestamp, client identity, target ECU, service requested, parameters, and result. Diagnostic proxy logs are a primary forensic data source when investigating potential unauthorized access. Failed authentication attempts, authorization denials, and unusual request patterns should trigger IdsM security events. R155 threat 7.2.2.2(c) covers unauthorized diagnostic access, and the proxy security architecture with its logging and monitoring capabilities is a key evidence item for type approval.

mappings.notes: Remote diagnostics proxy behavior should remain consistent with SOVD service invocation constraints.

clauses.title: Secure OTA client integration
clauses.guidance:
Secure OTA (Over-The-Air) client integration in AUTOSAR defines how the OTA update client interacts with AUTOSAR security modules to ensure that software updates are delivered and installed with appropriate security controls. The OTA client is the vehicle-side component that receives, validates, and installs software updates — it must integrate tightly with the Crypto Stack, KeyM, Secure Boot, and IdsM.

Update Package Validation. Before any update package is applied, the OTA client must verify its authenticity and integrity. The package is signed by the OEM's code signing infrastructure using asymmetric cryptography (typically ECDSA-P256 or RSA-3072). The OTA client uses CSM to perform signature verification, with the code signing public key stored in a KeyM-managed HSM key slot. The verification chain must extend to the trusted root certificate — the same chain used for secure boot. Update packages should include version metadata that the OTA client validates against the rollback protection counter before proceeding with installation. ISO 24089 Clause 8 defines the distribution and deployment requirements that the OTA client must satisfy.

Secure Download and Storage. Update packages are downloaded over an authenticated and encrypted channel (TLS 1.2+ with mutual authentication). The TLS connection terminates at the telematics control unit or central compute platform, which then distributes package segments to target ECUs via the vehicle's internal network. During download, the OTA client must verify package integrity incrementally (streaming hash verification) rather than waiting for the complete download, to detect corruption or tampering early. Temporary storage of update packages must be in protected memory regions — an attacker with access to the storage medium should not be able to substitute a different update package.

Installation and Activation. The installation process writes the verified update to the target ECU's flash memory using UDS programming services (0x34 RequestDownload, 0x36 TransferData, 0x37 RequestTransferExit). The OTA client must ensure that the programming session is properly authenticated and that the target ECU verifies the received image independently (the target ECU should not trust the OTA client's prior verification alone). After installation, the updated software is activated on next boot, where secure boot verifies the new image before execution. Activation confirmation is sent back through the OTA channel to the backend server.

Failure Handling and Recovery. OTA update failures must not leave the ECU in an inoperable state. A/B partition schemes allow fallback to the previous working version if the new version fails boot verification or self-test. The OTA client must report update status (success, failure with reason, rollback) to both the backend server and IdsM. Update failures due to signature verification errors or unexpected package contents should be flagged as potential security incidents. R155 Annex 5 threat 7.2.3 addresses OTA update security, and the OTA client's integration with AUTOSAR security modules is a primary evidence item for this threat.

mappings.notes: AUTOSAR integration should support governed and secure software distribution workflows.

clauses.title: Domain controller isolation
clauses.guidance:
Domain controller isolation in AUTOSAR defines trust boundaries, memory protection, and communication controls that prevent faults or compromises in one functional domain from affecting other domains. As domain controllers consolidate multiple functions onto single high-performance processors, isolation becomes critical for both safety (ISO 26262 freedom from interference) and security (ISO 21434 defense in depth).

Hardware Isolation Mechanisms. Modern automotive processors provide hardware-enforced isolation through Memory Protection Units (MPU) for Cortex-R cores in AUTOSAR Classic and Memory Management Units (MMU) with virtualization extensions for Cortex-A cores in AUTOSAR Adaptive. Hardware virtualization (Type 1 hypervisor) creates isolated virtual machines, each running a separate AUTOSAR instance with its own memory space, peripheral access, and network interfaces. The hypervisor enforces that a compromised guest VM cannot access memory, peripherals, or network interfaces assigned to other VMs. ARM TrustZone provides an additional isolation boundary, creating a secure world for cryptographic operations and key storage that is inaccessible from the normal world even if the OS is compromised.

Software Isolation in AUTOSAR. AUTOSAR Classic provides OS Application isolation through memory protection boundaries — each OS Application runs in a protected memory region and can only access configured resources. Trusted functions provide controlled privilege escalation for specific operations. AUTOSAR Adaptive uses POSIX process isolation with ara::exec managing process lifecycle and resource allocation. Process-level isolation combined with mandatory access control (based on the platform's IAM configuration) restricts inter-process communication to explicitly authorized channels.

Network Isolation. Domain controllers connected to multiple network segments must enforce network-level isolation between domains. Each virtual domain should have its own virtual Ethernet interface (mapped to VLANs) with firewall rules preventing cross-domain traffic except through explicitly authorized paths. CAN controller access should be partitioned — a function in the body domain should not be able to send CAN messages on the powertrain CAN bus. Network isolation configuration must align with the domain controller's hardware capabilities (number of CAN controllers, Ethernet switch port isolation).

Trust Boundary Enforcement. Each domain boundary represents a trust boundary that must be enforced consistently across hardware, software, and network layers. Cross-domain service calls must pass through authenticated interfaces (service authentication profiles). Shared resources (flash memory, HSM, network bandwidth) must be partitioned with enforceable quotas to prevent resource exhaustion attacks. Diagnostic access to individual domains should be independently authenticated. The trust boundary architecture should be documented as part of the ISO 21434 cybersecurity specifications and mapped to the TARA threat treatment plan, demonstrating how domain isolation mitigates identified cross-domain attack scenarios (R155 7.3.4).

mappings.notes: Controller isolation contributes directly to architecture-level cybersecurity design.

clauses.title: GB/T 40857 Section 9: Incident and response operations
clauses.guidance:
GB/T 40857 Section 9 defines incident detection, reporting, and response requirements for vehicle cybersecurity, mandating that manufacturers maintain operational capabilities to handle cybersecurity events affecting vehicles in the Chinese market. This section maps to UN R155 paragraph 7.3.7 (requirements for incident response) and corresponds to ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 13 (cybersecurity monitoring) and the incident handling provisions of Clause 8 (continual cybersecurity activities). China adds specific reporting obligations and coordination requirements that reflect the national regulatory framework.

The incident response capability must be operational before the vehicle type enters the Chinese market. Manufacturers must establish a vehicle cybersecurity incident response team (CSIRT) or equivalent function with defined roles, escalation procedures, and communication channels. The CSIRT must be able to receive incident reports from vehicle owners, dealers, suppliers, and Chinese regulatory authorities including MIIT and the China National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team (CNCERT/CC). Response procedures must be documented in Chinese and included in the CCC certification evidence package.

Detection requirements mandate that the vehicle and its supporting infrastructure generate sufficient telemetry to identify cybersecurity incidents. On-vehicle detection capabilities should include intrusion detection system (IDS) alerts from the gateway and network segments, secure boot failures, unauthorized firmware modification attempts, abnormal communication patterns on external interfaces, and V2X message verification failures. Backend detection capabilities should include monitoring of telematics server infrastructure, OTA update system anomalies, and aggregated fleet-level anomaly indicators. Detection data from vehicles in China must be stored within Chinese territory per data localization requirements.

China-specific reporting obligations require that significant cybersecurity incidents be reported to MIIT and, if personal data is affected, to the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC). The reporting timeline follows the incident classification scheme in China's cybersecurity incident reporting regulations — critical incidents require notification within one hour, major incidents within six hours. The report must include the incident description, affected vehicle types and estimated number of affected vehicles, potential safety impact, personal data exposure assessment, and initial containment measures taken. Manufacturers must have pre-established reporting templates and designated contacts at both MIIT and their CATARC or CAERI liaison.

Response procedures must address containment, eradication, and recovery phases. Containment may include remote disabling of compromised vehicle functions through the telematics channel (subject to safety constraints), emergency OTA update deployment, or dealer recall notification. For V2X-related incidents — such as compromise of V2X certificate infrastructure or discovery of a misbehaving V2X participant — the manufacturer must coordinate with the national V2X security credential management authority to revoke affected certificates and update certificate revocation lists distributed through the C-V2X infrastructure.

Post-incident activities include root cause analysis, evidence preservation for potential regulatory investigation, lessons learned documentation, and CSMS improvement actions. The manufacturer must report the incident resolution and root cause to MIIT within a timeframe specified in the initial incident notification. Testing of incident response procedures is evaluated during MIIT surveillance audits, which may include simulated incident exercises to verify the manufacturer's response capabilities per GB/T 40856 testing protocols.

mappings.notes: Incident response operations align with continual cybersecurity activities and post-production monitoring.

clauses.title: Clause 15: Continual conformance assessment
clauses.guidance:
Clause 15 of ISO 24089 defines continual conformance assessment — the ongoing process of verifying that the software update management system continues to meet its defined requirements and performs effectively over time. This is not a one-time certification exercise but a recurring evaluation that confirms the SUMS remains compliant with ISO 24089, aligned with UN R156 obligations, and effective in practice as the fleet, technology landscape, and threat environment evolve.

Conformance assessment activities include periodic internal audits of SUMS processes, review of campaign performance data against established KPIs, evaluation of incident response effectiveness, and verification that documentation and evidence packages remain complete and current. The assessment must cover all clauses of ISO 24089, from governance (Clause 5) through sunset execution (Clause 14), confirming that each process area continues to operate as documented and produces the expected work products.

Process effectiveness metrics distinguish conformance assessment from compliance checkbox exercises. It is not sufficient to confirm that a process exists — the assessment must evaluate whether the process achieves its intended outcomes. For example, the campaign management process (Clause 8) is conformant not just because a campaign plan template exists, but because actual campaigns consistently meet their success criteria, anomalies are detected and resolved within defined timeframes, and the staged rollout approach effectively limits the impact of update-induced issues.

The assessment must address changes in the operating environment that may affect SUMS effectiveness. These include new vehicle platforms with different update architectures, changes in supplier relationships, evolution of the threat landscape (new attack techniques targeting OTA systems), changes in regulatory requirements, and growth in fleet size that may stress infrastructure capacity. Each of these changes may require updates to SUMS processes, tools, or infrastructure, and the conformance assessment must evaluate whether the SUMS has adapted adequately.

UN R156 Paragraph 7.2.3 requires ongoing assessment of SUMS effectiveness, and ISO 24089 Clause 15 provides the structured process for meeting this obligation. From an ISO 21434 perspective, the continual conformance assessment parallels the continual cybersecurity activities required by Clause 8, and the two assessment processes should be coordinated to avoid duplication and ensure consistency. The CAL relevance of this clause reflects the fact that degradation in SUMS conformance — for example, weakened signing practices or incomplete monitoring — directly increases cybersecurity risk for the fielded fleet. Assessment findings must be reported to management and tracked to closure, creating an improvement loop that sustains SUMS maturity over the multi-decade lifecycle of automotive products.

mappings.notes: Continual conformance assessment supports SUMS effectiveness expectations.

clauses.title: Security event attribution
clauses.guidance: Defines attribution metadata for emissions diagnostics events with potential cybersecurity relevance.

mappings.notes: Attribution metadata strengthens harmonized reporting of diagnostics security events.
